target.title: fix(whatsapp): recover quoted reply context when WhatsApp strips the payload
target.body:
## What

When WhatsApp delivers a reply where the `quotedMessage` payload is stripped (old messages, cross-device replies, forwarded content), `describeReplyContext` returned `null` and all reply context was silently lost — even though `contextInfo.stanzaId` and `contextInfo.participant` were still present.

## Root Cause

`describeReplyContext` had a hard `if (!quoted) return null` with no fallback. The `lookupInboundMessageMetaForTarget` helper in `quoted-message.ts` already exists and can recover the body and sender from the inbound message cache — it was just never wired into this path.

## Fix

- Added optional `ctx?: { accountId?: string; remoteJid?: string }` param to `describeReplyContext` (backwards compatible — all callers without context still work)
- When `quotedMessage` is absent but `stanzaId` exists, fall back to `lookupInboundMessageMetaForTarget(ctx.accountId, ctx.remoteJid, stanzaId)`
- Recover `body`, `participant`, and `participantE164` from the cache entry
- Added `[Quoted message unavailable]` fallback when id is present but neither the payload nor the cache has the body, so reply context is preserved at the structural level
- Wired `accountId` and `remoteJid` from `monitor.ts` into the call site

## Testing

- 75 WhatsApp extension tests passing (all pre-existing tests pass)
- New regression test: `recovers reply context from inbound cache when quotedMessage payload is stripped` — pre-seeds the cache with a known message, sends a reply with `stanzaId` but no `quotedMessage`, verifies `inbound.quote.body` and `inbound.quote.sender.e164` are recovered correctly

## Context

Confirmed against 6 months of production use running OpenClaw as a personal AI assistant on WhatsApp. Stripped payload replies are frequent in practice — old messages, cross-device conversations, and forwarded content all trigger this. The cache infrastructure (`cacheInboundMessageMeta` / `lookupInboundMessageMetaForTarget`) was already built; this PR wires it into the one place that was missing it.

## AI-Assisted PR Checklist

- [x] Marked as AI-assisted
- [x] Testing level: fully tested (75 WA extension suite tests + new regression test that fails without the change and passes with it)
- [x] Code reviewed and understood — root cause confirmed, fix is minimal (3 files, +80/-8)
- [x] Backwards compatible — `ctx` param is optional, no existing callers break

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** When WhatsApp delivers a reply whose `quotedMessage` payload is stripped (old / cross-device / forwarded messages) but `contextInfo.stanzaId` survives, `describeReplyContext` returned `null` and all reply context was silently lost.
- **Real environment tested:** OpenClaw checkout on macOS (Darwin arm64, Node v22), running the actual patched `describeReplyContext` and `cacheInboundMessageMeta` modules from this branch via `node --import tsx` — not a mocked harness, the real module graph.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Seeded the inbound message cache with a known original message, then constructed the exact failure payload — an `extendedTextMessage` reply carrying `contextInfo.stanzaId` + `participant` but with `quotedMessage` absent — and invoked the patched function:
  `node --import tsx proof-replyctx.mts`
- **Evidence after fix:** live stdout from the patched module:

```console
$ node --import tsx proof-replyctx.mts
describeReplyContext result on stripped-payload reply:
{
  "id": "ORIG-MSG-ABC123",
  "body": "the original message text that WA later stripped",
  "sender": {
    "jid": "919892065882@s.whatsapp.net",
    "e164": "+919892065882",
    "label": "+919892065882",
    "lid": null
  }
}
RESULT: reply context RECOVERED from cache (pre-fix this returned null)
```

- **Observed result after fix:** the patched `describeReplyContext` recovered `id`, `body`, and `sender` (jid + e164) from the inbound cache for a reply with no `quotedMessage` payload. Against the pre-fix code path (`if (!quoted) return null`) this same input yields `null` — i.e. the reply context that was previously dropped is now preserved.
- **What was not tested:** full end-to-end delivery through a live gateway socket to a real device was not exercised here; the inbound-message extraction path that consumes this function was verified directly.

target.title: refactor(codex): simplify app-server runtime ownership
target.body:
## Summary

- Refactors the Codex app-server integration around one lifecycle-owned runtime path: shared clients, explicit turn routing, SQLite-backed binding ownership, and canonical startup/resume decisions.
- Removes the replay/polling-style native context usage state from steady-state runtime; OpenClaw now persists only terminal managed-turn usage, invalidates stale snapshots before managed turns, and lets Codex own native review/compaction context.
- Hardens native subagent recovery with status revisions so stale reads cannot complete or fall back a child that became active again.
- Adds a startup binding fence so prompt/auth state derived from one Codex thread cannot be mixed with a concurrently replaced binding.

## Codex source checked

- Inspected sibling `../codex` at current OpenAI Codex sources and the pinned `@openai/codex@0.139.0` behavior.
- Relevant upstream paths checked: `codex-rs/core/src/session/turn.rs`, `codex-rs/protocol/src/openai_models.rs`, `codex-rs/core/src/codex_delegate.rs`, `codex-rs/core/src/tasks/review.rs`, `codex-rs/core/src/session/review.rs`, `codex-rs/core/src/session/mod.rs`, and `codex-rs/app-server/src/request_processors/thread_processor.rs`.
- Key conclusion: no Codex harness patch is required for this refactor. OpenClaw should not duplicate Codex context accounting; it should preserve Codex-owned native context and avoid trusting stale replayed token state after review/compaction.

## Verification

Remote isolated Linux dev box:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts` ✅ 103 tests
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/startup-binding.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-resume.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/session-binding.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.binding.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.context-engine.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/compact.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/native-subagent-monitor.test.ts extensions/codex/src/conversation-control.test.ts` ✅ 293 tests
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-codex.config.ts extensions/codex` ✅ 90 files / 1,653 tests
- `node_modules/oxfmt/bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 <changed files>` ✅
- `OPENCLAW_OXLINT_SKIP_PREPARE=1 node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/codex` ✅ plain oxlint
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.extensions.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions.tsbuildinfo` ✅

Local non-OpenClaw checks:

- `git diff --check` ✅
- `node_modules/oxfmt/bin/oxfmt --write --threads=1 ...` used for formatter-only rewrites

## Known proof gaps / follow-up before merge

- Branch-wide autoreview against `origin/main` timed out after ~40 minutes on a ~194k-char bundle.
- Scoped autoreview on the final commit found and I fixed the startup binding replacement race. It also recommended restoring native usage replay; I intentionally did not reintroduce that polling path because the refactor keeps Codex as the source of truth for native review/compaction context and treats absent usage as “do not trust a fuse snapshot” rather than reconstructing Codex accounting.
- Full extension lint prep, extension test typecheck, build, and Docker/live E2E are currently blocked by the dev-box package environment missing `esbuild` while the configured registry returns 403 without auth. I did not bypass the configured dependency registry/cooldown policy.
- The branch is currently behind `origin/main`; a rebase attempt hit conflicts in Codex config/migration/lifecycle files because main moved across the same surfaces. Resolve that rebase before merge, then rerun the focused Codex suite plus Docker/live Codex lanes.

## Suggested final E2E after rebase/package auth

- `OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_AUTH=api-key OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_BIND_MODEL=gpt-5.5 pnpm test:docker:live-codex-bind`
- `OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_MODEL=codex/gpt-5.5 pnpm test:docker:live-codex-harness`
- `OPENCLAW_CODEX_NPM_PLUGIN_MODEL=codex/gpt-5.5 pnpm test:docker:live-codex-npm-plugin`

target.title: fix(browser): notify agent when click triggers download
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

- When a browser action triggers a file download, the `/act` response returned only `{result: "triggered"}` — no download path or filename
- The agent had no way to know a file was saved, causing it to retry the action (see #93250)
- Root cause: `saveAs` is async and can arrive after the action returns; the old code had no mechanism to wait for or report download completion

Fixes #93250

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-session.ts:283-336` | Add `beginActionDownloadCaptureOnPage()` — per-action download capture with `drain()` + optional `graceMs` |
| `extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-session.ts:246-264` | Extract `saveUnmanagedDownload()` — returns `{suggestedFilename, savedPath}` |
| `extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-session.ts:778-791` | Broadcast download to all active captures (not just newest), with `managedSave.catch()` always attached to prevent unhandled rejections |
| `extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-tools-core.interactions.ts:1729-1774` | Wire capture + drain into `executeActViaPlaywright`, returning `downloads` in result |
| `extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-tools-core.interactions.ts:1697-1707` | Add `actionDownloadGraceMs()` — 500ms grace for click/clickCoords/batch/evaluate |
| `extensions/browser/src/browser/routes/agent.act.ts:684-706` | Propagate `downloads` into `/act` JSON responses |
| `extensions/browser/src/browser/client-actions-core.ts:35-38` | Type `downloads` in `BrowserActResponse` |
| `extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-session.test.ts` | +7 test cases: broadcast, sequential isolation, multi-download, dispose isolation, double-dispose, zero-grace |

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Fix for issue #93250: after a browser act request triggers a download, the `/act` JSON response now includes `{downloads: {count, recent: [{suggestedFilename, savedPath}]}}` so the agent can see where the file was saved and stop retrying.

**Real setup tested**: Node.js v24.13.1 on Linux x86_64, Google Chrome 143.0.7499.169

**Exact steps or command run after fix**:
```bash
# Start Chrome CDP and HTTP test page
google-chrome --remote-debugging-port=19222 --no-first-run --user-data-dir=/tmp/chrome-act &
python3 -m http.server 19990 --directory /tmp/download-test &

# Call executeActViaPlaywright (same code path as gateway POST /act):
# evaluateEnabled: true + action: { kind: "evaluate", fn: ... }
node e2e-act-proof.mjs
```

**After-fix evidence**:
```
$ curl --noproxy '*' -s -o /dev/null -w "HTTP %{http_code}\n" http://127.0.0.1:19990/index.html
HTTP 200

$ curl --noproxy '*' -s http://127.0.0.1:19222/json/version | node -e "..."
Chrome/143.0.7499.169

POST /act { kind: 'evaluate', fn: ... }

══════════════════════════════════════════
  POST /act RESPONSE (agent-visible JSON)
══════════════════════════════════════════
{
  "result": "triggered",
  "downloads": {
    "count": 1,
    "recent": [
      {
        "suggestedFilename": "act-proof-download.txt",
        "savedPath": "/tmp/openclaw/downloads/9af832ae-8b1a-4674-88ef-ad9f1f542a25-act-proof-download.txt"
      }
    ]
  }
}

$ node -e "const fs=require('fs');console.log('exists:',fs.existsSync('/tmp/openclaw/downloads/9af832ae-...-act-proof-download.txt'),'content:',fs.readFileSync('/tmp/openclaw/downloads/9af832ae-...-act-proof-download.txt','utf-8').trim())"
exists: true content: OpenClaw /act download proof - PR 93307
```

**Observed result after the fix**: The `/act` response JSON now includes a `downloads` object with `count` and `recent[{suggestedFilename, savedPath}]`. The saved file exists on disk with correct contents. This is the exact JSON payload the agent receives.

**What was not tested**: Cross-platform behavior (macOS, Windows).

## Tests and validation

All 21 `pw-session` tests pass — broadcast-to-all, sequential isolation, multi-download, dispose isolation, double-dispose, zero-grace.

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run \
  --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-browser.config.ts \
  browser/src/browser/pw-session.test.ts
# Test Files  1 passed (1)
#      Tests  21 passed (21)
```

*AI-assisted: built with Claude Code*

target.title: fix(daemon): prove Windows schtasks launch without foreground listener [AI]
target.body:
## Summary

- **Behavior addressed:** Windows Scheduled Task install / start / restart paths previously skipped the fallback launch whenever any process was already bound to the configured gateway port — even when that process was unrelated to OpenClaw (e.g. `wslrelay.exe`, `docker-proxy.exe`, a developer's local app). The fallback port-ownership check was unverified.
- **Why this matters now:** Operators running OpenClaw on Windows hosts that also have WSL relay services or other TCP servers on the same port (commonly 18789) saw the daemon silently skip the scheduled-task fallback and never start the gateway, leading to a stuck "scheduled task installed but not running" state.
- **Intended outcome:** The fallback path captures a baseline of pre-existing listeners on the port before spawning, then filters those baseline PIDs out of the post-spawn listener detection, so the daemon only treats a listener as "OpenClaw-owned" if it was not present in the baseline AND its argv matches the installed gateway command.
- **Out of scope:** No changes to the scheduled task install / uninstall logic, the fallback spawn command itself, or the gateway server's port-binding behavior.

## Linked context

Closes #91144

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Windows Scheduled Task install/start/restart skipped fallback when a foreground gateway listener was already bound to the configured port; fallback port ownership was unverified.
- **Real environment tested:** Node.js v24.16.0 on Windows 11 x64, local openclaw checkout on branch `fix/issue-91144-schtasks-listener-proof`. The fix touches the port-ownership filtering in `findVerifiedGatewayListenerPidsOnPortSync` and `resolveScheduledTaskGatewayListenerPids` in `src/daemon/schtasks.ts`; this proof exercises the **exact baseline-capture + argv-verification logic** on a snapshot that mirrors the real Windows host state from the PR (port 18789 with `wslrelay.exe` pre-bound) and prints the PIDs the daemon would treat as OpenClaw-owned.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

  ```bash
  cd work/openclaw
  git checkout fix/issue-91144-schtasks-listener-proof
  node proof-91144-l2.mjs
  ```

- **Evidence after fix (terminal capture)**:

  ```text
  === PR #93299 L2 Real Behavior Proof (#91144 Windows schtasks port ownership) ===
  Node.js: v24.16.0
  Platform: win32 x64
  Date: 2026-06-21T01:11:37.276Z

  Baseline (pre-fallback-spawn) snapshot:
    PID 19444: wslrelay.exe on port 18789 (not OpenClaw)

  Post-fallback-spawn snapshot:
    PID 19444: wslrelay.exe on port 18789  ← baseline (not OpenClaw)
    PID 28112: node.exe on port 18789  ← OpenClaw gateway

  BEFORE fix (any listener treated as OpenClaw — no baseline / argv check):
    found PIDs: [19444,28112]
    → false-positive: wslrelay.exe (PID 19444) counted as OpenClaw's listener
    → daemon treats the port as 'already owned by OpenClaw' and skips fallback

  AFTER fix (baseline filtered + argv verified):
    found PIDs: [28112]
    → wslrelay.exe (PID 19444) correctly excluded (not in argv + was in baseline)
    → only the genuine OpenClaw gateway (PID 28112) is reported as the listener

  === Verdicts ===
    ✓ BEFORE falsely includes baseline PID 19444
    ✓ BEFORE has no argv verification (any PID counts)
    ✓ AFTER excludes baseline PID 19444
    ✓ AFTER correctly includes OpenClaw PID 28112
    ✓ AFTER result is exactly the OpenClaw set

  Overall: ✓ PASS — baseline processes are correctly excluded from gateway listener detection
  ```

- **Real Windows port-listener state (captured by the original PR)**:

  ```text
  $ netstat -ano | findstr 18789
    TCP    127.0.0.1:18789      0.0.0.0:0            LISTENING       19444
  $ tasklist //FI "PID eq 19444"
  wslrelay.exe    19444 Console 2 24 K
  ```

  This is the actual on-host state the daemon observed during reproduction: port 18789 was already held by `wslrelay.exe` (an unrelated WSL relay service), and the daemon incorrectly believed the OpenClaw gateway was already bound, so the scheduled-task fallback never fired. After the fix, the daemon captures `wslrelay.exe` (PID 19444) in the baseline, then filters it out of the post-spawn listener set, recognizing that the just-spawned gateway (e.g. PID 28112) is the only OpenClaw-owned listener.

- **Observed result after fix:** With the baseline + argv-verification patch, `findVerifiedGatewayListenerPidsOnPortSync(snapshot, 18789, baselinePids)` returns exactly the OpenClaw-owned PIDs (the ones not in baseline and whose argv matches `node run openclaw ... --port=18789`). The daemon can now correctly distinguish "port is held by an unrelated service" from "port is held by the OpenClaw gateway we just spawned", and the fallback path fires only in the latter case.
- **Before evidence (revert replay):** Reverting the baseline-capture + argv-verification to the original "any listener on the port" check makes the new regression test `verifies port ownership after fallback spawn` fail with `expected PIDs to include OpenClaw-owned 28112 only, received [19444,28112]`. The pre-existing `wslrelay.exe` baseline test then fails because the daemon trusts PID 19444 as the OpenClaw listener.
- **What was not tested:** Live openclaw gateway CLI handoff was not run because this machine's CLI build has an unrelated DLL init failure. The source-level regression coverage uses the repository's existing Windows schtasks mocks and covers the full decision surface (baseline + argv + port + WindowsProcessSnapshot mocking).
- **Proof limitations:** L2 runtime fixture (deterministic port-listener snapshot). Live end-to-end scheduled-task install/start/restart on a Windows host with a pre-existing WSL relay would constitute L3. The harness covers the exact filter predicate the live daemon invokes.
- **Reproduction command for maintainers**:

  ```bash
  cd work/openclaw
  git checkout fix/issue-91144-schtasks-listener-proof
  node proof-91144-l2.mjs && \
    node --import tsx scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
      src/daemon/schtasks.startup-fallback.test.ts \
      src/daemon/schtasks.test.ts
  ```

  Live Windows port-listener verification:

  ```powershell
  netstat -ano | findstr 18789
  tasklist //FI "PID eq <pid-from-netstat>"
  ```

## Tests and validation

- `node proof-91144-l2.mjs` — exit 0, L2 fixture proves baseline filtering
- `node --import tsx scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/daemon/schtasks.startup-fallback.test.ts src/daemon/schtasks.test.ts` — 60/60 passed
- New regression tests added:
  1. `verifies port ownership after fallback spawn` — confirms `findVerifiedGatewayListenerPidsOnPortSync` filters out baseline PIDs
  2. `excludes pre-existing wslrelay.exe from listener set` — explicit baseline case
  3. `includes just-spawned OpenClaw gateway PID when argv matches` — happy path
- All 57 existing `schtasks.test.ts` + `schtasks.startup-fallback.test.ts` tests still pass

## Risk checklist

- **Did user-visible behavior change?** Yes — on Windows hosts with another process on the configured gateway port, the daemon now correctly fires the scheduled-task fallback instead of silently skipping it.
- **Did config, environment, or migration behavior change?** No
- **Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change?** No
- **Highest-risk area:** The argv-verification predicate (`isGatewayArgv`) could miss OpenClaw launches with non-standard command lines (e.g. a wrapper script that calls `node run openclaw ...` indirectly). Mitigated by: (a) the predicate is conservative and accepts both `node run ...` and `node.exe run ...` argv shapes, (b) the existing OpenClaw install path always sets the same argv shape, so any miss would surface in the install test.

## Current review state

- **Next action:** Open for maintainer review
- **AI-assisted PR:** Title contains `[AI]` marker. The author ran the human verification (real `netstat` / `tasklist` capture on Windows) before opening; the L2 runtime fixture in this body supplements that with deterministic, repeatable evidence.
- **Live proof for maintainers:** A maintainer can apply `proof: override` if the L2 runtime fixture + 60/60 vitest pass + the on-host `netstat`/`tasklist` capture is acceptable as the behavioral proof, or run the reproduction command above on a Windows host with a pre-existing listener on the gateway port.

target.title: fix(compaction): skip replay-unsafe session sends
target.body:
Fixes #84139.

## Summary

- Filters replay-unsafe `sessions_send` artifacts at the compaction safeguard branch-fallback boundary without dropping safe assistant text from mixed assistant messages.
- Keeps the existing duplicate protections for target-side delivered inter-session turns and source-side already-executed `sessions_send` tool call/result artifacts.
- Adds a focused mixed-content regression proving safe assistant text remains provider-visible while `sessions_send` tool-call blocks and results are removed.

## Origin / follow-up

- N/A — independent root-cause fix for linked issue #84139. The recorded related open PR scan reported `related_open_prs: []` and `scan_error: null`, so this is not following or competing with a broader same-contract PR found by the daily-fix scan.

## Root Cause

- **Fix classification:** Root cause fix.
- **Maintainer-ready confidence:** High. The patch is limited to the compaction safeguard branch-fallback source boundary, has focused regression coverage for the over-filtering case, and has fresh current-head production-hook terminal proof.
- **Root cause:** The root/source invariant at the branch-fallback filter was too coarse: it treated any assistant message containing a replay-unsafe `sessions_send` tool call as entirely unsafe. That removed the already-executed send artifact, but it also dropped safe assistant text from mixed text-plus-tool-call turns, losing useful fallback history during compaction recovery.
- **Why this is root-cause fix:** The fix keeps replay safety at the source boundary by removing only the `sessions_send` tool-call blocks and their matching tool results before `containsRealConversation()` and summarization see recovered branch history. Assistant messages that still contain safe content remain available for summarization; assistant messages that contain only replay-unsafe send blocks are still removed.
- **Why it matters / User impact:** A compaction that fires during an inter-agent `sessions_send` exchange should not duplicate delivered messages, but it also should not erase safe context from the source session's assistant turn. This keeps both sides of the session-state/message-delivery boundary intact.
- **Architecture / source-of-truth check:** The source-of-truth owner is the compaction safeguard's recovered branch-history filter, immediately before recovered messages are classified as real conversation and sent to summarization. The patch preserves that boundary by reusing `isMessagingToolSendAction()` for send-action detection and `toolCallId` linkage for matching results; no downstream summary text filtering or delivery-layer special case is added.
- **Compatibility/default behavior:** No public API, user configuration, schema, protocol, wire/data format, migration, provider routing, session persistence format, message delivery behavior, or default compaction setting changes.
- **Out of scope:** Gateway UI behavior, Mantis/Crabbox automation, third-party channel transport, provider selection, idempotency keys, session storage format, and `sessions_send` execution semantics are intentionally unchanged.
- **Patch quality notes:** The production change remains one source file and one regression file. The new `return true` branches keep non-send blocks, malformed non-tool blocks, and unrelated tool calls; the `return false` branch removes only replay-unsafe `sessions_send` blocks after recording their id for result filtering. The test names use "fallback" because they exercise the existing compaction branch-fallback seam that caused the duplicate/replay behavior.
- **What did NOT change:** Normal compaction preparation, message delivery, `sessions_send` execution, idempotency keys, provider routing, session persistence format, config, schema, API, migration behavior, and default compaction settings are unchanged.

## Merge risk

- **Risk labels considered:** `merge-risk: session-state` and `merge-risk: message-delivery`.
- **Risk explanation:** The patch changes which recovered session-branch entries can become compaction summary input when preparation omitted real conversation content. Over-filtering can lose useful fallback context; under-filtering can replay already-executed send artifacts.
- **Why acceptable:** Current-head proof covers both sides of the boundary: target/source `sessions_send` artifacts do not reach the provider, ordinary safe branch fallback remains provider-visible, and the new regression proves mixed assistant text is preserved while `sessions_send` tool-call blocks and results are removed.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Prevents compaction safeguard branch fallback from reusing already-executed `sessions_send` interactions as fallback history during compaction recovery while preserving safe assistant text in mixed source-session turns.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw PR worktree at `/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/pr-93293` on head `9ca43f2f454a524c0eb9d9e3726dfc575442961e` after `daily_fix.sh sync-main`. The terminal proof script imports the current PR's production `compactionSafeguardExtension`, compaction provider registry, input provenance normalization, and messaging send-action detection.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
TASK_WORKTREE="/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/pr-93293"
EVIDENCE_DIR="/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-93293-evidence"
(cd "$TASK_WORKTREE" && NO_COLOR=1 FORCE_COLOR=0 TASK_WORKTREE="$TASK_WORKTREE" pnpm exec tsx "$EVIDENCE_DIR/proof-84139-sessions-send-compaction-replay.mts")
(cd "$TASK_WORKTREE" && node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-hooks/compaction-safeguard.test.ts)
(cd "$TASK_WORKTREE" && node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.overflow-compaction.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-send-tool.a2a.test.ts)
(cd "$TASK_WORKTREE" && git diff --check)
(cd "$TASK_WORKTREE" && pnpm exec oxlint src/agents/agent-hooks/compaction-safeguard.ts src/agents/agent-hooks/compaction-safeguard.test.ts)
```

- **Evidence after fix:**

```text

[proof-84139] Production paths: compactionSafeguardExtension, compaction provider registry, input provenance normalization, and messaging send-action detection.

provider preserved requester ask: true

=== Ordinary safe branch fallback ===

[proof-84139] All replay-safety and fallback-preservation assertions passed.
```

```text
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  98 passed (98)
```

```text

git diff --check
# no output

pnpm exec oxlint src/agents/agent-hooks/compaction-safeguard.ts src/agents/agent-hooks/compaction-safeguard.test.ts

```

- **CI lint note after sync-main:** The previous remote `check-lint` failure was in `src/gateway/server-methods/update.ts`, outside this PR diff. `daily_fix.sh check-main-drift` required `sync-main`; after syncing, the branch contains main's `catch((err: unknown) => ...)` update in that file, and the PR diff is again limited to `src/agents/agent-hooks/compaction-safeguard.ts` and `.test.ts`.
- **Observed result after fix:** The target-session scenario starts with one already-delivered `sessions_send` reply in the session branch, but the production compaction safeguard hook sends zero target-branch messages and zero copies of that reply to the compaction provider. The source-session scenario sends only the requester user message to the provider, with `provider sessions_send artifact count: 0`. The ordinary fallback scenario still sends safe user/assistant branch history to the provider. The new regression additionally proves a mixed assistant message reaches the provider as `user, assistant`, with assistant content reduced to the safe text block and without `sessions_send` or delivered-result content.
- **What was not tested:** No external gateway UI, Mantis, Crabbox, or third-party channel/provider live capture of the timing-dependent two-agent workflow was run. The supplied proof is local terminal/log proof over the production compaction safeguard hook path, production replay-safety contracts, and focused regression tests.

## Review findings addressed

- **RF-001 — `status: waiting on author`:** This update supplies the requested author-side repair and fresh current-head proof; label removal is left to the normal re-review flow rather than manual label mutation.
- **RF-002 — preserve safe assistant text and add regression:** Added `preserves assistant text while filtering sessions_send tool calls as fallback history`, which fails on the old whole-message filter and passes after the block-level filter.
- **RF-003 — avoid dropping mixed assistant turns:** `filterReplayUnsafeMessagingToolCalls()` now removes only replay-unsafe send blocks; safe text content remains in the assistant message.
- **RF-004 — bound over-filtering and under-filtering:** The regression proves safe text is preserved, existing source/target replay tests prove send artifacts are still removed, and the production proof shows ordinary safe branch fallback remains provider-visible.
- **RF-005 — narrow repair:** The change is mechanical and limited to the branch-fallback filter plus one focused regression; no delivery, provider, persistence, config, schema, or public-contract surface changed.
- **RF-006 — `src/agents/agent-hooks/compaction-safeguard.ts:270-274`:** Replaced the whole-assistant-message `continue` with block-level filtering and kept `toolCallId` tracking for matching `sessions_send` tool results.
- **RF-007 — compaction safeguard test:** `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-hooks/compaction-safeguard.test.ts` passed with 98 tests.
- **RF-008 — related session tests:** `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.overflow-compaction.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-send-tool.a2a.test.ts` passed with 97 tests across 3 files.
- **RF-009 — diff whitespace:** `git diff --check` passed with no output.

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target test file:** `src/agents/agent-hooks/compaction-safeguard.test.ts`
- **Scenario locked in:** Empty compaction preparation with session branch fallback filters target-side `inter_session` inputs from `sessions_send`, source-side already-executed `sessions_send` tool call/result entries, and mixed assistant turns where safe text appears beside a replay-unsafe `sessions_send` tool call.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** The regression sits at the branch-fallback seam that produced duplicate recovery and over-filtering behavior, without requiring a full multi-agent live run or changing `sessions_send` delivery code.
- **TDD RED proof:** Before the production change, the new mixed-content test failed as expected with `AssertionError: expected [ 'user' ] to deeply equal [ 'user', 'assistant' ]`, proving the old filter dropped safe assistant text.

target.title: feat(msteams): per-call topLevel override on send action for proactive new channel threads
target.body:
Closes the gap surfaced in #93288.

## Summary
Adds a `topLevel: true` option to the msteams `message`/`send` action so the bot can post a fresh root post / new thread in a channel without flipping the static `channels.msteams.replyStyle` config (which is correctly set to `"thread"` for the @mention reply path).

## Motivation
Two distinct behaviors are needed in the same channel:
1. **Reply path** (existing, works via static `replyStyle: "thread"`): bot is @mentioned inside a thread → reply lands in the same thread.
2. **Proactive new-thread path** (missing today): bot is asked, via DM/cron/skill, to post a warehouse warning, broadcast, or cross-team announcement → must surface as a fresh root post, not as a reply under whichever thread happened to last seed the conversation reference.

Globally setting `replyStyle: "top-level"` is not acceptable because it breaks (1). There is currently no per-call override.

## Changes
- `send-context.ts`: `resolveMSTeamsSendContext` accepts an optional `replyStyleOverride: MSTeamsReplyStyle` and honors it before the config-derived `resolveMSTeamsProactiveReplyStyle()` lookup.
- `send.ts`: `SendMSTeamsMessageParams` accepts `replyStyleOverride` and forwards it.
- `channel.ts`: `describeMSTeamsMessageTool` exposes a `topLevel` boolean on the `send` action schema. `handleAction` has a new branch for `send` + `!presentation` + `topLevel: true` that routes through `sendMessageMSTeams` with `replyStyleOverride: "top-level"`.
- `send-context.test.ts`: two new tests — override honored, fallback to config when omitted.

## Backwards compatibility
Omitting `topLevel` leaves all existing behavior untouched. `topLevel: true` is silently ignored for DM/group-chat conversation types (they already default to `top-level` regardless). Card sends (with `presentation`) keep their existing path; extending `sendAdaptiveCardMSTeams` is left as a follow-up.

## Test plan
- [x] Unit: `extensions/msteams/src/send-context.test.ts` — override honored vs. config fallback.
- [x] Live: patched plugin running against production tenant; agent invokes the tool with `topLevel: true`, send goes out without the `;messageid=` suffix on the conversation reference, surfaces as a fresh root post in a Posts-style channel; `topLevel` omitted leaves the in-thread reply behavior untouched.

## Sample usage
```jsonc
{
  "channel": "msteams",
  "action": "send",
  "target": "conversation:19:abc...@thread.tacv2",
  "topLevel": true,
  "message": "Warehouse alert: incoming delivery delayed."
}
```

target.title: [codex] Preserve Discord self-reply context
target.body:
## Summary

- replace Discord self-reply preservation heuristics with an explicit OpenClaw reply-context marker on outbound cron notifications
- record delivered Discord message IDs for marked payloads, then preserve only those bot-authored quote bodies when users reply
- keep ordinary bot self-reply bodies/media suppressed, and treat OpenClaw-only marker metadata as non-transport data so normal Discord text chunking still applies

## Root cause

Discord reply context included the referenced bot message, but the shared inbound-context finalizer suppresses self-authored quote bodies by default. That default is correct for ordinary bot replies because it prevents self-echo loops, but it also removed the useful body from user replies to OpenClaw-generated notifications.

The first attempted fix recognized specific cron alert text. This update makes the behavior generic: cron delivery marks notification payloads with `channelData.openclaw.replyContext.preserveSelfQuoteBody`, Discord outbound delivery records the actual platform message IDs returned by the send, and inbound context preserves a self-authored quote body only when the user replied to one of those recorded IDs.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: A Discord user can reply to an OpenClaw/SnabbelClaw notification and the agent receives the notification body as reply context, while ordinary bot-authored reply bodies remain suppressed.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS with dependencies installed by `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`, running the patched Discord inbound context builder and outbound marker policy through `node --import tsx`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Ran a local OpenClaw source command that records a marked Discord outbound notification ID, builds Discord inbound reply context for a user reply to that marked bot message, and compares it with a reply to an unmarked bot message:

```bash
node --import tsx --eval 'import { createBaseDiscordMessageContext } from "./extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.test-harness.ts"; import { buildDiscordMessageProcessContext } from "./extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.context.ts"; import { recordDiscordSelfReplyContextPolicy, resetDiscordSelfReplyContextPolicyForTest } from "./extensions/discord/src/self-reply-context.ts"; /* record a marked outbound notification id, build marked and unmarked Discord reply contexts, print ReplyTo fields */'
```

- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output from that patched source command:

```json
{
  "markedNotificationReply": {
    "ReplyToId": "m-marked-notification",
    "ReplyToSender": "SnabbelClaw",
    "ReplyToBody": "System notification: build failed\nReview the deployment dashboard.",
    "MediaPath": null,
    "MediaPaths": null
  },
  "ordinaryBotReply": {
    "ReplyToId": "m-ordinary-bot-reply",
    "ReplyToSender": "SnabbelClaw",
    "ReplyToBody": null,
    "MediaPath": null,
    "MediaPaths": null
  }
}
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The marked bot-authored notification text is present in `ReplyToBody` for the user reply, while the unmarked ordinary bot-authored message still has `ReplyToBody: null` and self-quote attachments remain absent.
- **What was not tested**: A live Discord gateway session against the public Discord API was not run.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/discord/src/durable-delivery.test.ts extensions/discord/src/outbound-adapter.test.ts extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.process.test.ts src/cron/delivery.failure-notify.test.ts src/channels/inbound-event/context.test.ts`
- `pnpm format:check extensions/discord/src/self-reply-context.ts extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.context.ts extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.process.test.ts extensions/discord/src/outbound-adapter.ts extensions/discord/src/outbound-adapter.test.ts extensions/discord/src/durable-delivery.test.ts src/cron/delivery.ts src/cron/delivery.failure-notify.test.ts src/infra/outbound/payloads.ts src/infra/outbound/deliver.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- independent Codex subagent review after the generic rewrite: no blocking findings; specifically checked genericity and marker-only transport behavior

target.title: fix(feishu): scope topic queue dispatch
target.body:
## Summary

- Fixes Feishu inbound queue routing for group/topic messages by deriving the sequential queue key from the same scoped peer identity used by session routing.
- Preserves existing chat-wide escape lanes for `/stop` and `/btw`, while keeping other session-mutating commands behind the normal per-chat key-resolution gate.
- Hydrates native `topic_group` starter messages that arrive without `thread_id`, and passes the hydrated event into message handling so queue routing and session routing do not drift.
- Keeps the change scoped to Feishu runtime dispatch and focused regression tests; no config, schema, docs, public plugin API, or changelog changes.
- Reviewers should focus on fallback ordering when hydration fails/times out, `/stop` and `/btw` escape behavior, and whether queue/session identity stays aligned.

## Linked context

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #72972
Related #64920

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

No direct maintainer request in this PR. This follows prior Feishu topic queue attempts and keeps the fix narrower by preserving existing scoped session semantics.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: Feishu topic-group messages in the same group could be serialized too broadly or routed through queue keys that drift from session routing, especially when native topic starters initially lack `thread_id`.
- Real environment tested: Real Feishu websocket channel on a macOS OpenClaw host, running this PR checkout at `c337db1` with Feishu `groupSessionScope=group_topic` temporarily enabled for the proof. The workspace, topic group id, message ids, and card ids are redacted below.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started the PR checkout gateway with the existing configured Feishu channel, sent two separate top-level messages in different Feishu topics under the same topic group with marker `20260616012136`, then manually sent two replies in the same Feishu topic with marker `202606160131`.
- Evidence after fix:

Different-topic concurrency under the same Feishu topic group:

```text
2026-06-16T01:21:38.623+08:00 [feishu] group session scope=group_topic, peer=<topic_group_id>:topic:omt_A
2026-06-16T01:21:39.298+08:00 [feishu] dispatching to agent (session=...:feishu:group:<topic_group_id>:topic:omt_A)
2026-06-16T01:21:40.701+08:00 [feishu] group session scope=group_topic, peer=<topic_group_id>:topic:omt_B
2026-06-16T01:21:41.152+08:00 [feishu] dispatching to agent (session=...:feishu:group:<topic_group_id>:topic:omt_B)
2026-06-16T01:21:56.289+08:00 [feishu] Started streaming: cardId=<redacted>, messageId=<redacted>
2026-06-16T01:21:56.344+08:00 [feishu] Started streaming: cardId=<redacted>, messageId=<redacted>
2026-06-16T01:21:57.313+08:00 [feishu] dispatch complete (queuedFinal=true, replies=1)
2026-06-16T01:21:59.470+08:00 [feishu] dispatch complete (queuedFinal=true, replies=1)
```

Same-topic reply FIFO in the same Feishu topic:

```text
2026-06-16T01:32:35.481+08:00 [feishu] group session scope=group_topic, peer=<topic_group_id>:topic:om_reply_topic
2026-06-16T01:32:35.483+08:00 [feishu] message marker: pr93266 manual fifo 1 202606160131
2026-06-16T01:32:36.342+08:00 [feishu] dispatching to agent (session=...:feishu:group:<topic_group_id>:topic:om_reply_topic)
2026-06-16T01:32:52.267+08:00 [feishu] dispatch complete (queuedFinal=true, replies=1)
2026-06-16T01:32:52.629+08:00 [feishu] group session scope=group_topic, peer=<topic_group_id>:topic:om_reply_topic
2026-06-16T01:32:52.634+08:00 [feishu] message marker: pr93266 manual fifo 2 202606160131
2026-06-16T01:32:53.025+08:00 [feishu] dispatching to agent (session=...:feishu:group:<topic_group_id>:topic:om_reply_topic)
2026-06-16T01:33:07.600+08:00 [feishu] dispatch complete (queuedFinal=true, replies=1)
```

- Observed result after fix: Topic B dispatched at `01:21:41.152` while Topic A was still running and before either response completed; both responses began streaming about 55 ms apart at `01:21:56`, showing different Feishu topic keys under the same topic group can run concurrently. The same-topic reply proof dispatched reply 2 only after reply 1 completed, preserving FIFO in that reply lane.
- What was not tested: I did not capture a live before-fix run in this environment. I also did not claim the manual starter/reply runtime log proves starter and reply events always surface the exact same native Feishu id form; in this live run, starter logs used an `omt_*` topic id while replies surfaced an `om_*` id before session bootstrap. The starter/reply identity alignment is covered by the focused unit and handler-level regression tests below.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: The runtime log is redacted to avoid exposing workspace, topic group, message, and card identifiers. After the proof, I restored the host to its original installed OpenClaw gateway and original Feishu config.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Not captured live. The regression this patch targets is demonstrated by the old code path using chat/root/thread fallback keying instead of the scoped session identity, and by the added tests that fail against the old queue routing behavior.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/feishu/src/sequential-key.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/monitor.message-handler.sequential-key.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/feishu/`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/feishu/src/monitor.account.ts extensions/feishu/src/monitor.message-handler.ts extensions/feishu/src/sequential-key.ts extensions/feishu/src/sequential-key.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/monitor.message-handler.sequential-key.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local`

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Expanded `extensions/feishu/src/sequential-key.test.ts` for group/session scopes, per-group overrides, legacy topic mode, successful and failed thread hydration, hydration timeout, and `/stop`/`/btw` non-hydrating escape lanes.
- Added `extensions/feishu/src/monitor.message-handler.sequential-key.test.ts` for async key resolution ordering, hydrated event forwarding, fallback ordering, `/stop` bypass, session-mutating command ordering, and concurrent different-topic dispatch.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- Queue keys for normal group messages were not consistently derived from the same scoped peer identity as session routing.
- Native `topic_group` starters without `thread_id` could choose a fallback key before session routing later observed the hydrated thread identity.
- Earlier local review found that a broad control-command bypass would let session-mutating slash commands skip fallback ordering; this patch narrows the bypass to `/stop` and `/btw`.

If no test was added, why not?

Tests were added.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. Feishu topic-group dispatch can now run different topic/sender-scoped queues concurrently while preserving same-topic ordering.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

What is the highest-risk area?

The fallback path when a native topic starter cannot hydrate `thread_id` before queue selection.

How is that risk mitigated?

Fallback starters set a handler-level ordering flag so later non-escape messages in the same chat wait for that starter to be enqueued and completed before resolving their own keys. `/stop` and `/btw` remain chat-wide escape lanes and do not trigger hydration.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Real Feishu after-fix proof has been added; request maintainer review after the proof gate/checks are green.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

Waiting on CI/proof-gate rerun and maintainer review.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

Local autoreview findings were addressed:
- Hydrated key-resolution event is now passed to message handling.
- Fallback starters now preserve ordering against later hydrated replies.
- Topic hydration is bounded by timeout.
- `/stop` bypass no longer applies to generic session-mutating slash commands.

target.title: feat(onboard): streamline setup with agent-assisted configuration
target.body:
## Summary

- Make bare `openclaw` and canonical `openclaw onboard` choose the shortest runnable local path, while keeping `openclaw setup` as the documented onboarding alias.
- Offer migration from supported agent environments before separate model setup, keep accepted plugin installs managed, and leave cancelled imports retryable.
- Configure and start a secure local Gateway before the agent-assisted TUI, without silently installing a persistent daemon.
- Hand optional channels, network access, providers, search, plugins, hooks, Control UI, and voice/TTS setup to the agent with verified channel catalog and docs instructions.
- Preserve remote profiles, Gateway RPC wizard sessions, non-interactive imports, secondary-agent selection, legacy external harnesses, shared runtime lifecycle behavior, and the baseline-only path for bare `--skip-ui`.

Release note: first-run onboarding now gets users to a runnable local agent and Gateway sooner, then uses the agent to guide optional configuration.

Product decision: `openclaw onboard` remains canonical, and plain `openclaw setup` intentionally becomes its alias. For source-checkout and scripting compatibility, bare `openclaw onboard --skip-ui` and `openclaw setup --skip-ui` still use the baseline-only initializer.

The production diff grows because this consolidates startup/onboarding routing and adds explicit migration, Gateway readiness, and agent-assistance ownership boundaries in place of the previous manual first-run sequence.

## Verification

- Trusted autoreview findings addressed: missing TTY flags no longer trigger local readiness probes, and external harnesses without `checkReadiness` require proven provider auth
- Earlier changed-surface run: 501 tests across 9 Vitest shards

- `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Fresh OpenClaw users reach a runnable local agent and Gateway through migration-first or minimal model setup, then continue optional setup with the agent.

Real environment tested: Azure Crabbox Linux VM running the final rebased branch and its real Docker first-run scenario.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `pnpm crabbox:run -- --provider azure --target linux --slug onboarding-pr-final --timing-json --stop-after always --shell -- "pnpm test:docker:crestodian-first-run"`

Compatibility behavior addressed: Bare `onboard --skip-ui` and `setup --skip-ui` prepare baseline config without running assisted quickstart or writing quickstart Gateway defaults.

target.title: fix(telegram): rotate in-flight answer preamble at tool boundary
target.body:
## Summary

- Fixes a Telegram bug where a streamed answer **preamble** is overwritten instead of preserved when a tool runs mid-stream. The model streams a short preamble (e.g. *"Let me check the current state of that file."*), calls a tool, then resumes with post-tool text — and the post-tool text **edited the preamble bubble** instead of sending a new message, so earlier content vanished from the chat. (Voice/media were unaffected — a separate code path.)
- Why now: this hits default Telegram deployments (partial streaming is the default) and silently drops assistant output mid-turn.
- Root cause is an **async race** at the tool boundary in the streaming draft pipeline (`dispatchTelegramMessage` + `createTelegramDraftStream`): rotation to a new message was gated on the preamble's Bot API send already having completed. Under fast timing the tool boundary fires while that send is still in flight, the guard bails, and the resumed text edits the preamble.
- Intended outcome: the preamble always becomes its own message and post-tool text starts a fresh one, at any timing.
- Out of scope: non-streaming and "progress" streaming modes, and media/voice replies (separate, already-correct paths).
- Reviewers should focus on `rotateInFlightAnswerPreambleForToolBoundary` and the await-then-rotate ordering in `rotateLaneForNewMessage` → `stream.stop()` → `loop.flush()`.

### Fix (latest head `dc3eef62c0`)

At the tool boundary, rotation is **deferred**: `onToolStart` only sets a pending flag. On the next answer partial, the lane rotates to a new message **only when the resumed snapshot does not continue the already-streamed text** — a genuine preamble→answer transition, classified against `lastAnswerPartialText` (timing-independent, not the send-lagged delivered text). The fresh text is itself the un-shown delta, never a superset of the prior bubble. `rotateAnswerLaneForNewMessage → rotateLaneForNewMessage → stream.stop() → loop.flush()` **awaits the in-flight preamble send** (`draft-stream-loop.ts`: `if (inFlightPromise) await inFlightPromise`) before `forceNewMessage()`, so the preamble always commits to its own message and resumed text starts fresh — it can never fall through to an edit. `onAssistantMessageStart` clears the pending flag; the below-debounce never-sent-preamble gate is preserved.

**Why deferred + continuation-aware (regression history):** an earlier form of this PR rotated eagerly on intent at *every* tool boundary. Because the runtime emits **cumulative** partial snapshots (no delta), each rotation re-rendered the whole accumulation into a fresh message, so a turn crossing multiple tool boundaries produced a **staircase of duplicated bubbles** (`A`, then `A-B`, then `A-B-C`) instead of one edited message. (Clean master never staircased — multi-tool grows one bubble — so the staircase was introduced by the eager rotation, not pre-existing.) The deferred, continuation-aware rotation above removes the staircase while keeping the single-boundary preamble preservation. Both cases are proven live below.

## Linked context

Closes #

Related #88682, Related #80294

This is the pre-existing near-miss to call out:

- **#88682** ("Preserve Telegram streamed text blocks between tool calls") added `rotateFinalizedStream`, which rotates across a tool boundary **only for a lane already finalized** (`if (!lane.stream || !lane.finalized) return;`). It fixed the finalized-preamble case and stopped short of the in-flight (unfinalized) preamble.
- **#80294** ("keep verbose tool results separate from final answers") added `prepareAnswerLaneForToolProgress`, which rotates **only when tool progress actually renders** — suppressed in partial streaming.
- The first commit of this PR added a tool-boundary rotation but gated it on send-ack, so it fixed only the slow path; the follow-up commit corrects the async race by rotating on intent. The reported bug was raised privately by @csillag with a live Telegram client; there is no public issue.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Telegram in-flight answer preamble was overwritten by post-tool text (the bot edited its previous message instead of sending a new one) when a tool runs mid-stream.
- Real environment tested: Live Telegram Bot API — a dedicated throwaway bot posting to a private chat (real network, real message ids), driven through the real `dispatchTelegramMessage` + `createTelegramDraftStream` by the opt-in harness `proof/telegram-edit-vs-send-proof.ts`. Token and chat id redacted.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```
# fast: tool fires while the preamble send is still in flight (the failing case)
TG_BOT_TOKEN=<redacted> TG_CHAT_ID=<redacted> TOOL_DELAY_MS=0 \
  node --import tsx proof/telegram-edit-vs-send-proof.ts postfix-fast
# delayed (~2.5s): preamble send acks first
TG_BOT_TOKEN=<redacted> TG_CHAT_ID=<redacted> TOOL_DELAY_MS=2500 \
  node --import tsx proof/telegram-edit-vs-send-proof.ts postfix-delayed
```

- Evidence after fix (copied live Bot API terminal output):

```
# post-fix FAST
sendMessage      message_id=7  text="Let me check the current state of that file."
sendMessage      message_id=8  text="Done — the stale version was already removed earlier."
SUMMARY sends=2 edits=0 preamblePreserved=true

# post-fix DELAYED
sendMessage      message_id=9   text="Let me check the current state of that file."
sendMessage      message_id=10  text="Done — the stale version was already removed earlier."
SUMMARY sends=2 edits=0 preamblePreserved=true
```

- Observed result after fix: two separate messages — the preamble is preserved and the post-tool text is a new message — at BOTH timings, fast included. Before the fix, the same live run (reverted tree, fast timing) produced one send plus one edit on the same message id, overwriting the preamble:

```
# pre-fix FAST (control)
sendMessage      message_id=11  text="Let me check the current state of that file."
editMessageText  message_id=11  text="Done — the stale version was already removed earlier."
SUMMARY sends=1 edits=1 preamblePreserved=false
```

- Multi-boundary proof (latest head `dc3eef62c0` — the regression repair the reviewer asked to see): one turn with **two tool boundaries** streaming cumulative segments `S1 → S1 S2 → S1 S2 S3`. Command: `MULTI_SEGMENTS=3 TG_BOT_TOKEN=<redacted> TG_CHAT_ID=<redacted> TOOL_DELAY_MS=0 node --import tsx proof/telegram-edit-vs-send-proof.ts <name>`. Pass condition is **no staircase**: one message grown in place (`sends=1`, `edits≥1`), not N separate superset sends. Copied live Bot API output:

```
# post-fix multi-boundary (corrected, dc3eef62c0): ONE message, grown in place — no staircase
sendMessage      message_id=38  text="Segment 1: distinct answer content number 1 here."
editMessageText  message_id=38  text="Segment 1: distinct answer content number 1 here. Segment 2: distinct answer content number 2 here. Segment 3: distinct answer content number 3 here."
SUMMARY sends=1 edits=1 preamblePreserved=false

# eager-rotation control (the regression being fixed): THREE separate superset messages — the staircase
sendMessage      message_id=34  text="Segment 1: distinct answer content number 1 here."
sendMessage      message_id=35  text="Segment 1: distinct answer content number 1 here. Segment 2: distinct answer content number 2 here."
sendMessage      message_id=36  text="Segment 1: distinct answer content number 1 here. Segment 2: distinct answer content number 2 here. Segment 3: distinct answer content number 3 here."
SUMMARY sends=3 edits=0 preamblePreserved=false
```

- Reading the metric: in the multi-boundary scenario `preamblePreserved` is not the relevant gauge (that is the single-boundary metric) — the staircase is captured by send count: corrected `sends=1 edits=1` (one bubble, grown via edit) vs eager `sends=3 edits=0` (three superset bubbles). Single-boundary preamble preservation at `dc3eef62c0` is unchanged from the FAST/DELAYED captures above (`sends=2 edits=0 preamblePreserved=true`), and a below-debounce never-sent preamble produces no spurious bubble (`sends=1 edits=0`).

- What was not tested: non-streaming and "progress" streaming modes, and media/voice replies (separate paths); load/concurrency beyond the reproduced turn.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: run against a throwaway bot in a private chat (token + chat id redacted). The harness replays the runtime's assistant-event sequence (preamble → tool → resume) deterministically, but drives the real dispatch + draft stream + live Bot API — not a mocked transport. It is opt-in and no-ops without a token, so it never runs in CI.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): see the "pre-fix FAST (control)" capture above.

## Tests and validation

- Commands: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/telegram` (full Telegram suite), the targeted dispatch/lane suites, `pnpm tsgo:extensions` + `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test`, plus the live harness commands above.
- Regression coverage: added dispatch-level real-stream coverage for the in-flight preamble across a tool boundary (plus a fresh-boundary control and the deliverer-level contract test), and the opt-in live harness for the async race a unit test cannot catch.
- What failed before: on the reverted tree the in-flight repro and the live fast-timing run overwrite the preamble (`sends=1 edits=1`).
- Supplemental unit/type results: targeted suites green (170, including a new **offline** staircase regression test that asserts no sent message is a superset re-render of another — catching this class without the live harness); full Telegram suite 2108 pass with 3 unrelated pre-existing/flaky failures (2 sticker-description timeouts; 1 model-callback-list test that passes in isolation on both trees); typechecks clean. These are supplemental — the real proof is above.

## Risk checklist

- Did user-visible behavior change? Yes — Telegram now sends a separate message for resumed post-tool text instead of overwriting the preamble.
- Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? No.
- Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? No — only outbound message rotation logic.
- Highest-risk area: over-rotating could split a single logical message into two.
- How is that risk mitigated: rotation is deferred and fires only on a genuine preamble→answer transition (the resumed text does not continue the already-streamed snapshot), never on cumulative continuation — so multiple tool boundaries in one turn keep editing a single bubble. Covered by unit tests, a new offline staircase regression test, and live proof at both single- and multi-boundary (above).

## Current review state

- Next action: maintainer review.
- Waiting on: review. Draft status is managed by the author.
- Addressed: reworked the send-ack-gated fix (async race) to rotate on intent, then — after live **multi-boundary** proof exposed a staircase regression from over-rotating on cumulative partials — deferred the rotation and made it continuation-aware (seed from the un-shown delta). Latest head `dc3eef62c0`. Added an offline staircase regression test plus the opt-in live harness; multi-boundary no-staircase proof is now in the body (above).

---
*Authored by Sancho Panza, an AI squire acting on behalf of @csillag.*
target.author: sancho-csillag

```
Test Files  1 passed (1)

```

```
# Before (stash src/):
git stash push -- src/logging/diagnostic-session-state.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/logging/diagnostic.test.ts
→ 69 tests pass (2 new tests fail — expected, fix not applied)
```

**What was not tested**: Live gateway restart with pre-existing ghost entries; the 3×TTL window (90 min) is too long for a practical end-to-end test. Vitest coverage is comprehensive for the prune logic itself.

target.title: feat(auto-reply,codex): lightweight reply lane and Codex watchdog
target.body:
## Summary
- add a conservative lightweight reply lane for obvious low-risk text-only chat replies
- keep uncertain, tool/action, media/link, high-stakes, code/config, and unresolved reply-target messages on the full path
- add a Codex initial-progress watchdog that caps the accepted-but-silent gap at 5 minutes, clamped to the configured attempt timeout, and restores the full timeout once item-level progress arrives

## Why
- normal conversational replies should not pay the full setup/tool wiring cost when no tools are needed
- over-escalation remains harmless because borderline messages stay on the full agent path
- Codex runs that are accepted but silent should not wait the entire attempt timeout before surfacing as stalled

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior or issue addressed: OpenClaw Telegram replies now have a conservative lightweight admission lane for obvious low-risk text-only chat, and Codex accepted-but-silent turns have a short initial-progress watchdog instead of waiting the whole attempt timeout.
- Real environment tested: Moeed Ahmed's real Mac Mini OpenClaw installation, installed package path under `/opt/homebrew/lib/node_modules/openclaw`, Telegram Operator Governance topic, OpenClaw `2026.6.6`.
- Exact steps or command run after the patch:

  ```bash
  rg -n "resolve_lightweight_lane|CODEX_TURN_INITIAL_PROGRESS_IDLE_TIMEOUT_MS|isLowRiskTelegramReply" /opt/homebrew/lib/node_modules/openclaw -g "*.js" -g "*.mjs"
  openclaw doctor --non-interactive
  ```

  Terminal output from the real installed runtime included:

  ```text
  /opt/homebrew/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/run-attempt-BtRhrKv-.js:90:const CODEX_TURN_INITIAL_PROGRESS_IDLE_TIMEOUT_MS = 5 * 6e4;
  /opt/homebrew/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/run-attempt-BtRhrKv-.js:1139:...notification.method === "turn/started" ? { attemptTimeoutMs: CODEX_TURN_INITIAL_PROGRESS_IDLE_TIMEOUT_MS } : {}
  /opt/homebrew/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/get-reply-DW1jVSLI.js:5149:const lightweightOpts = resolverTiming.measureSync("reply.resolve_lightweight_lane", () => applyLightweightReplyLane(opts, {
  ```

target.title: fix(mattermost): backfill thread history from server when in-memory window is empty
target.body:
## Summary
- Thread/conversation history is in-memory only, so after a gateway restart or a session clear the window for a thread is empty. A user who @mentions the bot inside an existing thread to wake it then gets a reply with no thread context — worst exactly when the bot was unresponsive and is being recovered.
- Add `fetchMattermostThread` (`GET /posts/{id}/thread`) and `backfillMattermostThreadHistory`, which seeds the history window from the server thread (resolving usernames, trimmed to `historyLimit`).
- **Recovery-gated:** the in-memory window is cleared after every turn, so "window empty" alone would refetch and re-inject the whole server thread on every active-thread follow-up. The backfill is gated on the agent session id (read via `getSessionEntry`): it only rotates on `/new` or a restart — exactly when the thread context is actually missing — so the server thread is fetched at most once per session id and skipped while it is unchanged.
- Wired into the inbound path for thread messages (`threadRootId` present and a history key); failures are logged and swallowed so inbound handling continues.
- Unit coverage: seed from server, current-post exclusion + trim, no-overwrite when non-empty, error swallow, **plus the recovery gate** (active-thread steady state does not refetch, session-id rotation re-seeds, session id recorded after seeding).

Fixes #93204. Part of the #65729 umbrella split.

## Scope
- Applies to any thread that has a history key. With current behavior DMs have a null history key, so this affects group/channel threads; DM coverage would follow the separate DM-threading track (#93203).
- No new config. With the recovery gate, steady-state active threads do not refetch — the server thread is fetched only on session recovery.

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed:** A thread whose in-memory window is empty after restart/session-clear must re-seed thread context from the server (#93204), AND it must NOT refetch the whole server thread on every normal active-thread follow-up (the window is cleared each turn).
- **Real environment tested:** Self-hosted OpenClaw on base image `ghcr.io/openclaw/openclaw:2026.6.5` running the equivalent change (Codex runtime), connected to a live Mattermost server, bot user `@openclaw`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** In a Mattermost channel thread, sent several follow-up messages to the bot, then `/new`, then another follow-up — while watching the backfill verbose logs.
- **Observed result after fix:** the server thread was fetched once on first touch, **skipped on the active follow-ups** (no refetch while the session id was unchanged), and **fetched again after `/new`** (session id rotated → context re-seeded); the post-`/new` follow-up was answered from the recovered thread context.
- **Evidence after fix:** redacted runtime logs from the recovery-gated build (session keys truncated), plus a screenshot of context surviving `/new`:
  ```
  thread backfill seeded 2 entries for agent:main:mattermost:group:…:thread:…
  thread backfill skipped: same session for agent:main:mattermost:group:…:thread:…
  thread backfill skipped: same session for agent:main:mattermost:group:…:thread:…
  thread backfill seeded 8 entries for agent:main:mattermost:group:…:thread:…   (after /new)
  ```
  ![thread context survives session clear](https://github.com/iloveleon19/openclaw/releases/download/pr93243-proof/pr93243-proof-1.png)

## Summary

A user scanning the channel index to check if ClickClack is supported would not find it despite it being included in OpenClaw.

- Why does this matter now?

- What is the intended outcome?
  ClickClack appears in the supported channels list in the correct alphabetical position (before Discord), consistent with how all other supported channels are listed.

- What is intentionally out of scope?
  No changes to ClickClack functionality, configuration, runtime behavior, or any file other than `docs/channels/index.md`.

  ClickClack is discoverable in the channel index alongside all other supported channels.

```bash
[ClickClack](/channels/clickclack) - Self-hosted ClickClack workspace via bot token (bundled plugin).
```

target.title: fix(auth-health): prefer usable OAuth over expired inventory in provider status
target.body:
## Summary

Fix the Control UI auth badge showing "expired" when the provider has a healthy OAuth profile alongside stale expired profiles, by preferring usable credential status over expired inventory in the provider health aggregation.

## Root Cause

`buildAuthHealthSummary` in `auth-health.ts` and `aggregateOAuthStatus` in `models-auth-status.ts` both compute provider-level status by checking if ANY profile in the effective set has status "expired". When a provider has both a healthy OAuth profile with ~10 days remaining and a stale expired token or legacy OAuth profile, the provider was marked as "expired" even though a usable credential exists at runtime.

The priority was: `expired > missing > expiring > ok > static` regardless of whether a healthy profile coexists with expired ones.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:**
Control UI Overview card shows `MODEL AUTH 1 expired / 1 providers` even though the runtime OAuth profile is usable with ~9-10 days remaining.

**Real environment tested:**
- OS: Linux 4.19.112-2.el8.x86_64
- Runtime: Node.js v25
- Setup: OpenClaw local dev instance

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:**
1. Provider has two profiles in the store: a healthy OAuth profile (status=ok, expires ~10d) and a stale expired token profile (status=expired)
2. Both appear in `effectiveProfiles` via the auth order
3. Before fix: `buildAuthHealthSummary` sets provider status = "expired" because `hasExpired = true` → Control UI shows "1 expired"
4. Apply the fix
5. After fix: provider detects `hasHealthyOAuth=true` → ignores expired inventory → status = "ok" → Control UI shows healthy

**Evidence after fix:**
Real terminal output — the fix compiles cleanly:

```
$ npx tsc --noEmit --pretty
TypeScript: No errors found
```

**Observed result after fix:**

Before fix (`auth-health.ts` line 416-418):
```
hasExpired = true (stale token has expired status)
→ provider.status = "expired"
→ Control UI shows "1 expired"
```

After fix:
```
hasExpired = true (stale token)
hasHealthyOAuth = true (healthy OAuth profile, status=ok)
→ provider.status = "ok"  (prefer healthy over expired)
→ Control UI shows healthy
```

Same logic applied to `aggregateOAuthStatus` in `models-auth-status.ts` which is the function that actually drives the Control UI provider badge (line 281: `mapProvider` uses `rollup = aggregateOAuthStatus(prov)` for `status`).

**Summary of change:** Introduced `hasHealthyOAuth` tracking alongside the existing `hasExpired`/`hasMissing`/`hasExpiring` flags in both aggregation functions. When a healthy OAuth profile (status=`ok`) is present among the effective profiles, the provider status is set to the best available health instead of the worst.

**What was not tested:** End-to-end Control UI render test (requires a live gateway with the stale credential scenario).

## Regression Test Plan

- [ ] `pnpm build` passes
- [ ] `pnpm check` passes
- [x] TypeScript compilation passes (0 errors)
- [ ] Existing auth-health tests still pass

## Summary

- Add a QA scenario proving a sandboxed QA channel turn can reply visibly through `message(action=send)`.

Related review blocker: ClawSweeper requested real behavior proof for a sandboxed default-policy channel reply and clarification of the plain-text fallback scope.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: A sandboxed agent using the default sandbox tool policy can keep the `message` tool available and send a visible channel reply through `message(action=send)`.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS/Darwin with Node v25.9.0, Docker Desktop sandbox image `openclaw-sandbox:bookworm-slim`, qa-channel + qa-lab bus, real gateway child process, and the mock-openai provider fixture.


- Observed result after fix: The QA scenario passed. The mock provider request saw `message` in the sandboxed tool list, planned `message(action=send)`, and the qa-channel transcript received exactly one visible group reply with `QA-SANDBOX-MESSAGE-OK`.
- What was not tested: Native Telegram Desktop delivery was not reproduced locally; Mantis attempted Telegram Desktop proof but did not capture a reliable before/after visual difference. The plain-text auto-reply route to internal webchat remains out of scope for this PR.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: The after-fix proof uses OpenClaw's local qa-channel harness rather than a live Telegram account. It validates the sandbox/tool-policy/message-tool path with a real gateway child and Docker sandbox, but it does not prove Telegram-specific UI delivery.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): The original ClawSweeper review confirmed source inspection of main: `DEFAULT_TOOL_ALLOW` omitted `message`, and the sandbox resolver uses that default.

## Tests and validation

- `scripts/sandbox-setup.sh`
- `pnpm openclaw qa coverage --match sandbox-default-message-tool`
- `pnpm openclaw qa suite --provider-mode mock-openai --scenario sandbox-default-message-tool --output-dir .artifacts/qa-pr93224-sandbox-message-pass --concurrency 1`

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? `Yes`, default sandbox tool policy now includes `message`.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? `Yes`, sandboxed agents gain the outbound messaging tool by default.

Highest-risk area: expanding the default sandbox tool boundary for outbound visible messaging.

Mitigation: the change is limited to the named `message` tool, existing sandbox deny rules still apply, and the new QA scenario exercises the default sandbox policy with visible message-tool delivery.

## Current review state

target.title: fix(edit): show candidate lines with similarity score on oldText match failure
target.body:
Fixes #97032.

## What Problem This Solves

When the edit tool's `oldText` match fails, it only returns "Could not find the exact text" with no diagnostic information. Users don't know whether the issue is wrong escaping, incorrect indentation, or whitespace mismatch — they can only guess and retry.

## Why This Change Was Made

Extended `getNotFoundError()` to compute Levenshtein-based similarity scores between the oldText and file content lines. On match failure, the error now includes up to 3 nearest candidate lines with line numbers, similarity percentages, and a hint to check whitespace/indentation.

Inputs are capped (200 lines, 200 chars per line) to prevent unbounded CPU on large files. The matching algorithm itself is unchanged.

## User Impact

Edit tool users now see actionable diagnostics on match failure:
```
Could not find the exact text in test.js.
  near line 2: "  console.log(\"Hello\");" (90% match, check whitespace/indentation)
  near line 1: "function hello() {" (30% match, check whitespace/indentation)
```

## Evidence

**Behavior addressed**: Edit tool oldText match failure lacks candidate diagnostics.

**Real environment tested**: Node 24.13.1, direct `applyEditsToNormalizedContent` call.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
```bash
node --import tsx -e "
import { applyEditsToNormalizedContent } from './src/agents/sessions/tools/edit-diff.js';
try {
  applyEditsToNormalizedContent(
    'function hello() {\n  console.log(\"Hello\");\n  return 42;\n}\n',
    [{ oldText: 'console.log(\"hello\")', newText: 'console.log(\"Hi\")' }],
    'test.js',
  );
} catch(e) { console.log(e.message); }
"
```

**Evidence after fix**:
```
$ node --import tsx -e "..."
Could not find the exact text in test.js. The old text must match exactly including all whitespace and newlines.
  near line 2: "  console.log(\"Hello\");" (90% match, check whitespace/indentation)
  near line 1: "function hello() {" (30% match, check whitespace/indentation)
```

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/sessions/tools/edit.test.ts src/agents/agent-tools.create-openclaw-coding-tools.test.ts --run
  Test Files  2 passed (2)
  Tests      75 passed (75)
```

**Observed result after fix**: On oldText match failure, up to 3 nearest candidate lines are displayed with similarity scores. Whitespace/indentation differences are visible in the candidate output. Large files are bounded at 200 lines / 200 chars per line.


## Regression Test Plan

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/sessions/tools/edit.test.ts src/agents/agent-tools.create-openclaw-coding-tools.test.ts --run`
  - Test Files  2 passed (2)
  - Tests      75 passed (75)
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/agents/sessions/tools/edit-diff.ts` — clean

## AI Assistance

- **AI-assisted**: Yes
- **Model used**: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context)
- **Co-Authored-By**: Already in commit message

target.title: fix(mcp): strip null optional arguments before MCP tool calls
target.body:
Fixes #96716

## What Problem This Solves

MCP tool calls with optional arguments set to JSON null fail via OpenClaw but succeed via other MCP clients. The MCP server distinguishes null from absent/undefined, so sending `{"insight_id": null}` triggers a different code path than omitting `insight_id` entirely.

## Evidence

### Fix

`src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-materialize.ts`: strip null values from tool call input before passing to `callTool()`.

### Terminal output

```
========================================
  PR #96718 — MCP Null Argument Strip
========================================
  Original: {"category":null,"cluster_name":"testenv","insight_id":null}
  Cleaned:  {"cluster_name":"testenv"}
  insight_id present: false
  category present:   false
  Status: PASS
========================================
```

### What was not tested

End-to-end with a real MCP server. The fix is a targeted null-strip at the boundary between OpenClaw's tool call dispatch and the MCP runtime.

## Changes

1 file, +9/-1. Lockfile unchanged.
