target.title: fix: support baseURL alias in memory embedding provider config resolution
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

When users configure `models.providers.openai.baseURL` (camelCase with capital R) pointing to a local Ollama or OpenAI-compatible endpoint, `ModelProviderSchema` silently drops the `baseURL` key via `.strict()`. Memory embeddings then fall through to the built-in OpenAI adapter and route to `api.openai.com/v1` instead of the configured local endpoint.

Fixes #93396

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/config/zod-schema.core.ts:495-522` | Accept `baseURL` as optional field + normalize to `baseUrl` in `superRefine`. Downstream code only reads `baseUrl` — no changes needed. URL redaction is auto-fixed (covers `baseUrl` paths). |
| `src/agents/memory-search.ts:182-185` | Skip built-in adapter when provider has custom `baseUrl`. Before: a `providerId === "openai"` match returned the direct adapter unconditionally. After: checks `providerConfig.baseUrl` and skips when configured. |
| `src/config/schema.test.ts` | +2 tests: normalization of `baseURL` → `baseUrl`, and `baseUrl` priority over `baseURL` |
| `src/agents/memory-search.test.ts` | +2 tests: skip adapter with custom baseUrl, use adapter without one |

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Fix for issue #93396 — `models.providers.*.baseURL` is accepted by `.strict()` and normalized to canonical `baseUrl`, routing memory embeddings to the configured endpoint instead of `api.openai.com/v1`.

**Real setup tested**: Node.js v24.13.1 on Linux x86_64

**Exact steps or command run after fix**:
```bash
# 1. Schema normalization — real production code path
node --import tsx /tmp/verify-baseurl-normalize.mjs

# 2. Tests
cd $WORKTREE
pnpm test src/agents/memory-search.test.ts src/config/schema.test.ts
```

**After-fix evidence**:
```
$ node --import tsx /tmp/verify-baseurl-normalize.mjs

=== Test 1: baseURL → baseUrl normalization ===
Input:  { baseURL: 'http://127.0.0.1:11434/v1' }
Output: baseUrl = "http://127.0.0.1:11434/v1"
PASS: true

=== Test 2: baseUrl already set → baseURL ignored ===
Input:  baseUrl='https://my-proxy.example.com/v1', baseURL='http://ignore-me.local:11434/v1'
Output: baseUrl = "https://my-proxy.example.com/v1"
PASS: true

=== Test 3: Memory search routing ===
Provider 'openai' with custom baseURL:
  embedding provider: skipped (custom endpoint)
PASS: true (built-in adapter skipped)

✅ All 3 real-behavior checks pass

$ pnpm test src/agents/memory-search.test.ts src/config/schema.test.ts
 ✓ |unit-fast| src/agents/memory-search.test.ts (37 tests)
 ✓ |runtime-config| src/config/schema.test.ts (57 tests)

 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  94 passed (94)
```

**Observed result after the fix**: `baseURL` passes `.strict()` validation. The existing `superRefine` normalizes it to `baseUrl` in-place, so all downstream call sites (memory search, provider config, embedding client, remote client, gateway `/v1/embeddings`) work without changes — they already read `baseUrl`. URL redaction also works because `baseUrl` is already in the sensitive URL path list.

**What was not tested**: End-to-end with a running Ollama instance serving embeddings. Schema normalization and routing logic are independently verified.

## Tests and validation

```bash
pnpm test src/agents/memory-search.test.ts src/config/schema.test.ts
# Test Files  2 passed (2)
#      Tests  94 passed (94)
```

*AI-assisted: built with Claude Code*

target.title: fix(googlechat): replace unbounded response.json() with readProviderJsonResponse
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`extensions/googlechat/src/api.ts` (`readGoogleChatJsonResponse`) and `extensions/googlechat/src/auth.ts` (`readGoogleChatCertsResponse`) both call `await response.json()` with no byte-level cap. A hostile or malfunctioning Google Chat API endpoint (or an intermediary) can return an oversized JSON body that exhausts process memory during JSON parsing.

This is the same bounded-read pattern Alix-007 applied to 15+ other extensions (`readProviderJsonResponse`, 16 MiB cap). Google Chat was one of the remaining unbounded `.json()` call sites.

## Changes

- `extensions/googlechat/src/api.ts:15-19` — `readGoogleChatJsonResponse` now delegates to `readProviderJsonResponse<T>(response, label)`. The local `try { return await response.json() } catch (cause) { throw new Error(..., { cause }) }` wrapper is replaced by the SDK helper which provides the same error shape (`${label}: malformed JSON response`) plus a 16 MiB byte cap.
- `extensions/googlechat/src/auth.ts:19-25` — `readGoogleChatCertsResponse` now delegates to `readProviderJsonResponse<Record<string, string>>(response, "Google Chat cert fetch failed")`. Error message preserved identically.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: unbounded `response.json()` on Google Chat API and cert HTTP responses; after the fix reads are capped at 16 MiB.
- **Real environment tested**: real `node:http` server (127.0.0.1, chunked transfer, no Content-Length) driving production `readProviderJsonResponse`. Node v22.22.0, Linux x86_64.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```bash
  node --import tsx _proof_googlechat_bounded.mts
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**:
  ```
  === Google Chat JSON bounded-read assertions ===

    PASS  hostile body: overflow error thrown — Google Chat JSON: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes
    PASS  hostile body: cap value in error — cap=16777216
    PASS  hostile body: bytes read ≈ cap — server sent ~16.0 MiB (cap=16 MiB)
    PASS  hostile body: not full body — server sent 16 MiB, would have been 32 MiB without cap
    PASS  hostile body: connection handled — reader stopped before full body
    PASS  negative control: small body parses — status=ok
    PASS  negative control: bytes < cap — 0 KiB
    PASS  happy path: valid JSON parses — text="hello"
    PASS  happy path: bytes < cap — 0 KiB

  Results: 9/9 passed
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: `readProviderJsonResponse` threw canonical overflow at 16 MiB (JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes). Server sent ~16.0 MiB of a 32 MiB hostile payload — capped before full body. Negative control (small JSON, status=ok) and happy path (valid JSON, text="hello") parsed correctly. Server-side bytes-sent confirmed cap activation at 16.0 MiB, not the full 32 MiB.
- **What was not tested**: live Google Chat API call (the proof exercises the same production SDK helper against the same attack shape the live endpoint could carry); cross-platform Node differences (Node 22 only, matches CI).

## Out of scope

- `extensions/googlechat/src/api.ts:117` (`fetchBuffer`) already uses `readResponseWithLimit` for media downloads — not affected.
- Other extensions (image-generation, etc.) — separate per-surface PRs.

## Risk

- **Very low**: swap from a local `(await response.json()) as T` to `readProviderJsonResponse<T>(response, label)`. Error messages are preserved (`${label}: malformed JSON response`). The helper is already used by 15+ extensions. The byte cap (16 MiB, default) is invisible to normal-sized responses.
- **No SDK promotion**: `readProviderJsonResponse` has been in `openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http` since the Alix-007 bounded-read sweep.

AI-assisted; reviewed before submission.

target.title: feat(codex): scope user MCP server attachment by the tool allowlist
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

An OpenClaw operator running a **Codex-runtime agent** cannot give that agent access to a configured MCP server without also granting it Codex's full native code-mode shell/filesystem surface. The two are conflated behind a single gate.

In `extensions/codex/src/app-server/dynamic-tool-build.ts`, `shouldEnableCodexAppServerNativeToolSurface` returns true only when the effective tool allowlist contains a literal `*`. That one boolean (`nativeToolSurfaceEnabled`) is then reused, unchanged, in `attempt-startup.ts` as `userMcpServersEnabled` — which decides whether `buildCodexUserMcpServersThreadConfigPatch` projects `cfg.mcp.servers` into the Codex thread at all. Consequences:

1. **All-or-nothing.** Any least-privilege allowlist (`tools.allow` / `tools.alsoAllow` without `*`) silently disables **every** user MCP server, even ones the allowlist explicitly names. The MCP process spawns but its tools never register in the compiled toolset.
2. **Over-exposure when servers do attach.** `buildCodexUserMcpServersThreadConfigPatch` projected **every** enabled `cfg.mcp.servers` entry, filtered only by the per-server `codex.agents` field — it never consulted the tool allowlist.

This is a general gap for any scoped Codex agent with any MCP server, not a single integration.

## What Changed

Decouple the two capabilities and make MCP attachment flow through the **same allowlist contract OpenClaw already uses for every other tool** (`tool-policy.ts`):

- **Native code mode is unchanged** — `shouldEnableCodexAppServerNativeToolSurface` keeps its fail-closed, wildcard-only rule.
- **`shouldEnableUserMcpServersForCodexAppServer`** now answers only *"is it safe to attach external MCP servers this turn?"* (not a memory-flush run; OpenClaw sandbox, if active, can be honored). The per-server decision moves to the projection.
- **`resolveCodexMcpServerAllow`** matches against the **provider-safe model-facing tool ids** OpenClaw exposes — `<sanitizedServer>__<sanitizedTool>` via `sanitizeServerName` + `normalizeToolName`, **not** the raw `mcp.servers` key — and honors the same entries as the bundle-MCP allowlist contract:
  - `undefined` → include, all tools (legacy unrestricted behavior preserved)
  - `*`, `bundle-mcp`, `group:plugins` → include, all tools
  - `<server>__*` → include that server, all tools
  - `<server>__<tool>` → include that server, scoped to the named tool(s)
  - no matching token → exclude the server
- **`buildCodexUserMcpServersThreadConfigPatch`** projects **only** referenced servers, and for exact-tool entries emits Codex's per-server **`enabled_tools`** filter so other tools from an attached server stay unreachable. The thread-config fingerprint varies with the patch, so a thread rebinds when scope changes.

### Addresses ClawSweeper review
- **Provider-safe matching** (was raw-key): `Outlook Graph` (ids `outlook-graph__*`) resolves; `bundle-mcp` / `group:plugins` honored.
- **Per-tool scope** (was discarded): exact entries emit `enabled_tools` instead of attaching the whole server. (`enabled_tools` carries the sanitized tool fragment; exact for provider-safe names, fail-closed otherwise, since the live tool list isn't known at projection time.)

## Files
- `extensions/codex/src/app-server/{dynamic-tool-build,run-attempt,attempt-startup,thread-lifecycle}.ts` — decouple + plumb `userMcpServersEnabled`, pass `toolsAllow` to the projection
- `src/agents/cli-runner/bundle-mcp-codex.ts` — `resolveCodexMcpServerAllow` + allowlist-aware projection with `enabled_tools`
- `docs/plugins/codex-harness.md`; tests as below

## Evidence

### Unit tests (`vitest run`)
- `src/agents/cli-runner/bundle-mcp-codex.user-config.test.ts` — **24 passed** (undefined/`*`/`bundle-mcp`/`group:plugins` attach all; `opik__*` only opik, no `enabled_tools`; exact `opik__list,opik__read` → `enabled_tools:["list","read"]`; provider-safe `Outlook Graph` ↔ `outlook-graph__*`; `["message"]`/`[]` → none).
- `extensions/codex/src/app-server/dynamic-tool-build.test.ts` — **51 passed**, incl. the assertion that `shouldEnableCodexAppServerNativeToolSurface(["opik__*"]) === false` — **native code mode stays disabled** under a scoped MCP allowlist.

### Runtime proof — patched OpenClaw projection (real code, unmocked)
Driving the actual `buildCodexUserMcpServersThreadConfigPatch` over a config with `opik` + `notion` + `atlas`:
```
toolsAllow=undefined                    -> servers=["opik","notion","atlas"]  scope=all tools each
toolsAllow=["*"]                        -> servers=["opik","notion","atlas"]  scope=all tools each
toolsAllow=["opik__*"]                  -> servers=["opik"]                    scope={"opik":"(all tools)"}
toolsAllow=["opik__list","opik__read"]  -> servers=["opik"]                    scope={"opik":["list","read"]}
toolsAllow=["notion__*"]                -> servers=["notion"]                  scope={"notion":"(all tools)"}
toolsAllow=["message","web_search"]     -> servers=[]                          scope=none
```

### Runtime proof — real Codex app-server (`codex-cli 0.139.0`)
Drove the **real** `codex app-server` over JSON-RPC stdio with two stub stdio MCP servers (each exposing `list,read,write,schema`): `opik` configured with `enabled_tools=["list"]` (what the projection emits for `["opik__list"]`), `notion` unfiltered (the `["notion__*"]` case). Then `mcpServerStatus/list` (`detail: toolsAndAuthOnly`):
```
=== LIVE MCP TOOLS REGISTERED BY CODEX ===
opik:   [list]                       <- enabled_tools filter honored by the real binary
notion: [list, read, schema, write]  <- unscoped server, all tools
(other entries omitted: unrelated servers from local ~/.codex/config.toml)
```
This confirms the real Codex binary (a) treats `enabled_tools`/`disabled_tools` as first-class per-server fields (visible in `codex mcp get` schema output) and (b) registers **only** the scoped tool when the filter is set.

### Types
Full-CI `check-prod-types` / `check-lint` / `check-docs` pass; standalone extension `tsc` shows only pre-existing plugin-sdk `dist`-sync noise, none referencing this PR's symbols.

## Security posture
- Native code-mode shell/file access still requires a literal `*` — unchanged (proven above).
- Default-open for unrestricted (`undefined`) agents preserved; sandbox-without-exec-server still attaches no user MCP servers.
- Net effect is *tighter*: scoped allowlists attach only referenced servers; exact-tool entries attach only named tools via `enabled_tools`.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: feat(mcp): add shared bundled runtime scope
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #95506. Bundled MCP runtimes are session-scoped today, so matching sessions in the same workspace/config can repeatedly start the same MCP server process set and rebuild the same tool catalog. That is useful isolation by default, but it is expensive for deployments that intentionally share MCP state across sessions.

## Why This Change Was Made

This adds an opt-in `mcp.runtimeScope` config with the default preserved as `session`. When set to `shared`, the runtime manager keys a shared runtime by workspace directory plus the loaded MCP config fingerprint, attaches multiple session IDs to that runtime, reuses in-flight creation, and only disposes the runtime when the last attached session is gone or idle eviction applies. The shared runtime key is synthetic and hashed, so the underlying runtime is not accidentally tied to any one public session ID.

## User Impact

Existing configurations keep per-session isolation. Users who opt into `mcp.runtimeScope: "shared"` can reuse one bundled MCP runtime/catalog/process set across matching sessions, reducing MCP startup churn while still separating different workspaces or different MCP configs.

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts` passed: 40 tests, including real stdio MCP proof that default session scope starts two server processes/list calls for two sessions while shared scope reuses one runtime/server process/list call.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/schema.test.ts src/config/schema.hints.test.ts src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts src/config/doc-baseline.test.ts src/config/doc-baseline.integration.test.ts` passed: 2 shards, 31 tooling tests + 62 runtime-config tests.
- `corepack pnpm config:docs:check` passed.
- `corepack pnpm tsgo:core` passed.
- `git diff --check` passed.
- 2026-06-24 maintenance rebase on `af2b0a611`: re-ran `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts` (44 tests), `node --import tsx scripts/generate-config-doc-baseline.ts --check`, `corepack pnpm tsgo:core`, and `git diff --check`; all passed.
- 2026-06-24 redacted real OpenClaw bundled MCP runtime proof: `node --import tsx` invoked the production `getOrCreateSessionMcpRuntime` path with a real stdio MCP child process and two session IDs against the same workspace/config. Redacted terminal output:

```text
OpenClaw bundled MCP runtime proof

  runtime-object-reused=false
  runtime-a-session-id=session-session-a
  runtime-b-session-id=session-session-b
  catalog-a-tools=proofProbe/session_proof_tool
  catalog-b-tools=proofProbe/session_proof_tool

SCENARIO shared
  runtime-object-reused=true

  catalog-b-tools=proofProbe/shared_proof_tool

  unique-server-pids=58572
```

- `corepack pnpm tsgo:test:src` was attempted locally but produced no diagnostics and was interrupted after several minutes; not counted as passing evidence.

target.title: fix(mcp): strip null optional arguments before MCP tool calls
target.body:
Fixes #96716

## What Problem This Solves

MCP tool calls with optional arguments set to JSON null fail via OpenClaw but succeed via other MCP clients. The MCP server distinguishes null from absent/undefined, so sending `{"insight_id": null}` triggers a different code path than omitting `insight_id` entirely.

## Evidence

### Fix

`src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-materialize.ts`: strip null values from tool call input before passing to `callTool()`.

### Terminal output

```
========================================
  PR #96718 — MCP Null Argument Strip
========================================
  Original: {"category":null,"cluster_name":"testenv","insight_id":null}
  Cleaned:  {"cluster_name":"testenv"}
  insight_id present: false
  category present:   false
  Status: PASS
========================================
```

### What was not tested

End-to-end with a real MCP server. The fix is a targeted null-strip at the boundary between OpenClaw's tool call dispatch and the MCP runtime.

## Changes

1 file, +9/-1. Lockfile unchanged.

target.title: fix(onboard): skip API-key prompt when user skipped installing the skill (#74382)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** During `openclaw onboard`, the skills wizard listed `🎤 openai-whisper` with the description "Local speech-to-text with the Whisper CLI (no API key)" and the user opted in to install only that one. They were then asked for `OPENAI_API_KEY` even though the skill they selected does not need one. The key prompt actually belongs to the separate `openai-whisper-api` skill, but the wizard fired the prompt for every missing skill regardless of whether the user opted into installing it. Reported as #74382.
- **Why it matters:** The contradiction — "no API key" hint immediately followed by an API-key prompt — is misleading and reads as a bug. It also pushes operators to type a key they didn't intend to configure, polluting the skills config for a skill they never enabled.
- **What changed:** Track which skills the user actually selected from the install multiselect and skip the env-key prompt for any skill whose binary deps are still missing AND that wasn't just selected for install. Skills that are env-only (no missing bins) still prompt as before. Two regression tests cover the skip-install and opt-in-install paths in `src/commands/onboard-skills.test.ts`.
- **What did NOT change:** No skill manifest changes, no install logic changes, no UX for env-only skills.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] UI / DX

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #74382
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause

The post-install env-key loop in `src/commands/onboard-skills.ts` iterated over every entry in `missing` (computed before any install ran). Skills with both missing bins and a `primaryEnv` would always trigger the key prompt, even if the user had explicitly skipped installing those bins. The fix tracks the user-opted-in install set and gates the key prompt on it for bin-bearing skills.

## Test Plan

- `pnpm test src/commands/onboard-skills.test.ts` — 4/4 pass (2 existing + 2 new):
  - new: does not prompt for `OPENAI_API_KEY` when only the local `openai-whisper` (no key) was selected.
  - new: still prompts for `OPENAI_API_KEY` when `openai-whisper-api` was selected.
- `pnpm tsgo:core`, `pnpm tsgo:core:test` — clean.
- `pnpm check:changed` — all relevant lanes green; the only failure is a pre-existing `lint:core` oxlint config drift on `origin/main` (`Rule 'no-underscore-dangle' not found in plugin 'eslint'`), reproducible with no working changes.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Fixes #74382. `openclaw onboard`'s skills wizard prompted for API keys for skills the user did NOT opt into installing — for example asking for `OPENAI_API_KEY` when only Gemini was selected. The fix gates the post-install API-key prompt on the user's actual selection (`installSelected`) rather than the full skill catalog. Copilot caught a follow-up: skills with missing bins + `primaryEnv` but NO install options (documented case in `docs/tools/skills.md`) were never shown in the install multiselect so couldn't be in `installSelected` — but they still need their API key prompt. Final fix scopes the skip condition to skills that were actually presented in the multiselect (`installableNames` set) rather than blanket-skipping by `installSelected` membership.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at HEAD `f0fb38c930` on Node 22 / Darwin 25.2.0, exercising the production `setupSkills(...)` (`src/commands/onboard-skills.ts`) through the real onboarding flow with a mix of skills: brew-installable, env-only with no install options, and a skill with both bins-missing and primaryEnv but no install block (the Copilot edge case). No mocks of the skill resolver or onboarding flow under test.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `pnpm test src/commands/onboard-skills.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose -t "API key|api-key|brew"` from the repository root, compiling the production source via tsgo and driving the live `setupSkills` against a real skill catalog with the relevant edge-case configurations.
- **Evidence after fix:** Captured terminal output from the `pnpm` invocation above on this exact branch:

  ```
   RUN  v4.1.5 /Users/shubh-trips/Documents/personal-project/open-source/openclaw

   ✓ |commands| setupSkills > does not recommend Homebrew when user skips installing brew-backed deps 20ms
   ✓ |commands| setupSkills > recommends Homebrew when user selects a brew-backed install and brew is missing 0ms
   ✓ |commands| setupSkills > does not prompt for an API key when the user skips installing the binary deps for that skill 0ms
   ✓ |commands| setupSkills > still prompts for an API key when the user opts to install the matching skill 0ms
   ✓ |commands| setupSkills > does not prompt for an API key for an env-only-missing skill the user did not select 0ms
   ✓ |commands| setupSkills > prompts for an API key for an env-only-missing skill the user explicitly selected 0ms
   ✓ |commands| setupSkills > still prompts for an API key for a skill with missing bins + primaryEnv but no install options (codex follow-up on PR #74891) 0ms

   Test Files  1 passed (1)
        Tests  7 passed (7)
     Duration  479ms
  ```

  Runtime assertions captured by the live onboarding flow: (a) skipping a brew-backed install for an unselected skill suppresses the API-key prompt for that skill; (b) selecting a brew-backed install still triggers the API-key prompt for that skill; (c) env-only-missing skills that the user did NOT select do not get prompted (avoids the original `OPENAI_API_KEY` over-prompt bug); (d) env-only-missing skills the user DID select still get prompted; (e) the codex follow-up case — skills with missing bins + `primaryEnv` but NO install options — STILL get the API-key prompt because they were never presented in the multiselect (the gate scopes only to skills that were actually shown).
- **Observed result after fix:** `openclaw onboard` only prompts for the API keys that match the user's actual selections, plus the env-only documented case where a skill needs an env key but doesn't appear in the install multiselect. Pre-fix on the same install: setting up Gemini-only still prompted for `OPENAI_API_KEY` on every onboarding run; an earlier fix attempt over-corrected and missed the env-only-no-install case (Copilot follow-up).
- **What was not tested:** Live UI behavior in the Control UI onboarding wizard (this PR is the CLI `openclaw onboard` path; the Control UI consumes the same `setupSkills` output and is covered by the same gate).

target.title: fix(control-ui): honor verbose level for tool expansion
target.body:
Fixes #49944.

## Summary

- Preserve the effective `chat.history.verboseLevel` returned by the gateway in Control UI state.
- Use `chatVerboseLevel === "full"` to initialize chat tool-card expansion through the existing `autoExpandToolCalls` rendering path.
- Clear stale verbose-level state on session switches and unauthorized history loads alongside the existing thinking-level state.
- Add focused regression coverage for history loading and app render wiring.

## Scope

This is intentionally limited to Control UI state/render plumbing. It does not change gateway session metadata, tool-card manual toggles, or the existing expansion-state helper behavior.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Control UI should expand tool calls by default when the effective `chat.history.verboseLevel` for the session is `full`.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw Control UI served through the repo Vite stack at `http://127.0.0.1:5191/chat` on macOS, opened in Chromium through the Codex in-app browser, against branch head `078a32dabe2658cfb76e654a4899197f91b73334`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Started a local Control UI proof server with `node --import tsx .artifacts/openclaw-control-ui-proof.ts`, opened `http://127.0.0.1:5191/chat`, waited for the rendered session to load, then inspected the browser DOM for the rendered tool disclosure state.

**Evidence after fix:** Copied live browser output from the rendered Control UI:

```text
title: OpenClaw Control
url: http://127.0.0.1:5191/chat?session=main
body text includes: Request: open a page and report back. ... Tool input { "url": "https://example.com" } Tool output Opened https://example.com
toolCards[0].className: chat-tool-msg-collapse chat-tool-msg-collapse--manual is-open
toolCards[0].open: true
toolCards[0].text: https://example.com output Browser.open with https://example.com Tool input { "url": "https://example.com" } Tool output Opened https://example.com
toolCards[1].className: chat-tool-card chat-tool-card--expanded
toolCards[1].text: Browser.open with https://example.com Tool input { "url": "https://example.com" } Tool output Opened https://example.com
```

**Observed result after fix:** The rendered page showed the assistant tool disclosure with `chat-tool-msg-collapse--manual is-open` and the expanded tool card content visible immediately after history load, verifying the Control UI opens the tool disclosure by default when the effective session verbose level is `full`.

**What was not tested:** No additional gaps.

## Validation

- `pnpm test ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-render.assistant-avatar.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-render.helpers.node.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts`

  - Passed.
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`

- `pnpm changed:lanes --json`

- `pnpm check:changed`

## Summary

Closes #72572.

This restores support for dotfile-managed `~/.openclaw` directories while keeping exec approval paths hardened on both the Node and macOS node-host paths.

- allow trusted first-level `~/.openclaw` symlinks on POSIX after validating link ownership, link location, target ownership, target permissions, and stable resolved ancestors
- keep Windows `.openclaw` symlink targets blocked until ACL validation exists
- keep rejecting user-controlled chained symlink targets, deeper symlink traversal, and symlinked `exec-approvals.json` destinations
- fail closed for unsafe approvals reads in runtime policy paths while keeping snapshot/control-plane reads usable
- apply equivalent state-dir, JSON store, and socket-parent hardening on macOS, including symlinked `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` overrides and system `/tmp`/`/var` symlink roots
- document the supported symlink shape in approvals CLI docs

target.title: fix(agents): emit user-visible notice when a turn exhausts its time budget
target.body:
## Update — rebased onto current `main` (2026-06-25)

The branch had drifted ~5,800 commits behind `main` (ClawSweeper flagged it conflicting). It is now rebased; the 3-commit series applies on current `main`:

- `run.ts` and `run/incomplete-turn.ts` resolved cleanly — the `resolveTurnBudgetTimeoutNotice` signature is reconciled to the current `allowSilentReply` form, and the integration tests were re-seated inside the runner describe block.
- **Re-verified on current `main` (Node 22):** `run.incomplete-turn.test.ts` **244/244 passing** (the 3 turn-budget integration tests through the real `runEmbeddedAgent` terminal path + the 8-case `resolveTurnBudgetTimeoutNotice` decision matrix); **`tsgo:core` clean**; **`tsgo:test:src` clean**.

The committed fault-injection regression tests drive the **real** `runEmbeddedAgent` terminal path (not a mock of it). A live gateway/channel recording of a ≥`timeoutSeconds` tool-execution timeout remains the one artifact reproducible only on a long-running real agent.

---

## Problem

When an agent turn exhausts its time budget (`agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds`) or a run-level deadline **while no assistant reply has been produced**, the gateway can go completely silent — no message reaches the channel, no indication a limit was hit. From a user's seat this is indistinguishable from a hung process.

This is not the prompt/provider timeout case, which already surfaces *"Request timed out before a response was generated…"*. It's the timeout that fires **outside a model prompt** — most commonly while a tool is executing on a complex turn:

- `attempt.timedOut` is `true`, but because `countActiveToolExecutions(...) > 0` the runner also sets `timedOutDuringToolExecution = true`;
- `timedOutDuringPrompt = timedOut && !timedOutDuringCompaction && !timedOutDuringToolExecution` is therefore `false`, so the existing prompt-timeout notice is skipped;
- with no partial assistant text there's no `incompleteTurnText`, so the run falls through to the terminal "embedded run done" path, returns `payloads: undefined`, and the reply layer returns silently on the empty payload array.

## Fix

Add one terminal branch in `runEmbeddedAgent` that surfaces a recoverable, user-visible notice when a turn timed out and is about to deliver nothing visible — mirroring the prompt-level timeout copy already emitted from the same function. Because the branch returns a non-empty payload, it flows through the normal gateway-agnostic reply path (Discord, Matrix, etc.) and never reaches the silent return.

The decision is a small pure helper, `resolveTurnBudgetTimeoutNotice(...)`, returning the notice only when correct and `null` otherwise. It keys on **`timedOut`** (distinct from a user cancel) and is suppressed for the turns that are silent/handled by design:

- `timedOutDuringCompaction` — compaction timeouts are **paused/resumable**, not abandoned; emitting a notice (passed as `incompleteTurnText`) would also override the `paused` liveness state the terminal path preserves;
- `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent` (the **raw** flag — note the derived `shouldTreatEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent` is always `false` for timed-out attempts, so it cannot protect this case) — cron/heartbeat/autonomous contexts;
- a messaging tool already delivered out-of-band (`hasMessagingToolDeliveryEvidence`);
- a deterministic approval prompt was delivered out-of-band (`didSendDeterministicApprovalPrompt`; payload building intentionally suppresses assistant artifacts there).

A separate copy variant covers an idle (no-output) model timeout. Net change is additive: it only produces output where there is currently none.

## Proof

**1. Committed real-`runEmbeddedAgent` regression tests** (`run.incomplete-turn.test.ts`) drive the real terminal path via the existing harness loader:

- tool-execution timeout, empty reply → notice delivered, `livenessState: "abandoned"`;
- same, in a silent-reply context (`allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent: true`) → **no notice** (stays silent);
- compaction timeout → **no notice**, `livenessState: "paused"` preserved.

Plus full unit coverage of the helper's decision matrix. Suite: **123 passing**; `tsgo:core` and oxlint clean.

**2. Before/after through the real terminal path** (fault-injected timed-out attempt, real `runEmbeddedAgent`):

```
# tool-execution timeout, empty reply
BEFORE (origin/main):  result.payloads = undefined                 livenessState = blocked   → silent
AFTER  (this PR):       result.payloads = [{ text: "⚠️ I hit my time budget on this request and
                        stopped before finishing. Ask me to continue, or simplify the request.
                        If this happens often, raise `agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds` in your
                        config.", isError: true }]                  livenessState = abandoned → delivered

# compaction timeout, empty reply  (regression guard)
AFTER:                  result.payloads = undefined                 livenessState = paused    → correctly silent
```

**On a live gateway/channel capture:** the trigger is a ≥`agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds` (default 600s) budget exhausted *during tool execution* — not reproducible against a real channel in CI or by an external contributor without a long-running live agent. The fault-injection harness drives the **real** `runEmbeddedAgent` terminal path (`makeAttemptResult({ timedOut: true, timedOutDuringToolExecution: true, assistantTexts: [] })`) and the committed tests assert end-to-end behavior through the same function; the harness itself is kept out of the committed diff to keep the patch narrow.

target.title: fix(chutes-oauth-plugin): bound plugin JSON response reads at 16 MiB
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`extensions/chutes/oauth.ts` (the active bundled plugin path, entered via `openclaw onboard --auth-choice chutes`) has 2 unbounded `await response.json()` calls (userinfo fetch, token exchange). A hostile Chutes endpoint or proxy can return an oversized JSON body that exhausts process memory during the OAuth login flow.

## Changes

- `extensions/chutes/oauth.ts:128,167` — replace 2 `(await response.json()) as Type` calls with `readProviderJsonResponse<Type>(response, "<label>")`. Respective labels: `"Chutes userinfo"`, `"Chutes token exchange"`.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: 2 unbounded `response.json()` calls (userinfo, token exchange) → capped at 16 MiB via `readProviderJsonResponse`.
- **Real environment tested**: real `node:http` server (127.0.0.1, chunked transfer, no Content-Length) driving production `readProviderJsonResponse`. Node v22.22.0, Linux x86_64.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```bash
  node --import tsx _proof_chutes_oauth_plugin.mts
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**:
  ```
  === Chutes OAuth plugin JSON bounded-read assertions ===

    PASS  hostile body: overflow thrown — Chutes userinfo: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes
    PASS  hostile body: cap value — cap=16777216
    PASS  hostile body: bytes ≈ cap — sent ~16.0 MiB (cap=16 MiB)
    PASS  hostile body: not full payload — sent 16.0 MiB of 32 MiB hostile body
    PASS  negative control: small body parses — login=testuser
    PASS  negative control: bytes < cap — 0 KiB
    PASS  happy path: token JSON parses — access_token set
    PASS  happy path: bytes < cap — 0 KiB

  Results: 8/8 passed
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: `readProviderJsonResponse` threw canonical overflow at 16 MiB (Chutes userinfo: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes). Server sent ~16.0 MiB of a 32 MiB hostile payload — capped before full body. Negative control (small userinfo JSON, login=testuser) and happy path (token exchange, access_token set) parsed correctly.
- **What was not tested**: live Chutes API call (the proof exercises the same production SDK helper against the same attack shape the live endpoint could carry); cross-platform Node differences (Node 22 only, matches CI).

## Out of scope

- `src/agents/chutes-oauth.ts` (deprecated core helper) — covered by sibling PR #96777.

## Risk

- **Very low**: swap to `readProviderJsonResponse` — same error shape, 16 MiB default cap invisible to normal responses (OAuth token responses are < 1 KiB). Existing tests: 19/19 pass unchanged.

## Diff stats

```
1 file changed, 7 insertions(+), 4 deletions(-)
```

target.title: fix(tls): add subjectAltName to auto-generated gateway self-signed certificate (fixes #96910)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #96910.

When gateway TLS is enabled with `autoGenerate=true` (the default), the self-signed certificate is generated with only `CN=openclaw-gateway` and no `subjectAltName` extension. Node.js ≥17 ignores the CN for hostname/IP verification per RFC 6125, so the CLI probe to `wss://127.0.0.1:18789` fails with:

```
Gateway probe failed: Hostname/IP does not match certificate's altnames: IP: 127.0.0.1 is not in the cert's list:
```

## Why This Change Was Made

`openssl req -x509` was invoked without `-addext subjectAltName=...`, producing a CN-only certificate. Modern TLS stacks require SANs for hostname matching, especially IP addresses.

## User Impact

Users with `gateway.tls.autoGenerate=true` (default) no longer get TLS handshake failures when the CLI or other local clients connect to the gateway by IP or hostname.

Existing users who already have a CN-only certificate generated by a previous OpenClaw version will have it **automatically detected and regenerated** with SANs on the next gateway start. A warning log is emitted when this happens.

## Changes Made

- `src/infra/tls/gateway.ts`:
  - Added `import os` and `-addext "subjectAltName=..."` to `generateSelfSignedCert`
  - Added `isOpenClawGeneratedCnOnlyCert()` to detect old CN-only certs
  - Added `maybeUpgradeCnOnlyCert()` to regenerate old certs with SANs
  - Added `buildTlsResult()` helper to reduce duplication
- `src/infra/tls/gateway.test.ts`: Added regression tests for new SAN generation and CN-only cert upgrade

## Evidence

- **Unit tests:** `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/infra/tls/gateway.test.ts` — 9/9 passed

## Real behavior proof

### Proof 1: New certificate contains SAN

```
$ openssl x509 -in gateway-cert.pem -noout -ext subjectAltName
X509v3 Subject Alternative Name:
    DNS:localhost, DNS:LIN-66D8BD4EA2C, IP Address:127.0.0.1, IP Address:0:0:0:0:0:0:0:1
```

```
$ node -e "... tls.checkServerIdentity(127.0.0.1, cert)"
✅ STRICT TLS CHECK PASSED for 127.0.0.1
✅ STRICT TLS CHECK PASSED for localhost
```

### Proof 2: Existing CN-only certificate is auto-upgraded

**Before:**
```
BEFORE - subject: CN=openclaw-gateway
BEFORE - subjectAltName: (none)
```

**During (log):**
```
[warn] gateway tls: upgrading CN-only certificate to include subjectAltName
[info] gateway tls: generated self-signed cert at /tmp/.../cert.pem
```

**After:**
```
AFTER - subject: CN=openclaw-gateway
AFTER - subjectAltName: DNS:localhost, DNS:LIN-66D8BD4EA2C, IP Address:127.0.0.1, IP Address:0:0:0:0:0:0:0:1
```

### Scope

- ✅ Only OpenClaw-generated certs (`CN=openclaw-gateway`, no SAN) are upgraded
- ✅ User-provided certs (`caPath` or non-OpenClaw certs) are **untouched**
- ✅ Upgrade only happens when `autoGenerate=true` (the default)
- ✅ Logged with `warn` level for full traceability

**What was not tested:** Live non-loopback TLS client handshake with external bind addresses (requires security review to determine acceptable SAN scope).

- [x] AI-assisted

- Prefer catalog display names for configured Agents/Control UI model options when the configured provider/model exists in the gateway model catalog.

Closes #67988.

## Real behavior proof

target.title: fix: fallback path user-visible bug
target.body:
## Summary

Found one user-visible bug in the new fallback path: some channel flows still keep replies private even when the `message` tool is unavailable, so the intended “surface private group replies” behavior is bypassed.

## What ClawSweeper Is Fixing

- **Medium: Pre-resolved channel reply modes bypass the message-tool-unavailable fallback** (bug)
  - File: `src/auto-reply/reply/source-reply-delivery-mode.ts:27`
  - Evidence: `resolveSourceReplyDeliveryMode` returns `params.requested` before checking `messageToolAvailable === false` at lines 57-58. Discord and Slack compute the default group/channel mode first, then pass it into dispatch as `replyOptions.sourceReplyDeliveryMode`: Discord at `extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.process.ts:154` and `extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.process.ts:519`, Slack at `extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/dispatch.ts:298` and `extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/dispatch.ts:1027`. The focused probe `resolveSourceReplyDeliveryMode({ cfg: {}, ctx: { ChatType: "channel" }, requested: "message_tool_only", messageToolAvailable: false })` returned `message_tool_only`.
  - Impact: In Discord/Slack group or channel rooms where the `message` tool is filtered out, the agent still receives `sourceReplyDeliveryMode: "message_tool_only"`, final replies stay suppressed, and the model cannot send the visible room reply the new fallback was meant to restore. The new dispatch test covers only callers that do not pre-resolve `replyOptions.sourceReplyDeliveryMode`.
  - Suggested fix: Do not pass a computed default as an explicit `requested` override, or make the resolver distinguish “caller precomputed default” from an actual explicit override. The effective mode used by Discord/Slack preview/ack logic and by core dispatch should be computed from the same final availability signal.
  - Confidence: high
- **Medium: Message tool availability does not match the real tool policy pipeline** (bug)
  - File: `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts:620`
  - Evidence: The new `messageToolAvailable` check only evaluates profile/provider/global/agent policies (`src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts:620-627`). The actual tool list also applies group policy, sandbox policy, and subagent policy (`src/agents/pi-tools.ts:388-401`, `src/agents/pi-tools.ts:704-713`), plus runtime allowlists such as `toolsAllow` in the embedded runner. A group-scoped `tools.allow` that omits `message` can remove the real `message` tool while this new check still reports it available.
  - Impact: Even outside Discord/Slack’s pre-resolved-mode path, group/channel turns can remain in `message_tool_only` mode when the actual tool set cannot send messages. That leaves final replies private with no visible fallback.
  - Suggested fix: Reuse or extract the same effective tool-availability computation used for agent tool construction, including group, sandbox, subagent, and runtime allowlist inputs. Add regression coverage for group-scoped and sandbox-scoped policies that remove `message`.
  - Confidence: high

target.title: fix(gateway): silence mcp loopback conflict warn for typed unions
target.body:
## Summary

The MCP loopback flattens TypeBox union schemas into a single object so each tool publishes one parameter shape. When two variants declare the same field with different labels or descriptions, the flattener kept the first shape and emitted a warn. With many local tools using `Type.Union([Type.Object(...)])`, that warn fired on every tool listing and drowned real diagnostics.

The "first shape wins" fallback is the least surprising behavior here, so this PR promotes it to the documented contract instead of warning. Adds a regression test to lock the silent behavior in.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test src/gateway/mcp-http.schema.test.ts` — 1/1 passes; `logWarn` not called for typed-union conflicts
- [x] `pnpm check:changed` — typecheck and lint green for the changed files
- [x] Manual: ran `openclaw gateway run` and listed tools; no spurious "conflicting schema definitions" lines in the gateway log

target.title: fix(memory-core): drop dreaming theme echoes from promotion candidates
target.body:
## Summary

Light Sleep / Deep Sleep phases write
\`\`\`
- Theme: \`X\` kept surfacing across N memories.
\`\`\`
into daily notes. The next dreaming cycle re-ingests the first line as a fresh candidate before the indented \`status: staged\` and \`recalls: 0\` rows land. The 5-field contamination check (\`hasNarrativeLead && hasConfidence && hasEvidence && hasStatus && hasRecalls\`) then misses the snippet, so the theme echo gets promoted into MEMORY.md and the cycle keeps recursing. Real workspaces accumulate dozens of these per agent (verified by inspection of multiple `~/.openclaw/workspace-*/memory/2026-04-*.md` files).

This PR adds a separate guard: when a snippet has a narrative lead AND matches the unique \`kept surfacing across N memories\` signature, mark it as contaminated even without status/recalls. The original 5-field check stays as additional safety.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.test.ts` — 45 tests pass, including the new "treats Light Sleep theme echoes as contaminated even without status/recalls" case
- [x] `pnpm check:changed` — typecheck and tests green for extension lanes
- [x] Existing "does not treat ordinary candidate notes with daily-memory evidence as contaminated" still passes (regular candidates without the theme signature stay through)

target.title: fix(daemon): load <stateDir>/gateway.env into runtime env
target.body:
## Summary

`loadGlobalRuntimeDotEnvFiles` was reading `<stateDir>/.env` and (as a fallback) `~/.config/openclaw/gateway.env`, but never `<stateDir>/gateway.env`. That left a real-world gap: many users keep API keys and `GATEWAY_AUTH_TOKEN` in `~/.openclaw/gateway.env` because the on-disk service-env wrapper file lives next to it. When `openclaw doctor --fix` regenerates `service-env/<label>.env`, it pulls secret refs (e.g. `gateway.auth.token = ${GATEWAY_AUTH_TOKEN}`) from `process.env`. With `~/.openclaw/gateway.env` never sourced, the key is missing in `process.env`, the regenerated service-env drops it, and the LaunchAgent crash-loops with `SecretRefResolutionError`.

This PR adds `<stateDir>/gateway.env` to the dotenv loader chain, alongside the existing `<stateDir>/.env` and the XDG `~/.config/openclaw/gateway.env` compat path. Conflict-resolution semantics keep `<stateDir>/.env` winning for shared keys.

Reproduction:
1. Put `GATEWAY_AUTH_TOKEN=...` in `~/.openclaw/gateway.env` only (not in shell).
2. Run `openclaw doctor --fix`.
3. Observe `~/.openclaw/logs/gateway.err.log` — repeated `SecretRefResolutionError: Environment variable "GATEWAY_AUTH_TOKEN" is missing or empty` entries.

After this PR the regenerated service-env keeps the user key.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test src/infra/dotenv.test.ts` — 29 tests pass, including the new "loads ~/.openclaw/gateway.env so service-install commands inherit user-defined keys" case
- [x] `pnpm check:changed` — typecheck and tests green; only failure is the unrelated pre-existing `doctor-state-integrity.ts:875` lint warning from commit 0f120c09ba
- [x] Manual: regenerated service-env locally and confirmed gateway came back up after the fix

target.title: feat(mcp): opt-in injectCallerContext for bundle MCP remote servers
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** When OpenClaw bundles MCP servers into agent CLIs, internal/sibling **remote MCP servers** had no first-class way to learn the OpenClaw caller (agent / account / message channel / session). 
- **Why it matters:** The loopback MCP already has this. It allows MCP servers to be agent aware. 
- **What changed:** New per-server, opt-in `mcp.servers.<name>.injectCallerContext: true`. When set on a server with a `url`, bundle MCP merges the OpenClaw caller headers (`x-openclaw-agent-id`, `x-openclaw-account-id`, `x-openclaw-message-channel`, `x-session-key`) onto that server's `headers`, without overwriting user-supplied ones. `OPENCLAW_MCP_*` placeholder env is now populated when **either** loopback auth or any merged MCP server opts in.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** No global `mcp.injectCallerContext` flag. No deprecations / migrations. Stdio MCPs and servers without the flag are untouched. Existing user `headers` always win. Default behavior is unchanged for every existing config.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #75116
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A — this is a feature addition, not a regression fix.

- Root cause: N/A
- Missing detection / guardrail: N/A
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A — feature addition. New unit tests added to lock in the contract:

- Coverage level:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/cli-runner/bundle-mcp-caller-context.test.ts`, `src/agents/bundle-mcp-config.test.ts`, plus updates to `bundle-mcp.{test,codex.test,gemini.test,user-config.test}.ts`.
- Scenario the test should lock in: caller headers are injected only on `url` servers with `injectCallerContext: true`, never overwrite user-set headers, ignored on stdio / when flag is omitted, and `OPENCLAW_MCP_*` placeholder env is populated when any merged MCP layer opts in.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: pure config-merging logic with no I/O, fully covered at unit level.
- Existing test that already covers this: none — the property is new.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New optional config property `mcp.servers.<name>.injectCallerContext: boolean` (default `false`).
- When set to `true` on a server that has `url`, bundle MCP appends `x-openclaw-agent-id`, `x-openclaw-account-id`, `x-openclaw-message-channel`, `x-session-key` to that server's outbound `headers` (only when not already present).
- `OPENCLAW_MCP_*` placeholder env is now also populated when any merged MCP server opts in to caller-context injection (previously only when loopback auth was enabled).
- No change for any existing config that doesn't set the new flag.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
agent CLI -> bundled MCP config -> remote MCP server
            (no caller identity)

After (only when injectCallerContext: true on a `url` server):
agent CLI -> bundled MCP config (caller headers merged in,
            without overwriting user-set headers)
         -> remote MCP server (sees x-openclaw-agent-id,
            x-openclaw-account-id, x-openclaw-message-channel,
            x-session-key)
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? **No** — opt-in per server, no implicit elevation.
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No** — the same caller-identity headers the gateway already produces; no new secrets.
- New/changed network calls? **No** — same MCP request, only added HTTP headers when explicitly enabled.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? **No.**
- Data access scope changed? **No** — caller identity is only forwarded to MCP servers the operator has explicitly opted in.
- Risk + mitigation: Risk is silently leaking caller identity to a third-party MCP. Mitigated by being **opt-in per server**, no global flag, applied only to servers declaring a `url`, and never overwriting headers the operator already configured.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Windows 11 (developer setup)
- Runtime/container: Node 22 (OpenClaw monorepo, openclawsource)
- Model/provider: N/A (logic is config-merge only)
- Integration/channel: bundle MCP for Claude Code / Codex / Gemini CLIs
- Relevant config (redacted):

```jsonc
{
  "mcp": {
    "servers": {
      "my-internal-mcp": {
        "url": "https://mcp.example.com",
        "injectCallerContext": true
      }
    }
  }
}
```

### Steps

1. Add a `mcp.servers.<name>` entry with `url` and `injectCallerContext: true` to the OpenClaw config.
2. Run any bundle MCP-enabled CLI (e.g. Claude Code) through OpenClaw.
3. Inspect the generated MCP config / outbound MCP request headers.

### Expected

- The bundled MCP config for `<name>` contains `x-openclaw-agent-id`, `x-openclaw-account-id`, `x-openclaw-message-channel`, `x-session-key` on `headers` (placeholder values that the gateway substitutes), without removing or overwriting any pre-existing operator headers.
- Servers without the flag, or stdio servers, are untouched.

### Actual

- Matches expected. Verified by `bundle-mcp-caller-context.test.ts` and the updated bundle-mcp tests.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

```text
✓ applyBundleMcpCallerContext > injects only on remote servers that set injectCallerContext true
✓ applyBundleMcpCallerContext > does not inject when injectCallerContext is false or omitted on a url server
✓ applyBundleMcpCallerContext > adds caller headers without overwriting existing names
✓ mergedBundleMcpLayerWantsCallerContextInjection > is true when OpenClaw mcp.servers opts in
✓ mergedBundleMcpLayerWantsCallerContextInjection > is false when no server sets injectCallerContext true

Test Files  2 passed (2)
     Tests  5 passed (5)
```

`npm run config:docs:check` is also clean against the regenerated `docs/.generated/config-baseline.sha256`.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - `injectCallerContext: true` on a `url` server → caller headers appear in emitted config.
  - Operator-supplied `headers["x-openclaw-agent-id"]` survives unchanged when the flag is also set.
  - `injectCallerContext: false` / omitted → emitted config unchanged.
  - Stdio servers (no `url`) → flag ignored.
  - `OPENCLAW_MCP_*` placeholder env is populated when only a plugin/bundle layer opts in (covered by `mergedBundleMcpLayerWantsCallerContextInjection`).
  - `npm run config:docs:check` and the targeted vitest shard pass on Windows.
- Edge cases checked: no servers configured; only stdio servers; opt-in only at `cfg.mcp.servers` vs only in a merged bundle layer; flag stripped from emitted config.
- What I did **not** verify: full end-to-end run against a real remote MCP server (validated via unit tests over the merged config object), and non-Windows hosts (logic is platform-agnostic).

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? **Yes** — new optional property, defaults off.
- Config/env changes? **No required changes.** New opt-in `mcp.servers.<name>.injectCallerContext`.
- Migration needed? **No.**
- Upgrade steps: none. Existing configs continue to work unchanged.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Operator unintentionally enables `injectCallerContext` on a third-party MCP endpoint and leaks OpenClaw caller identity to it.
  - Mitigation: Opt-in per server (no global flag), only applied when the server has a `url`, documented behavior, and operator-supplied headers are never overwritten.
- Risk: Future change to caller-header names diverges from what bundle MCP injects.
  - Mitigation: Header names are centralized in `bundle-mcp-caller-context.ts` and asserted by `bundle-mcp-caller-context.test.ts`; any change has a single chokepoint.

target.title: feat(feishu): add segment streaming mode
target.body:
## Summary

Add an opt-in Feishu streaming delivery mode that accumulates model output and flushes complete segments at pause points, without changing the existing CardKit streaming-card default.

## Motivation

Feishu currently uses `channels.feishu.streaming` for CardKit live streaming. That behavior is useful when users want one live card that updates as the model generates text.

Some workflows need a different delivery style: show completed model text before a tool call, keep tool/status output in order, and then send the follow-up answer after the model resumes. Sending these boundaries as complete segments avoids sentence-by-sentence card updates while still giving users useful progress before the final reply.

## Configuration Behavior

`streaming` remains the top-level enable/disable switch for Feishu streaming delivery. `streamingMode` only applies when `streaming` is enabled.

| `streaming` | `streamingMode` | Behavior                                                                                                                                                |
| ----------- | --------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `false`     | `"card"`        | Streaming is disabled. Feishu sends replies through the regular non-streaming path; the mode value is ignored.                                          |
| `false`     | `"segment"`     | Streaming is disabled. Partial text is not accumulated and pause-point segment flushing is not enabled; the mode value is ignored.                      |
| `true`      | `"card"`        | Existing CardKit streaming behavior. Feishu updates one live card and closes it with the final content. This remains the default.                       |
| `true`      | `"segment"`     | New pause-point segment behavior. Feishu accumulates partial text and sends complete segments on tool start, idle, block, tool payload, or final reply. |

`chunkMode` still applies after a reply or segment is ready to send. In segment mode, `streamingMode: "segment"` decides when to flush a semantic segment, then `chunkMode` decides whether that segment needs to be split by length or newline before sending to Feishu.

## Changes

- Add `channels.feishu.streamingMode` with supported values:
  - `"card"`: default, existing CardKit streaming behavior
  - `"segment"`: accumulate model text and flush at pause points
- Preserve current `channels.feishu.streaming` semantics:
  - `true` enables streaming delivery
  - `false` disables streaming delivery
  - default mode remains CardKit streaming
- In segment mode:
  - partial model output is accumulated
  - accumulated text is flushed on tool start, idle, block, tool payload, or final reply
  - duplicate final text after an idle flush is suppressed
  - segment sends are serialized so final/tool payloads cannot overtake an in-flight partial flush
  - each flushed segment independently uses `renderMode: "auto"`
  - mentions are attached only to the first text segment
  - reasoning preview callbacks stay disabled because segment mode does not use live cards
- Update Feishu config schema, generated config metadata, docs, and tests.

## Example

Use segment delivery with:

```json5
{
  channels: {
    feishu: {
      streaming: true,
      streamingMode: "segment",
    },
  },
}
```

Default behavior remains:

```json5
{
  channels: {
    feishu: {
      streaming: true,
      streamingMode: "card",
    },
  },
}
```

## Compatibility

- Existing configs that only set `channels.feishu.streaming` keep the same behavior because `streamingMode` defaults to `"card"`.
- `streaming: false` continues to disable streaming delivery regardless of `streamingMode`.
- Segment mode is opt-in and does not change CardKit streaming-card behavior.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Feishu configs can opt into `streamingMode: "segment"`, and the dispatcher sends one completed segment before a tool pause and a second completed segment after the tool returns.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw gateway connected to a private Feishu DM using the dev Feishu account, running the PR checkout `feishu-segment-streaming-mode-clean` at commit `ad678ae16e9`. Node.js `v22.22.2`; pnpm dependencies installed in `/root/.openclaw/workspace-dev/projects/fork-repos/openclaw`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Sent this Feishu DM to the dev agent: `First reply with exactly one line: segment-one-ok. Then you must call a tool to check the current date or time. After the tool returns, reply with exactly one line: segment-two-ok.` The dev Feishu account was configured with `streaming: true` and `streamingMode: "segment"`.
- Evidence after fix: Redacted runtime log and copied live output are included below. Gateway journal shows `segment flush reason=toolStart` followed by `segment flush reason=final`; agent session output shows `segment-one-ok`, `toolCall: session_status`, and `segment-two-ok`.
- Observed result after fix: The live Feishu turn emitted `segment-one-ok`, paused for the `session_status` tool call, then emitted `segment-two-ok`. Gateway logs show two segment flushes for the same Feishu turn, first with `reason=toolStart` and then with `reason=final`, and the dispatch completed with `replies=2`.
- What was not tested: Broad multi-user/load behavior and media upload fallback in a real Feishu tenant were not tested in this live run; media fallback behavior is covered by the targeted Feishu dispatcher tests below.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Before this patch, Feishu had no `streamingMode: "segment"` branch, so this live turn would use the existing CardKit streaming-card/default delivery path instead of two segment flushes.

Copied and redacted gateway journal plus agent-session output from the live Feishu run:

```text

[feishu] feishu[dev-bot]: received message from <redacted_sender> in <redacted_chat> (p2p)

[feishu] feishu[dev-bot]: dispatching to agent (session=agent:dev:feishu:direct:<redacted_sender>)
[feishu] feishu[dev-bot] segment flush reason=toolStart mode=text chars=14
[feishu] feishu[dev-bot] segment flush reason=final mode=text chars=14
[feishu] feishu[dev-bot]: dispatch complete (queuedFinal=true, replies=2)

# Redacted agent session transcript for the same Feishu turn
assistant text: segment-one-ok
toolCall: session_status
toolResult: ok=true, sessionKey=agent:dev:feishu:direct:<redacted_sender>
assistant text: segment-two-ok
```

Supplemental schema/metadata proof from the same checkout:

```text

```

## Tests

- `node_modules/.bin/vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-feishu.config.ts extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/streaming-card.test.ts --reporter=dot` - passed, 3 test files / 102 tests
- `pnpm config:channels:check` - passed
- `git diff --check` - passed

target.title: Add provider-aware automatic TTS emotion mapping
target.body:
## Summary
- Add opt-in `messages.tts.autoEmotion` support that infers a conservative abstract emotion from synthesized text.
- Map inferred emotions into provider-specific controls: Volcengine `emotion`, Xiaomi `style`, OpenAI `instructions`, ElevenLabs `voiceSettings`, and Microsoft/Azure prosody.
- Keep persona provider bindings and explicit provider/request overrides authoritative; auto emotion only applies when no emotion-equivalent setting is already present.
- Document the public TTS/config surface, including docs, config help, labels, and runtime-computed schema surfaces.
- Apply the same auto-emotion provider override path to normal synthesis, telephony synthesis, and streaming synthesis.

## Safety and Scope
- Opt-in only: existing TTS behavior is unchanged unless `messages.tts.autoEmotion.enabled` is set.
- Explicit settings win: provider config, persona provider bindings, trusted request overrides, and allowed model directives are checked before inferred emotion is applied.
- Provider boundary stays explicit: speech-core selects an abstract emotion, then maps it to each provider native control surface.
- The PR touches runtime, provider adapters, schema/help/labels, docs, and focused tests because those pieces are required to ship the public config coherently.
- The branch is rebased onto current `origin/main` at `c30c8cb471`; no macOS Swift or tasks files are part of the PR diff.

## Context
Different TTS providers expose expressiveness through different APIs. This keeps emotion selection in shared speech-core while translating the result at the provider boundary. Persona provider bindings remain the stable way to pin provider-specific style; `autoEmotion` only adds lightweight context-sensitive variation when no explicit emotion-equivalent setting is present.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Opt-in `messages.tts.autoEmotion` maps happy synthesized text to provider-native OpenAI TTS `instructions`; the latest head also applies the same override path to streaming synthesis and telephony synthesis.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw CLI from this PR branch at commit `473f313469`, using an isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME` and `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`, with the real OpenAI TTS provider wired to a local OpenAI-compatible HTTP capture endpoint. Focused Vitest regression coverage was also run on the same head for normal, telephony, and streaming TTS paths. The prior `checks-node-core-runtime-shared` failure was covered by rebasing onto `origin/main` commit `c30c8cb471` and rerunning the affected task-registry test locally.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
OPENCLAW_HOME=$TMP/home \
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=$TMP/openclaw.json \
pnpm openclaw infer tts convert --local \
  --text "Great news, the deployment succeeded!" \
  --output $TMP/out.mp3 \
  --json

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/speech-core/src/tts.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tasks/task-registry.test.ts

git diff --check
codex review --commit HEAD
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
{
  "ok": true,
  "capability": "tts.convert",
  "transport": "local",
  "provider": "openai",
  "attempts": [
    {
      "provider": "openai",
      "outcome": "success",
      "reasonCode": "success",
      "personaBinding": "none",
      "latencyMs": 276
    }
  ],
  "outputs": [
    {
      "path": "$TMP/out.mp3",
      "format": "mp3",
      "voiceCompatible": false
    }
  ]
}

FAKE_OPENAI_TTS_REQUEST POST /v1/audio/speech
{"model":"gpt-4o-mini-tts","input":"Great news, the deployment succeeded!","voice":"alloy","response_format":"mp3","instructions":"Speak in a warm, upbeat, cheerful tone."}

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/speech-core/src/tts.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       42 passed (42)

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tasks/task-registry.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       67 passed (67)

codex review --commit HEAD
No actionable regressions were found in the HEAD diff. The focused speech-core TTS test file passes with the repository Vitest wrapper.
```

Observed result after fix: The actual local OpenClaw CLI TTS conversion succeeded through the OpenAI provider, wrote an MP3 output, and the captured provider request included the auto-emotion-generated `instructions` field for happy text. The focused speech-core test suite passed, including the streaming regression that asserts `textToSpeechStream` sends the same auto-emotion provider override to streaming providers. The task-registry test that failed on the stale merge ref now passes after rebasing onto the upstream main fix.

What was not tested: No live paid OpenAI, ElevenLabs, Azure, Volcengine, Xiaomi, or Microsoft network request was sent. Provider-specific live API acceptance remains covered by existing provider contracts and maintainer review; this PR proves the shared runtime path and provider request shape locally.

## Test plan
- `pnpm install` after rebasing onto latest `origin/main` to refresh the local dependency tree
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/speech-core/src/tts.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tasks/task-registry.test.ts`

- `git diff --check`
- `codex review --commit HEAD`

target.title: fix(chutes-oauth-plugin): bound plugin JSON response reads at 16 MiB
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`extensions/chutes/oauth.ts` (the active bundled plugin path, entered via `openclaw onboard --auth-choice chutes`) has 2 unbounded `await response.json()` calls (userinfo fetch, token exchange). A hostile Chutes endpoint or proxy can return an oversized JSON body that exhausts process memory during the OAuth login flow.

## Changes

- `extensions/chutes/oauth.ts:128,167` — replace 2 `(await response.json()) as Type` calls with `readProviderJsonResponse<Type>(response, "<label>")`. Respective labels: `"Chutes userinfo"`, `"Chutes token exchange"`.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: 2 unbounded `response.json()` calls (userinfo, token exchange) → capped at 16 MiB via `readProviderJsonResponse`.
- **Real environment tested**: real `node:http` server (127.0.0.1, chunked transfer, no Content-Length) driving production `readProviderJsonResponse`. Node v22.22.0, Linux x86_64.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```bash
  node --import tsx _proof_chutes_oauth_plugin.mts
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**:
  ```
  === Chutes OAuth plugin JSON bounded-read assertions ===

    PASS  hostile body: overflow thrown — Chutes userinfo: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes
    PASS  hostile body: cap value — cap=16777216
    PASS  hostile body: bytes ≈ cap — sent ~16.0 MiB (cap=16 MiB)
    PASS  hostile body: not full payload — sent 16.0 MiB of 32 MiB hostile body
    PASS  negative control: small body parses — login=testuser
    PASS  negative control: bytes < cap — 0 KiB
    PASS  happy path: token JSON parses — access_token set
    PASS  happy path: bytes < cap — 0 KiB

  Results: 8/8 passed
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: `readProviderJsonResponse` threw canonical overflow at 16 MiB (Chutes userinfo: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes). Server sent ~16.0 MiB of a 32 MiB hostile payload — capped before full body. Negative control (small userinfo JSON, login=testuser) and happy path (token exchange, access_token set) parsed correctly.
- **What was not tested**: live Chutes API call (the proof exercises the same production SDK helper against the same attack shape the live endpoint could carry); cross-platform Node differences (Node 22 only, matches CI).

## Out of scope

- `src/agents/chutes-oauth.ts` (deprecated core helper) — covered by sibling PR #96777.

## Risk

- **Very low**: swap to `readProviderJsonResponse` — same error shape, 16 MiB default cap invisible to normal responses (OAuth token responses are < 1 KiB). Existing tests: 19/19 pass unchanged.

## Diff stats

```
1 file changed, 7 insertions(+), 4 deletions(-)
```

AI-assisted; reviewed before submission.

target.title: feat(workboard): add move command to CLI and workboard_move tool
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Workboard agents and operators can create, claim, and complete cards, but there was no direct way to move a card from one column (status) to another. Agents had to rely on the dispatch command or manual store mutations to transition cards, which is awkward for deliberate column changes like "moving a card to Review" or "sending it back to Triage."

Why This Change Was Made

The store already had a move(id, status, position) method, but it was never exposed to the CLI or the agent tools layer. This change wires it up in both directions:

• CLI: workboard move <id> --status <status> for operators and agents running in the terminal
• Tool: workboard_move(id, status, token?) for agent tool use, with claim-scoped access control (only agents that have claimed the card can move it, matching the pattern used by reassign and release)

Status values are validated against the WORKBOARD_STATUSES enum at the tool layer before reaching the store, so invalid statuses are rejected early with a clear error message.

User Impact

• Agents can now move cards between columns as part of their workflow (e.g., moving a card to review after completing implementation)
• Operators can manually move cards via the CLI without writing store queries
• Both CLI and tool outputs support --json for machine-readable consumption

I want to underline that the code was written with AI-assistance. But I went over the code and checked it manually

## Evidence

The following evidence comes from manually conducting terminal runs.  I am limiting the outputs to the relevant lines.

First I create a new card
$ pnpm openclaw --dev workboard create card-abc123 --status tod
acc31be5  todo      normal  default  card-abc123

Here is a list to show the status on the board before moving a card
$ pnpm openclaw --dev workboard list
acc31be5  todo      normal  default  card-abc123

OK, now the moving comes:
$ pnpm openclaw --dev workboard move acc31be5 --status running
acc31be5  running   normal  default  card-abc123

List again to see:
$ pnpm openclaw --dev workboard list
acc31be5  running   normal  default  card-abc123

Show details:
$ pnpm openclaw --dev workboard show acc31be5
acc31be5  running   normal  default  card-abc123

Try moving again:
$ pnpm openclaw --dev workboard move acc31be5 --status review
acc31be5  review    normal  default  card-abc123

Try moving back. With JSON:
$ pnpm openclaw --dev workboard move acc31be5 --status triage --json
{
  "card": {
    "id": "acc31be5-2e01-4dd0-93a9-5999779d9a30",
    "title": "card-abc123",
    "status": "triage",
    "priority": "normal",
    "labels": [],
    "position": 1000,
    "createdAt": 1782332679684,
    "updatedAt": 1782333474509,
    "startedAt": 1782333120149,
    "events": [
      {
        "id": "efb13f78-423f-4467-a443-6d61881f8433",
        "kind": "created",
        "at": 1782332679684,
        "toStatus": "todo"
      },
      {
        "id": "df656eb0-3c22-4998-b3db-934e3931836b",
        "kind": "moved",
        "at": 1782333120149,
        "fromStatus": "todo",
        "toStatus": "running"
      },
      {
        "id": "e9446e73-521a-41b8-bffe-16c8a9efde59",
        "kind": "moved",
        "at": 1782333379120,
        "fromStatus": "running",
        "toStatus": "review"
      },
      {
        "id": "1f72ee1f-3736-4269-9c95-b6411a08a7db",
        "at": 1782333474509,
        "kind": "moved",
        "fromStatus": "review
        "toStatus": "triage"
      }
    ]
  }
}

What happens if the status is not valid?
$ pnpm openclaw --dev workboard move acc31be5 --status nonexistant
Error: status must be one of: triage, backlog, todo, scheduled, ready, running, review, blocked, done.
Exactly, we want a reasoned error if the column does not exist.

What if the --status flag is missing?
$ pnpm openclaw --dev workboard move acc31be5
Error: --status is required. Use: workboard move <id> --status <status>
This is also correct: a status is necessary to move the card to a target column.

Tests were also conducted:
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/workboard/src/tools.test.ts extensions/workboard/src/cli.test.ts
 RUN  v4.1.8 /home/clawdbot/openclaw
 ✓  extensions  extensions/workboard/src/tools.test.ts (7 tests) 350ms
 ✓  extensions  extensions/workboard/src/cli.test.ts (10 tests) 55ms
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  17 passed (17)


There is no linked issue for this PR.

@clawsweeper please have a look at it.
