target.title: feat: add Gradium realtime speech-to-text provider
target.body:
## Summary

  - **Problem:** PR #64958 added Gradium TTS but no matching STT, so Gradium-only voice deployments still had to bring in a second vendor for inbound transcription on Voice Call.
  - **Why it matters:** Lets operators run end-to-end speech (in and out) on a single Gradium account, simplifying credential and billing setup.
  - **What changed:** New `extensions/gradium/realtime-transcription-provider.ts` registers a `RealtimeTranscriptionProviderPlugin` that opens a WebSocket to
  `wss://api.gradium.ai/api/speech/asr`, performs the setup handshake, base64-encodes inbound audio frames, and surfaces `text` events as transcripts. Plugin manifest now declares
  `realtimeTranscriptionProviders: ["gradium"]`. Docs updated for `voice-call`, `media-overview`, and the Gradium provider page.
  - **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** TTS provider (shipped in #64958), other transcription providers, voice-call core wiring, batch/HTTP STT path (Gradium only exposes streaming),
   Gradium TTS PR's `shared.ts` defaults (we reuse them).

  ## Change Type (select all)

  - [ ] Bug fix
  - [x] Feature
  - [ ] Refactor required for the fix
  - [ ] Docs
  - [ ] Security hardening
  - [ ] Chore/infra

  ## Scope (select all touched areas)

  - [ ] Gateway / orchestration
  - [ ] Skills / tool execution
  - [ ] Auth / tokens
  - [ ] Memory / storage
  - [x] Integrations
  - [ ] API / contracts
  - [ ] UI / DX
  - [ ] CI/CD / infra

  ## Linked Issue/PR

  - Closes #
  - Related #64958
  - [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

  ## Root Cause (if applicable)

  N/A — feature addition, not a fix.

  ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

  N/A — feature addition. New unit + live tests cover the new code path.

  ## User-visible / Behavior Changes

  - New `gradium` value for `plugins.entries.voice-call.config.streaming.provider`.
  - New `plugins.entries.voice-call.config.streaming.providers.gradium` config block (`apiKey`, `baseUrl`, `modelName`, `inputFormat`, `language`).
  - API key resolves from the existing `GRADIUM_API_KEY` env var (already declared by the TTS PR's manifest).
  - Default `inputFormat` is `ulaw_8000` so it works out of the box with Twilio media streams.

  ## Diagram (if applicable)

  ```
  Voice Call (Twilio media stream)
         |  ulaw_8000 audio frames
         v
  RealtimeTranscriptionSession (gradium)
         |  WebSocket: wss://api.gradium.ai/api/speech/asr
         |  -> {"type":"setup","model_name":"default","input_format":"ulaw_8000"}
         |  <- {"type":"ready"}                      (markReady)
         |  -> {"type":"audio","audio":"<base64>"}   (per frame)
         |  <- {"type":"text","text":"..."}          (onTranscript)
         |  -> {"type":"end_of_stream"}              (on close)
         v
  Voice Call agent loop
  ```

  ## Security Impact (required)

  - New permissions/capabilities? **No** (reuses `GRADIUM_API_KEY` already declared by the TTS PR).
  - Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No** (resolved via the standard `normalizeResolvedSecretInputString` path).
  - New/changed network calls? **Yes** — outbound WebSocket to `wss://api.gradium.ai/api/speech/asr` when configured.
  - Command/tool execution surface changed? **No.**
  - Data access scope changed? **No.**

  Risk + mitigation: when configured, live call audio is forwarded to Gradium. Mitigation: provider only activates when an operator opts in via `streaming.provider: "gradium"`;
  default-disabled, no auto-selection over existing providers.

  ## Repro + Verification

  ### Environment

  - OS: macOS
  - Runtime/container: Node 22, local pnpm dev tree
  - Model/provider: Gradium (TTS to generate test audio + STT to transcribe)
  - Integration/channel (if any): Voice Call streaming-STT contract, no Twilio in this verification
  - Relevant config (redacted): `GRADIUM_API_KEY`

  ### Steps

  1. `export GRADIUM_API_KEY=...`
  2. `pnpm install && pnpm build`
  3. `pnpm test extensions/gradium` → 17/17 pass
  4. `OPENCLAW_LIVE_TEST=1 pnpm test:live -- extensions/gradium/gradium.live.test.ts` → 2/2 pass

  ### Expected

  - Live test synthesizes a marker phrase via Gradium TTS, streams μ-law frames into the new STT provider, and the returned transcript matches the marker.

  ### Actual

  - Both live tests pass; STT round-trip completes in ~4s; transcript matches.

  ## Evidence

  - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after — new tests pass; no behavior was previously asserted for this provider since it didn't exist.
  - [x] Trace/log snippets — live test output:

  ```
  [live] loaded 4 env vars from ~/.profile
  Audio written to /var/folders/.../gradium-live-test.wav
   ✓ extensions/gradium/gradium.live.test.ts (2 tests) 5507ms
       ✓ synthesizes speech through the registered provider  1448ms
       ✓ streams realtime STT through the registered transcription provider  4058ms

   Test Files  1 passed (1)
        Tests  2 passed (2)
  ```

  ## Human Verification (required)

  What I personally verified (not just CI), and how:

  - **Verified scenarios:**
    - End-to-end Gradium round-trip on live API: TTS → STT, transcript matches the phrase.
    - `pnpm test extensions/gradium` (17/17), `pnpm test src/plugins/contracts/registry.contract.test.ts` (19/19), `pnpm check:changed` (clean), `pnpm test:changed` (clean).
    - Manifest contract picks up `realtimeTranscriptionProviders: ["gradium"]` automatically (capability snapshot is dynamic, no manual regen needed).
  - **Edge cases checked:**
    - Missing `GRADIUM_API_KEY` raises `"Gradium API key missing"` from `createSession`.
    - Invalid `inputFormat` rejected at config-resolution time.
    - WebSocket setup payload only includes `json_config.language` when configured.

  ## Review Conversations

  - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
  - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need a reviewer or maintainer judgment.

  ## Compatibility / Migration

  - Backward compatible? **Yes** — purely additive provider registration; no existing config keys touched.
  - Config/env changes? **No** new env vars (`GRADIUM_API_KEY` already declared by #64958).
  - Migration needed? **No.**

  ## Risks and Mitigations

  - **Risk:** Gradium ASR endpoint outage causes streaming STT failures for opted-in operators.
  - **Mitigation:** Voice-call streaming providers fail open (log a warning and skip media streaming rather than failing the call); operators can fall back to a different
  `streaming.provider` without code changes.

## Summary

Resolves the documentation gap identified in #74732. Adds focused recipes
for using oMLX (Apple Silicon MLX inference server) as a memorySearch
embedding provider via the existing OpenAI-compatible adapter.

## Changes

- `docs/concepts/memory-search.md`: New "Using oMLX" subsection under
  Quick start, plus an oMLX row in the Supported providers table.
- `docs/reference/memory-config.md`: New oMLX example under Remote
  endpoint config, with a warning against the common `provider: "ollama"`
  mistake.
- `docs/providers/openai.md`: New "Local OpenAI-compatible servers"
  subsection under Memory embeddings, surfacing oMLX as a notable
  Apple Silicon use case.

## What this addresses

Per the Codex review on #74732, this is the **narrow docs-only fix**:

> Add a focused docs recipe for oMLX that uses the existing OpenAI-compatible
> embedding path, warns users not to configure oMLX as Ollama, includes the
> local /v1 base URL and model example from the report, and tells users to
> reindex after changing embedding models or vector spaces.

All three points are covered. No core behavior change; no new provider
adapter introduced. The first-class `omlx` adapter discussion (Option B in
#74732) remains separate and is **not** part of this PR.

## Verification

| Component | Version |

target.title: config: accept per-agent elevatedDefault overrides and complete per-agent verbose/elevated flow (#73680)
target.body:
## Summary

Makes a **per-agent `elevatedDefault`** actually work, and completes per-agent
`verboseDefault` flow in the reply path (issue #73680).

## The gap

On current `main`, the runtime already reads a per-agent `elevatedDefault`
(directive resolution + status surfaces) and the docs document it, **but**:
- `AgentEntrySchema` does not include `elevatedDefault`, so config validation
  silently rejects/strips a per-agent `elevatedDefault`.
- `resolveAgentConfig` never populates `elevatedDefault` (unlike `verboseDefault`).
- The reply directive resolution reads only `agentCfg.elevatedDefault` (the global
  default) and `agentCfg.verboseDefault`, skipping the per-agent `agentEntry`
  layer that `reasoningDefault` already uses.

Net effect: a documented per-agent `elevatedDefault` had no effect.

## The fix

- Add `elevatedDefault` to the per-agent config type (`AgentConfig`) and
  `AgentEntrySchema`, with schema help + label entries.
- Resolve it in `resolveAgentConfig` as `entry.elevatedDefault ?? defaults.elevatedDefault`,
  mirroring `verboseDefault`.
- In `resolveCurrentDirectiveLevels` and `resolveReplyDirectives`, read
  `agentEntry.elevatedDefault` (and `agentEntry.verboseDefault`) as the per-agent
  layer between session overrides and the global default — the same precedence
  `reasoningDefault` already uses (`session ?? agentEntry ?? global`). Elevated
  resolution stays gated by `elevatedAllowed`, so this does not widen when elevated
  output is disallowed.
- Regenerate the config doc baseline hash for the new key.

`verboseDefault`'s per-agent schema landed on `main` independently since this PR
was opened; this revision drops the now-redundant schema bits and keeps the still
-missing `elevatedDefault` schema plus the per-agent verbose/elevated fallback in
the directive path. Rebased onto current `main`.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-scope.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/directive-handling.levels.test.ts src/config/config.schema-regressions.test.ts` → 46 passed (new: per-agent elevated resolution, directive precedence session > agent > global, schema acceptance).
- `node --import tsx scripts/generate-config-doc-baseline.ts --check` → OK.
- `pnpm tsgo:core` and `pnpm tsgo:test:src` → clean. `oxfmt --check` / `oxlint` clean.

Closes #73680

target.title: fix(matrix): honor MSC3967 and surface MAS reset URL on cross-signing bootstrap
target.body:
# Honor MSC3967 first-publish and surface MAS reset stage cleanly when cross-signing requires UIA

## What this PR does

Makes the Matrix channel's cross-signing bootstrap survive Synapse homeservers fronted by Matrix Authentication Service (MSC3861, e.g. ESS deployments) when the bot logs in with a Personal Access Token. Today those bots fail with a misleading "provide `matrix.password` for `m.login.password` fallback" error and never publish cross-signing keys, breaking E2EE message decryption and self-verification.

After this change:

- A bot with **no** server-side master cross-signing key publishes a fresh identity using the [MSC3967](https://github.com/matrix-org/matrix-spec-proposals/pull/3967) first-publish path (no UIA, no auth callback round trip).
- A bot whose local secret-storage state is stale relative to the server gets a typed `MatrixCrossSigningResetRequiredError` carrying the MAS reset URL, so the operator can complete the OAuth re-auth in a browser and re-run the bootstrap inside the MAS approval window.
- The UIA callback now inspects the 401 response body, routes by *advertised* stage rather than blindly trying `m.login.dummy` then `m.login.password`, and threads the UIA `session` token through subsequent requests (matrix-js-sdk's `authCallback` contract requires this).

Bug report with full reproduction steps and environment details: [MAS-CROSSSIGNING-BUG-REPORT.md](./MAS-CROSSSIGNING-BUG-REPORT.md) — should be filed as the companion issue.

## Changes

### `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk/types.ts`

- New exported error classes:
  - `MatrixCrossSigningResetRequiredError(stages, resetUrl?, session?)` — thrown when the homeserver demands `org.matrix.cross_signing_reset`. Message includes the MAS URL when advertised, and a fallback note pointing at the Synapse admin reset endpoint when no URL is provided.
  - `MatrixUiaUnsupportedStagesError(stages, hasPassword)` — thrown when the homeserver advertises only stages this client cannot satisfy non-interactively (e.g. `m.login.password` on a passwordless PAT-only bot).
- New types: `MatrixHomeserverCapabilities`, `MatrixUiaResponseBody`.

### `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk/crypto-bootstrap.ts`

- `MatrixCryptoBootstrapperDeps` gains an optional `getHomeserverCapabilities?: () => Promise<MatrixHomeserverCapabilities>`.
- New `getHomeserverCapabilitiesCached()` / `isMasFronted()` / `masResetActionUrl()` helpers (the last constructs `<account_management_uri>?action=org.matrix.cross_signing_reset` per MSC2965 §6.4.4).
- New `isUiaChallenge()` / `extractUiaStages()` / `extractMasResetUrl()` / `isCrossSigningKeyMismatchError()` parser helpers (private to the file).
- `createSigningKeysUiAuthCallback` rewritten:
  - First attempt is always `makeRequest(null)` (the MSC3967 first-publish path).
  - If that throws a 401-with-UIA-body, parse the advertised stages.
  - Route by stage: `m.login.dummy` → `{type, session}`; `m.login.password` (with configured password) → `{type, identifier, password, session}`; `org.matrix.cross_signing_reset` → `MatrixCrossSigningResetRequiredError` carrying URL (from the 401 body if present, else constructed via MSC2965 from the cached `account_management_uri`) + session.
  - On MAS-fronted homeservers without a satisfiable stage, also surface `MatrixCrossSigningResetRequiredError`. On non-MAS, throw `MatrixUiaUnsupportedStagesError`.
  - All other 401 bodies and non-401 errors re-throw unchanged.
- `bootstrapCrossSigning()` keeps the existing first-pass / forced-reset / fallback-reset structure, with two changes:
  - Every catch block now propagates `MatrixCrossSigningResetRequiredError` instances as-is rather than wrapping/logging them, so the actionable URL reaches the CLI cleanly.
  - The bootstrapper deliberately **does not** short-circuit before the upload attempt on MAS. A reset upload to MAS looks doomed (Synapse 401s with the cross-signing-reset stage), but when the operator has just approved that action in MAS via a browser, the next upload **within the approval window** succeeds. Letting the upload happen in both states means the same code path serves both the "tell me what to do" and "do it" sides of recovery — the 401 turns into a structured error with the URL; the 200 lands cross-signing.

### `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk.ts`

- New `detectHomeserverCapabilities()` method that probes `/_matrix/client/v1/auth_metadata` and returns `{ msAuthService, accountManagementUri }`. `msAuthService` is true when the endpoint returns 200 with an OAuth `issuer`; `accountManagementUri` carries the MSC2965 base URL used to construct action URLs. Result is cached on the instance.
  - Note: an earlier draft used `unstable_features["org.matrix.msc3861"]` from `client.getVersions()`. That doesn't work — Synapse 1.145+/ESS no longer surfaces the unstable flag, so the only reliable in-band MAS marker is the auth_metadata endpoint itself.
- Wired into the `MatrixCryptoBootstrapper` deps in `ensureCryptoSupportInitialized()`.

## What this PR explicitly does **not** do

- Does **not** implement an OAuth re-auth flow inside the bot. The `org.matrix.cross_signing_reset` stage is designed to require a human in a browser; we surface the URL and stop. Operators who need fully unattended recovery should use Synapse's `POST /_synapse/admin/v1/users/<user>/_reset_cross_signing_keys` admin endpoint and then let the bot do a clean MSC3967 first-publish.
- Does **not** modify matrix-js-sdk. The UIA callback is the SDK's already-supported extensibility point; openclaw just wasn't using it correctly.
- Does **not** touch `account_data` to "reset" cross-signing — server-side cross-signing public keys live in a separate keys table queried via `/keys/query`, not in `account_data`, so clearing `m.cross_signing.{master,self_signing,user_signing}` would not unblock MSC3967. (An earlier draft of this patch tried that path and it was dropped after verification.)
- Does **not** change matrix-rust-sdk OTK regeneration behaviour, the `verify list/accept/start/sas/confirm-sas` gateway-RPC routing, or the Bonjour mDNS spam — those are separate issues.

## Verified end-to-end on real MAS-fronted Synapse

Confirmed against `matrix.thepolycule.ca` (Synapse 1.145.0+ess.1, MAS-fronted, OAuth issuer `https://auth.matrix.thepolycule.ca/`). With this patch the bot can now:

1. Detect MAS via `/_matrix/client/v1/auth_metadata` (status 200, OAuth issuer present).
2. On `verify bootstrap --force-reset-cross-signing` with no MAS approval current, attempt the reset upload, receive Synapse's 401, and surface `MatrixCrossSigningResetRequiredError` with the URL `https://auth.matrix.thepolycule.ca/account/?action=org.matrix.cross_signing_reset` (constructed via MSC2965 from the cached `account_management_uri`).
3. After the operator opens that URL and approves the cross-signing reset action in MAS, the next `verify bootstrap --force-reset-cross-signing` within the MAS approval window has its `/keys/device_signing/upload` succeed (200), and `/_matrix/client/v3/keys/query` thereafter returns the bot's freshly published `master_keys` and `self_signing_keys` for the first time.

Pre-existing matrix-rust-sdk OTK regeneration collisions on `/keys/upload` (`signed_curve25519:AAAAAAAAAA0 already exists`) and incomplete owner-side device verification are observable in the same e2e run but are unrelated to this PR — they are filed as separate upstream issues.

## Tests

`extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk/crypto-bootstrap.test.ts`:

- 2 existing UIA tests rewritten to use real 401-shaped error bodies (`{httpStatus: 401, data: {flows, session, params}}`) instead of generic `Error("need auth")`, matching the matrix-js-sdk MatrixError shape that the SDK actually delivers to `authCallback`.
- 5 new regression tests:
  - `uses dummy UIA stage when advertised even alongside other stages` — verifies stage prioritisation when both dummy and password are offered.
  - `surfaces MatrixCrossSigningResetRequiredError with the MAS reset URL` — asserts the error carries `resetUrl`, `session`, and `stages`.
  - `attempts forced reset on MAS so a freshly approved reset can land within the MAS window` — asserts the bootstrapper does NOT short-circuit; it attempts the upload so a current MAS approval can land cross-signing on the same call.
  - `allows MSC3967 first-publish on MAS when the user has no master key yet` — asserts no-auth path succeeds.
  - `falls through to a reset upload after an import-key mismatch on MAS, surfacing the MAS URL on 401` — asserts the post-mismatch fallback attempts a `setupNewCrossSigning` upload (so a recent MAS approval can land it) and surfaces `MatrixCrossSigningResetRequiredError` with the MSC2965-constructed URL when no approval is current.

`pnpm test --filter @openclaw/matrix` (or the upstream equivalent) — 26 tests in `crypto-bootstrap.test.ts` pass; full sdk-dir suite (5 files, 65 tests) passes.

## Test plan for reviewers

End-to-end against a Synapse 1.79+ deployment fronted by MAS (e.g. ESS) with a PAT-authenticated bot:

1. Create the bot account in MAS, mint a PAT, configure `channels.matrix.<account>.userId` and `MATRIX_ACCESS_TOKEN`, deploy.
2. Ensure server-side cross-signing is empty for the bot (use `POST /_synapse/admin/v1/users/<user>/_reset_cross_signing_keys` if not).
3. Run `openclaw matrix verify bootstrap`.
   - Expected: `POST .../keys/device_signing/upload [... 200]` (MSC3967 path).
   - Expected: `PUT .../room_keys/version [... 200]` (key backup created).
4. Run `openclaw matrix verify status` — should show `Cross-signing published: yes`.
5. Negative test: corrupt the bot's local secret storage `m.cross_signing.master` blob and re-run `verify bootstrap`. Expected: clean exit with `MatrixCrossSigningResetRequiredError`, MAS URL printed, no silent retry loop.
6. Encrypted DM round-trip from a verified Element X session — expect successful Megolm decrypt in the bot log and a decrypted reply on the sender's client.

## Related

- Bug report: [MAS-CROSSSIGNING-BUG-REPORT.md](./MAS-CROSSSIGNING-BUG-REPORT.md)
- [MSC3967 — Do not require UIA when first uploading cross-signing keys](https://github.com/matrix-org/matrix-spec-proposals/pull/3967)
- [MSC3861 — Next-generation auth for Matrix](https://github.com/matrix-org/matrix-spec-proposals/pull/3861)
- [Synapse PR #17509 — `org.matrix.cross_signing_reset` UIA stage for MAS](https://github.com/element-hq/synapse/pull/17509)

target.title: fix: ensure CLI processes exit after command completion on Windows
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes CLI processes (node.exe openclaw.mjs) remaining alive after command execution on Windows. The CLI entrypoint was missing process.exit() calls after commands completed, causing processes to hang.

## Changes

- Added explicit `process.exit(process.exitCode ?? 0)` call after CLI entrypoint execution in openclaw.mjs
- Ensures all async code paths properly terminate on Windows

## Testing

- Verified CLI commands now exit cleanly on Windows after completion
- Existing test suite passes

Fixes openclaw/openclaw#74378

target.title: feat(browser): add agent download actions
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: the agent-facing `browser` tool did not expose the managed browser download helpers even though the CLI/control API already supported `/download` and `/wait/download`.
- Why it matters: agents could inspect pages and snapshots but could not ask the managed browser to click a download ref and return the saved file path.
- What changed: add `download` and `waitfordownload` actions to the browser tool schema/routing, export the existing runtime helpers, document the actions, and add regression coverage for host and node proxy routing.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): existing-session download interception remains managed-only; this does not bypass the browser server's existing 501/unsupported behavior for profiles that cannot intercept downloads.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes N/A
- Related #38519, #12378
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the browser extension already had managed download endpoints and client helpers, but the agent tool schema and routing never exposed `download` / `waitfordownload`. During implementation, the new action routing also needed to align outer transport timeouts with browser-side download waits instead of keeping the older fixed 20s HTTP/proxy timeout.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no agent-tool regression test asserting download action routing or transport timeout propagation.
- Contributing context (if known): prior PR #12378 attempted a similar action surface but did not land.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.test.ts`, `extensions/browser/src/browser/client-actions-core.downloads.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: agent `browser` actions route `download` and `waitfordownload` through the host client or node proxy, preserve request bodies, require explicit `ref`/`path` where needed, and give long download waits enough outer transport timeout.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the browser-side download implementation already exists; the risk here is the agent tool contract and proxy/client timeout handoff.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Agents can now call the built-in `browser` tool with `action="download"` to click a snapshot ref and save the resulting file, or `action="waitfordownload"` to wait for the next managed download. Results include the saved download path returned by the existing browser control API.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[agent browser tool] -> [snapshot/action helpers only] -> [no download action]

After:
[agent browser tool] -> [download/waitfordownload] -> [managed browser endpoint] -> [saved file path]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: this exposes existing managed browser download endpoints through the agent tool. It keeps the existing browser server/profile capability checks, keeps existing-session download interception managed-only, and constrains saved paths to the browser download path handling already used by the control API.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux 6.8.0-55-generic x86_64
- Runtime/container: Node v24.13.0, pnpm 10.33.0
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Browser plugin / built-in browser tool
- Relevant config (redacted): default managed browser action routing; node proxy tests use mocked browser-capable nodes

### Steps

1. Create the browser tool.
2. Execute `action="download"` with a snapshot `ref` and output `path`, or execute `action="waitfordownload"` with an optional output `path`.
3. For node targets, route the request through `browser.proxy` and preserve both body timeout and outer transport timeout.

### Expected

- The action reaches `/download` or `/wait/download` and returns the browser download payload, including the saved file path.
- Explicit and default download waits are not cut short by the outer host/proxy transport timeout.

### Actual

- Implemented behavior matches expected in unit/seam tests.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Verification run locally:

```text
pnpm test extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/client-actions-core.downloads.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose
# passed: 2 files, 56 tests

NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test:changed
# passed: 2 shards, 854 passed, 1 skipped

pnpm docs:check-mdx
# passed

pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md docs/tools/browser-control.md extensions/browser/plugin-registration.ts extensions/browser/src/browser-runtime.ts extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.runtime.ts extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.schema.ts extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/constants.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/client-actions-core.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/client-actions-core.downloads.test.ts
# passed

git diff --check
# passed

NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=2048 node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig tsconfig.oxlint.extensions.json extensions/browser/plugin-registration.ts extensions/browser/src/browser-runtime.ts extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.runtime.ts extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.schema.ts extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.ts extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/client-actions-core.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/client-actions-core.downloads.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/constants.ts
# passed: 0 warnings, 0 errors
```

Note: `pnpm check:changed` passed conflict-marker checks, changelog attribution checks, extension production typecheck, and extension test typecheck, then local broad `lint:extensions` was terminated by `tsgolint` with SIGTERM on an 8 GB host. I used the targeted repo lint wrapper above to avoid exceeding the host memory limit.

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: host-client download routing; host-client wait-for-download routing; node proxy download routing; node proxy wait-for-download routing; default 120s download wait transport timeout; explicit 30s download wait transport timeout; required `ref`/`path` validation; docs/format/lint/typecheck coverage listed above.
- Edge cases checked: omitted `timeoutMs` uses the browser-side default download wait plus transport slack; explicit `timeoutMs` scales both proxy and gateway waits; existing-session managed-only scope is documented and unchanged.
- What you did **not** verify: a live Chromium download in a real browser session; this PR relies on the existing browser-side download implementation and adds the missing agent tool contract/routing coverage.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: exposing downloads through the agent browser tool could let agents save files unexpectedly.
  - Mitigation: the action uses existing managed browser endpoints and their path/profile constraints; unsupported existing-session profiles remain blocked by the browser server.
- Risk: long download waits could be cut short by outer transport timeouts.
  - Mitigation: host client and node proxy transport timeouts now scale from the effective download wait with slack, with tests for explicit and default waits.

target.title: fix(control-ui): preserve numeric chat input in composer
target.body:
Add autocomplete/autocorrect/autocapitalize/spellcheck/inputmode attributes to chat textarea to prevent browser/OS numeric auto-formatting. Add regression tests for composer attributes and numeric payload pass-through.

## Summary

**Problem:** Control UI chat composer textarea lacked plain-text input attributes, so browser/OS could auto-format numeric-only drafts (e.g. `123456789` becoming `123,456,789`) before `chat.send`.

**Fix:** Five HTML attributes (`autocomplete`, `autocorrect`, `autocapitalize`, `spellcheck`, `inputmode`) suppress auto-formatting and preserve numeric input as typed.

**Out of scope:** No gateway/message normalization/send-pipeline changes. Broader Chinese input formatting (refs #80198) not addressed.

## Linked context

Closes #74354

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: Control UI chat composer auto-formats numeric-only input (e.g. `123456789` becomes `123,456,789`) on mobile browsers before `chat.send`; textarea now has explicit plain-text input attributes to prevent this
- Real environment tested: Windows 11, Node.js v22.19, OpenClaw latest main (commit c8d21fe, 2026-06-02), Chrome 126
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Applied 5 plain-text attributes to composer textarea in `chat.ts`; ran `pnpm openclaw chat` and typed numeric-only message `123456789` into the composer; inspected textarea DOM attributes via Chrome DevTools Elements panel
- Evidence after fix: DOM inspection of the chat composer textarea after fix confirms all 5 plain-text input attributes are present at the correct element boundary:
```
<textarea
  class="chat-composer"
  autocomplete="off"
  autocorrect="off"
  autocapitalize="off"
  spellcheck="false"
  inputmode="text"
  dir="ltr"
  aria-autocomplete="list"
>
123456789
</textarea>
```
Chrome DevTools Elements panel, Node.js v22.19, commit eba3b69.
- Observed result after fix: The textarea accepts `123456789` as plain text without browser auto-formatting; all 5 attributes confirmed via DOM inspection; `chat.send` receives the original numeric string unchanged
- What was not tested: Live mobile browser numeric formatting on iOS Safari / Android Chrome specifically; voice input behavior; third-party keyboard apps

## Tests and validation

- `ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts`: New `describe("chat composer input behavior")` -- asserts 5 attributes on composer textarea
- `ui/src/ui/app-chat.test.ts`: New `it("forwards numeric-only chatMessage to chat.send unchanged")` -- verifies `123456789` pass-through unchanged
- No CHANGELOG.md edits

## Risk checklist

- userVisible: No (static HTML attributes only, no visual or functional difference for non-numeric input)
- configEnvMigration: No
- securityAuthNetwork: No
- Mitigation: Attributes are standard HTML, improve input behavior, no breaking change

## Current review state

- Branch refreshed against latest upstream main (c8d21fe). Dependency Guard checkable.
- Addressed: Greptile P2 test title, ClawSweeper P1 CHANGELOG removal, RomneyDa rebase request.
- Fixed: describe block corruption in app-chat.test.ts (descr/ibe syntax error resolved, commit eba3b69).
- Pre-existing CI failures (not from this PR): dependency-guard reports unused files in src/state/ and src/agents/cache/ which are not touched by this PR.
- [x] AI-assisted (built with Codex)

target.title: docs(gateway): add external auth-proxy recipe
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a recipe page documenting how to use existing OpenClaw primitives (custom `baseUrl`, eager SecretRef snapshot) to build an external auth-injection proxy that closes the agent-self-read gap acknowledged in #11829 and #10659.

The page does **not** propose any core change. It documents a pattern users can apply today.

## Why now

`@steipete`'s 2026-04-26 review on #10659 explicitly framed current SecretRef + redaction + plugin hooks as **partial** hardening, and noted that native masked-secret / credential-provider boundaries are unbuilt. PR #9271 attempted a full in-core proxy and was closed unmerged at 15.9K LOC. PRs #12839 and #22744 followed similar fates.

That leaves users hitting this issue with no documented workaround. This recipe gives them one — focused on the surface OpenClaw already exposes — without staking a position on what the eventual native solution should look like.

## Field validation

Implemented and running on a 21-tenant Badclaw deployment (Hetzner CPX41) for the Anthropic provider. End-to-end verification:

- Agent container has no `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` in env, in `/run/secrets/`, or anywhere on its filesystem.
- A live chat completion through the proxy returns 200 in 1326ms (Anthropic-side latency dominates; localhost-hop overhead measured at <1ms).
- Proxy access logs show source-IP-based tenant identification working as designed.

Reference implementation linked from the page: https://github.com/coletebou/Badland/tree/master/badclaw/services/auth-proxy (~150 LOC stdlib-only Node, MIT-licensed).

## What this PR doesn't do

- Doesn't add a built-in proxy. That's a much larger conversation (#9271 territory) and would need maintainer alignment on shape.
- Doesn't change SecretRef semantics, OAuth refresh, or any runtime behavior.
- Doesn't conflict with the active plugin-SDK work in #59165 / #72548 — those operate on a different bridge (credential resolution, not request rewriting).

## Test plan

- [x] Mintlify navigation entry added under "Authentication and secrets"
- [x] Verified `docs.json` parses (no orphan groups, page reachable from sidebar)
- [x] Internal links to `/gateway/secrets`, `/gateway/authentication` resolve
- [x] External links to issues/PRs (#11829, #10659, #9271) verified live

## Related

- #11829 — Security Roadmap: Protecting API Keys from Agent Access
- #10659 — Masked Secrets
- #9271 — Earlier full-proxy attempt (referenced in the page as a useful but more ambitious comparison)
- The comment I just left on #11829 with field experience: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/11829#issuecomment-4342619242

target.title: feat(codex): scope user MCP server attachment by the tool allowlist
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

An OpenClaw operator running a **Codex-runtime agent** cannot give that agent access to a configured MCP server without also granting it Codex's full native code-mode shell/filesystem surface. The two are conflated behind a single gate.

In `extensions/codex/src/app-server/dynamic-tool-build.ts`, `shouldEnableCodexAppServerNativeToolSurface` returns true only when the effective tool allowlist contains a literal `*`. That one boolean (`nativeToolSurfaceEnabled`) is then reused, unchanged, in `attempt-startup.ts` as `userMcpServersEnabled` — which decides whether `buildCodexUserMcpServersThreadConfigPatch` projects `cfg.mcp.servers` into the Codex thread at all. Consequences:

1. **All-or-nothing.** Any least-privilege allowlist (`tools.allow` / `tools.alsoAllow` without `*`) silently disables **every** user MCP server, even ones the allowlist explicitly names. The MCP process spawns but its tools never register in the compiled toolset.
2. **Over-exposure when servers do attach.** `buildCodexUserMcpServersThreadConfigPatch` projected **every** enabled `cfg.mcp.servers` entry, filtered only by the per-server `codex.agents` field — it never consulted the tool allowlist.

This is a general gap for any scoped Codex agent with any MCP server, not a single integration.

## What Changed

Decouple the two capabilities and make MCP attachment flow through the **same allowlist contract OpenClaw already uses for every other tool** (`tool-policy.ts`):

- **Native code mode is unchanged** — `shouldEnableCodexAppServerNativeToolSurface` keeps its fail-closed, wildcard-only rule.
- **`shouldEnableUserMcpServersForCodexAppServer`** now answers only *"is it safe to attach external MCP servers this turn?"* (not a memory-flush run; OpenClaw sandbox, if active, can be honored). The per-server decision moves to the projection.
- **`resolveCodexMcpServerAllow`** matches against the **provider-safe model-facing tool ids** OpenClaw exposes — `<sanitizedServer>__<sanitizedTool>` via `sanitizeServerName` + `normalizeToolName`, **not** the raw `mcp.servers` key — and honors the same entries as the bundle-MCP allowlist contract:
  - `undefined` → include, all tools (legacy unrestricted behavior preserved)
  - `*`, `bundle-mcp`, `group:plugins` → include, all tools
  - `<server>__*` → include that server, all tools
  - `<server>__<tool>` → include that server, scoped to the named tool(s)
  - no matching token → exclude the server
- **`buildCodexUserMcpServersThreadConfigPatch`** projects **only** referenced servers, and for exact-tool entries emits Codex's per-server **`enabled_tools`** filter so other tools from an attached server stay unreachable. The thread-config fingerprint varies with the patch, so a thread rebinds when scope changes.

### Addresses ClawSweeper review
- **Provider-safe matching** (was raw-key): `Outlook Graph` (ids `outlook-graph__*`) resolves; `bundle-mcp` / `group:plugins` honored.
- **Per-tool scope** (was discarded): exact entries emit `enabled_tools` instead of attaching the whole server. (`enabled_tools` carries the sanitized tool fragment; exact for provider-safe names, fail-closed otherwise, since the live tool list isn't known at projection time.)

## Files
- `extensions/codex/src/app-server/{dynamic-tool-build,run-attempt,attempt-startup,thread-lifecycle}.ts` — decouple + plumb `userMcpServersEnabled`, pass `toolsAllow` to the projection
- `src/agents/cli-runner/bundle-mcp-codex.ts` — `resolveCodexMcpServerAllow` + allowlist-aware projection with `enabled_tools`
- `docs/plugins/codex-harness.md`; tests as below

## Evidence

### Unit tests (`vitest run`)
- `src/agents/cli-runner/bundle-mcp-codex.user-config.test.ts` — **24 passed** (undefined/`*`/`bundle-mcp`/`group:plugins` attach all; `opik__*` only opik, no `enabled_tools`; exact `opik__list,opik__read` → `enabled_tools:["list","read"]`; provider-safe `Outlook Graph` ↔ `outlook-graph__*`; `["message"]`/`[]` → none).
- `extensions/codex/src/app-server/dynamic-tool-build.test.ts` — **51 passed**, incl. the assertion that `shouldEnableCodexAppServerNativeToolSurface(["opik__*"]) === false` — **native code mode stays disabled** under a scoped MCP allowlist.

### Runtime proof — patched OpenClaw projection (real code, unmocked)
Driving the actual `buildCodexUserMcpServersThreadConfigPatch` over a config with `opik` + `notion` + `atlas`:
```
toolsAllow=undefined                    -> servers=["opik","notion","atlas"]  scope=all tools each
toolsAllow=["*"]                        -> servers=["opik","notion","atlas"]  scope=all tools each
toolsAllow=["opik__*"]                  -> servers=["opik"]                    scope={"opik":"(all tools)"}
toolsAllow=["opik__list","opik__read"]  -> servers=["opik"]                    scope={"opik":["list","read"]}
toolsAllow=["notion__*"]                -> servers=["notion"]                  scope={"notion":"(all tools)"}
toolsAllow=["message","web_search"]     -> servers=[]                          scope=none
```

### Runtime proof — real Codex app-server (`codex-cli 0.139.0`)
Drove the **real** `codex app-server` over JSON-RPC stdio with two stub stdio MCP servers (each exposing `list,read,write,schema`): `opik` configured with `enabled_tools=["list"]` (what the projection emits for `["opik__list"]`), `notion` unfiltered (the `["notion__*"]` case). Then `mcpServerStatus/list` (`detail: toolsAndAuthOnly`):
```
=== LIVE MCP TOOLS REGISTERED BY CODEX ===
opik:   [list]                       <- enabled_tools filter honored by the real binary
notion: [list, read, schema, write]  <- unscoped server, all tools
(other entries omitted: unrelated servers from local ~/.codex/config.toml)
```
This confirms the real Codex binary (a) treats `enabled_tools`/`disabled_tools` as first-class per-server fields (visible in `codex mcp get` schema output) and (b) registers **only** the scoped tool when the filter is set.

### Types
Full-CI `check-prod-types` / `check-lint` / `check-docs` pass; standalone extension `tsc` shows only pre-existing plugin-sdk `dist`-sync noise, none referencing this PR's symbols.

## Security posture
- Native code-mode shell/file access still requires a literal `*` — unchanged (proven above).
- Default-open for unrestricted (`undefined`) agents preserved; sandbox-without-exec-server still attaches no user MCP servers.
- Net effect is *tighter*: scoped allowlists attach only referenced servers; exact-tool entries attach only named tools via `enabled_tools`.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(infra): wrap provider auth resolution in timeout for status --usage --json
target.body:
## Summary
- Wraps `resolveProviderAuths()` in the existing `withTimeout` helper so auth resolution (OAuth token refresh, plugin auth hooks, secret exec resolvers) cannot hang indefinitely
- Returns empty providers on auth timeout instead of blocking forever, allowing the JSON command to complete

## Problem
`openclaw status --usage --json` hangs when called from a non-TTY subprocess (regression from 2026.4.23 to 2026.4.26). The individual provider usage fetches are bounded by `withTimeout`, but `resolveProviderAuths()` — which iterates sequentially over 7 providers calling plugin auth hooks, OAuth token refresh, and secret ref resolvers — had **no timeout boundary**. In a non-TTY subprocess, any of these can block indefinitely.

## Changes

### `src/infra/provider-usage.load.ts`
- Wrapped the `resolveProviderAuths()` call at line 94 in `withTimeout(…, timeoutMs, [])` using the same timeout budget as the fetch phase
- On timeout, returns `[]` (empty auths) which causes `loadProviderUsageSummary` to return `{ providers: [] }` instead of hanging

### `src/infra/provider-usage.load.test.ts`
- Added mock for `resolveProviderAuths` to support testing the timeout path
- Added test: "returns empty providers when auth resolution exceeds timeout" — mocks auth resolution as a promise that never settles, verifies the function resolves within the timeout

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: non-TTY `status --usage --json` should complete instead of hanging while provider usage auth is resolved.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout on branch `fix/status-json-usage-auth-timeout-74085`, head `9852b6356b6a8369bc4f9e19b71745466d6103c5`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```text
node -e '<script uses child_process.spawnSync("node", ["scripts/run-node.mjs", "status", "--usage", "--json"], { timeout: 45000, encoding: "utf8", non-TTY stdio capture }) and prints only exit status, elapsed time, JSON parse status, top-level keys, and usage summary shape>'
```

Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "branch": "fix/status-json-usage-auth-timeout-74085",
  "head": "9852b6356b6a8369bc4f9e19b71745466d6103c5",

    "updateChannelSource",

  ],
  "hasUsage": true,
  "usageKeys": [
    "providers",
    "updatedAt"
  ],
  "usageProviders": 1,
  "stderrPreview": []
}
```

Observed result after fix: The non-TTY subprocess exited successfully in about 6.5s, did not hit the 45s timeout, emitted parseable JSON on stdout, and included a top-level `usage` object with provider usage data.

What was not tested: This was a local source checkout proof rather than a packaged install, and it does not print provider names, auth profiles, credentials, or raw usage values.

Fixes #74085

target.title: fix(inworld): bound TTS audio and error response body reads to prevent OOM
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

This exists to stop Inworld's externally controlled streaming TTS and error responses from buffering attacker-sized bodies into memory. The Inworld streaming TTS endpoint read its response with an unbounded `await response.text()` (`extensions/inworld/tts.ts:97`) before splitting newline-delimited JSON and concatenating base64 audio chunks, so a controlled or hijacked endpoint could stream an arbitrary body and force OpenClaw to buffer it. The non-OK error body (`extensions/inworld/tts.ts:93`) and the analogous `listInworldVoices` error path had the same unbounded-read shape.

## Changes

- Bound the main `inworldTTS` audio response with `readResponseWithLimit`, using `INWORLD_TTS_BODY_MAX_BYTES = MAX_AUDIO_BYTES * 2` (32 MiB) plus a 30s idle timeout.
- Bound Inworld error-body reads with `readResponseTextSnippet` so hostile error responses cannot inline unbounded bodies into thrown messages.
- Re-exported `readResponseTextSnippet` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/response-limit-runtime` so extensions use the existing response-limit entry instead of a new helper path.
- Added Inworld TTS regression coverage for oversized audio streams, bounded error snippets, cancellation, and normal payload decoding.

## Evidence

The earlier proof for this PR used an in-process stream fixture, so this proof was upgraded to a real terminal `node:http` check. The scratchpad proof script is local only and is not committed. It starts a real loopback CONNECT proxy because Inworld's production SSRF policy allowlists the configured hostname and does not permit a direct loopback base URL; the exported `inworldTTS` still drives the real `fetchWithSsrFGuard` / undici dispatcher path, `readResponseWithLimit`, `readResponseTextSnippet`, and a real TCP socket. No in-process `ReadableStream` fixture is used.

Command: `node --import tsx scratchpad/inworld-http-proof.mts`

```text
[proof] real node:http loopback proxy on http://127.0.0.1:33889, target=http://example.com, audioCap=33554432 bytes, audioWouldStream=134217728 bytes
PASS  oversized Inworld TTS audio stream throws bounded error :: threw=true msg="Inworld TTS audio stream too large: 33619968 bytes (limit: 33554432 bytes)"
PASS  audio stream cancelled: proxy saw socket abort before full body :: aborted=true bytesSent=35651584 (<134217728) chunks=34
PASS  audio stream cancelled near the 32 MiB cap :: bytesSent=35651584 (<67108864)
PASS  negative control: raw unbounded read buffers the full body past the 32 MiB cap :: buffered=134217728 bytes (> 33554432)
PASS  negative control consumed far more than bounded read :: unbounded buffered=134217728 vs bounded sent≈35651584
PASS  oversized error body stays bounded and cancels stream :: messageLength=430 aborted=true bytesSent=1048576 (<33554432) cap=8192
PASS  happy path: small NDJSON audio payload still parses :: audio="real-audio-bytes" bytes=16

[proof] 8 PASS, 0 FAIL
[proof] ALL PASS
```

Focused regression test:

Command: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/inworld/tts.test.ts`

```text
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       19 passed (19)
Result      [test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 24.04s
```

## Symmetry with #95108

#95108 bounded the analogous Anthropic Messages error-response read with `readResponseWithLimit` / `readResponseTextSnippet`. This PR is the symmetric fix for the Inworld TTS main audio stream plus its error bodies, reusing the same `@openclaw/media-core` helper through the `response-limit-runtime` plugin entry rather than introducing a new abstraction.

**Label**: security

---

AI-assisted: implemented and verified with AI assistance; the proof commands above were run for real against the actual code path.

target.body:
## Summary
- Reuse shared presentation URL buttons in Telegram inline keyboards.
- Render direct-chat HTTPS presentation buttons as Telegram Mini App web_app buttons.

target.title: fix(agents): harden subagent lifecycle calls against transient gateway readiness failures
target.body:
## Summary

- add a bounded `sessions.list` readiness probe before subagent spawn starts the child agent
- retry transient finalize lifecycle calls in subagent announce (`sessions.patch` / `sessions.delete`)
- retry transient sweep cleanup `sessions.delete` calls in subagent registry cleanup
- restore the missing sweep delete helper wiring on current `main`
- restore/expand focused regression coverage for sweep cleanup and announce finalize retry paths
- keep the patch narrowed to the still-missing lifecycle hardening pieces on current `main`

## Why this refreshed PR

The earlier PR branch became stale relative to current `main` because related subagent delivery/orchestration code moved substantially. This refreshed branch keeps the same bug-fix intent, but re-applies only the still-missing resilience behavior on top of current `main` with a smaller diff.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Root Cause

Several subagent lifecycle paths still treat short gateway readiness/lifecycle gaps as single-shot failures:
- spawn starts the child agent immediately, without a bounded readiness preflight
- subagent announce finalize calls swallow transient lifecycle errors instead of retrying them
- registry sweep cleanup retries only on a later sweep, rather than immediately retrying known transient lifecycle failures in bounded fashion

## Test Plan

- `OPENCLAW_TEST_FAST=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE=tmp/pr-74131-final-verify.json node --no-maglev ./node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents.config.ts`

## Results

- `src/agents/subagent-registry-cleanup.test.ts` ✅
- `src/agents/subagent-registry.test.ts` ✅
- `src/agents/subagent-spawn.test.ts` ✅
- `src/agents/subagent-announce.test.ts` ✅
- 4/4 targeted files passed
- 41/41 targeted tests passed

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Subagent lifecycle calls can now tolerate transient gateway readiness/lifecycle failures around spawn, completion announce/finalize, and registry cleanup while keeping permanent failures non-retried.

Real environment tested: Repository `openclaw/openclaw`, PR #74131 worktree `/datad/github/openclaw-pr74131-followup`, local validation commit `62dbc3f15271`, PR head `f6511ea7426f` (`fix/subagent-gateway-readiness-refresh`), on Linux using Node.js v24.14.1. The only diff between the validated local commit and PR head was `CHANGELOG.md` release-note cleanup (3 deleted lines); source and test files were unchanged. To avoid disrupting the user's production gateway, I ran the PR build against a temporary loopback gateway on port 19131 with a temporary OpenClaw HOME/workspace under `/tmp/openclaw-pr74131-proof`; channels, cron, browser control, and canvas host were disabled. The temporary gateway reused only copied provider auth profile data converted into the temporary SQLite auth store. The production gateway on 18789 was not restarted or used for this proof and was checked healthy afterward.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```shell
# Start temporary PR gateway from the PR worktree.
OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-pr74131-proof/home OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-pr74131-proof/home/openclaw.json OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-pr74131-proof/home OPENCLAW_WORKSPACE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-pr74131-proof/workspace OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1 OPENCLAW_SKIP_CRON=1 OPENCLAW_SKIP_BROWSER_CONTROL_SERVER=1 OPENCLAW_SKIP_CANVAS_HOST=1 node openclaw.mjs gateway run --port 19131 --bind loopback --auth token --token '<temp-token>' --verbose

# Run a live parent agent turn that must spawn a child, yield, receive the child completion event, then answer from the parent session.
OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-pr74131-proof/home OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-pr74131-proof/home/openclaw.json OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-pr74131-proof/home OPENCLAW_WORKSPACE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-pr74131-proof/workspace OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_URL=ws://127.0.0.1:19131 OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN='<temp-token>' node openclaw.mjs agent --json   --session-key 'agent:main:pr74131-proof-1780818361-22239'   --timeout 360   --message 'Run this exact OpenClaw subagent lifecycle proof. Use tool calls, not prose.
Step 1: call sessions_spawn with task exactly: "Reply exactly PR74131_CHILD_OK_1780818361-22239 and nothing else." Use cleanup="keep" and context="isolated".
Step 2: after sessions_spawn returns accepted, call sessions_yield to wait for the child completion event.
Step 3: after the child completion event is visible, reply exactly PR74131_PARENT_SAW_1780818361-22239 and nothing else.
Do not reply with PR74131_PARENT_SAW_1780818361-22239 before the child completion event is visible.'

# Run targeted forced-transient lifecycle tests.
OPENCLAW_TEST_FAST=1 node scripts/test-projects.mjs   src/agents/subagent-spawn.test.ts   src/agents/subagent-announce.test.ts   src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.test.ts   src/agents/subagent-registry-helpers.test.ts   src/agents/subagent-registry.test.ts
```

Evidence after fix: Redacted terminal/log evidence from the temporary PR gateway and targeted tests:
```text
2026-06-07T15:45:38.251+08:00 [gateway] ready
2026-06-07T15:46:31.898+08:00 [ws] ← connect client=gateway-client clientDisplayName=gateway:sessions.list ... auth=token
2026-06-07T15:46:32.982+08:00 [ws] ⇄ res ✓ sessions.list 445ms
2026-06-07T15:46:35.568+08:00 [diagnostic] lane dequeue: lane=subagent waitMs=1 queueSize=0
2026-06-07T15:46:37.333+08:00 [ws] → event agent ... session=subagent:<uuid> stream=assistant ... text=PR74131_CHILD_OK_1780818361-22239
2026-06-07T15:46:37.337+08:00 [ws] → event agent ... session=subagent:<uuid> stream=lifecycle ... phase=end
2026-06-07T15:46:39.793+08:00 [agent/embedded] embedded run start: runId=announce:v1:agent:main:subagent:<uuid>:<uuid> ... provider=deepseek model=deepseek-v4-flash
2026-06-07T15:46:41.499+08:00 [agent/embedded] embedded run agent end: runId=announce:v1:agent:main:subagent:<uuid>:<uuid> isError=false
2026-06-07T15:46:41.568+08:00 [agent/embedded] embedded run done: runId=announce:v1:agent:main:subagent:<uuid>:<uuid> ... aborted=false
Parent trajectory assistantTexts: ["PR74131_PARENT_SAW_1780818361-22239"]

[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.agents.config.ts
✓ agents src/agents/subagent-registry.test.ts (60 tests)
✓ agents src/agents/subagent-spawn.test.ts (23 tests)
✓ agents src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.test.ts (98 tests)
✓ agents src/agents/subagent-announce.test.ts (12 tests)
✓ agents src/agents/subagent-registry-helpers.test.ts (8 tests)
  ✓ retries up to the limit on transient errors
  ✓ throws the last transient error after exhausting retries
  ✓ retries lifecycle readiness failures shared with subagent spawn
Test Files 5 passed (5)
Tests 201 passed (201)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 55.81s

Post-check: temporary port 19131 had no remaining listener; production gateway 18789 remained running and connectivity probe was ok.
```

Observed result after fix: The live parent session performed a real `sessions_spawn` call, the PR's admin-scoped `sessions.list` readiness probe succeeded before child spawn, the child subagent completed with the expected token, the completion announce resumed the parent session, and the parent replied only after observing the child completion event. The targeted test shard also passed all 201 tests covering transient retry behavior for spawn readiness, announce/finalize delivery, and registry cleanup helpers.

What was not tested: I did not deliberately restart or kill the user's production gateway. I also did not inject real network-level gateway outages in the live temporary gateway; transient failure branches were covered by the targeted tests, while the live run validated the after-fix real subagent spawn/completion/announce lifecycle on the PR code path.

## CI notes

- `Real behavior proof` check is now passing — both live runtime proof and forced-transient lifecycle proof are in the PR body.
- No CHANGELOG entries in the PR diff — release notes are maintainer-owned.
- Remaining CI failures (if any) are infrastructure issues (actionlint binary download, corepack config, `message-action-runner.core-send.test.ts` timeout) not caused by this PR's code changes.

## Notes

- Greptile / review findings about missing helper wiring and weakened cleanup coverage are addressed in this refresh.
- Added focused announce finalize retry coverage for both `sessions.delete` and `sessions.patch` transient failure paths.
- `subagent-announce` finalize timeout assertions were updated to match the current `SUBAGENT_ANNOUNCE_SESSION_FINALIZE_TIMEOUT_MS` behavior.
- P1 fix: `isGatewayLifecycleTransientError` now excludes permanent WS close codes (1008 Policy Violation, 1003, 1007) and explicit auth denials (unauthorized, forbidden, access denied) from retry — these fail fast instead.
- P2 fix: CHANGELOG entries removed from PR branch.

## PR Self-check

- [x] helper definition/import/export chain re-checked
- [x] cleanup / finalize paths re-checked end-to-end
- [x] previously weakened regression assertion restored or replaced with equivalent protection
- [x] targeted tests added for the exact transient retry paths touched by this PR
- [x] targeted agents verification rerun after final edits

## Risks / Notes

- The lifecycle retry classifiers are still message-based, so future transient variants may need to be added explicitly.
- Retries are bounded and localized to the known gateway readiness/lifecycle paths.
- I have not treated full-repo heavy gates as complete for this patch; this update is grounded on focused path-level verification.

## Supersedes / Related

- Related to / refreshes the intent of #70434

target.title: fix(agents): warm context-window cache for the gateway daemon
target.body:
## What

The long-running gateway daemon never warms the context-window cache, so synchronous lookups on the status path return `DEFAULT_CONTEXT_TOKENS = 200_000` until the first agent run completes. That value then gets persisted onto the session record and ratchets every subsequent model switch to the wrong context window.

## How to reproduce

1. Configure `agents.defaults.models["github-copilot/claude-opus-4.6-1m"] = { alias: "opus1m" }` (Copilot's `/models` advertises 1,000,000 input tokens for this id).
2. Restart the gateway daemon.
3. In a fresh session, run `📊 session_status` immediately. It reports `Context: …/200k` instead of `…/1000k`.
4. Switch to `claude-opus-4.6` (`/model opus`) - it also reports 200K, even though pi-ai's catalog has it at 1M.
5. Switch back to `claude-opus-4.6-1m` - still 200K.

The 200K comes from `lookupContextTokens(...) ?? DEFAULT_CONTEXT_TOKENS` in `getSessionDefaults` and the equivalent in `status-message.ts`. The persisted value is then read by subsequent status calls via `entry.contextTokens`, so the wrong window sticks until something explicitly clears or overwrites it.

## Root cause

The gateway daemon launches via systemd as:

```
ExecStart=/usr/bin/node /home/.../dist/index.js gateway --port 18789
```

Which means `argv[0]="node"`, `argv[1]="/home/.../dist/index.js"`, `argv[2]="gateway"`.

Two things conspire to disable eager cache warmup for the daemon:

1. `isLikelyOpenClawCliProcess` only matches `argv[1]` basenames `openclaw`, `openclaw.mjs`, `entry.js`, `entry.mjs`. The bundled `dist/index.js` entry is not recognised, so the function returns `false`.
2. `SKIP_EAGER_WARMUP_PRIMARY_COMMANDS` includes `"gateway"`. Even when the recognition above is fixed, the primary command `gateway` would still be skipped.

So `shouldEagerWarmContextWindowCache()` returns `false` for the daemon and the cache load is never kicked off at startup. Every `allowAsyncLoad: false` lookup returns `undefined` until the first agent run happens to warm the cache through some other path - by which point the 200K fallback has already been persisted onto the session record.

## Fix

- Recognise `index.js` / `index.mjs` as legitimate OpenClaw entry basenames in `isLikelyOpenClawCliProcess`.
- Drop `gateway` from `SKIP_EAGER_WARMUP_PRIMARY_COMMANDS`. The warmup is async and best-effort, so short-lived `openclaw gateway status` / `gateway restart` CLI invocations don't pay any blocking cost.

I left a comment block in the skip list explaining why `gateway` must not return - this is the kind of thing that's easy to add back without realising the daemon depends on it.

## Test plan

- New unit tests in `src/agents/context.test.ts` cover:
  - the gateway daemon argv shape (`node dist/index.js gateway --port ...`)
  - the CLI `gateway status` shape
  - help/version invocations (still skipped)
  - generic plugin-sdk-style imports from random Node scripts (still skipped, since `index.js` is too generic to match without an OpenClaw-specific path)
- Manually verified locally: after rebuilding and restarting the gateway, `📊 session_status` reports the correct context window for each Copilot Claude variant on a fresh session, and switching between models (`/model opus`, `/model opus1m`, `/model opus47`) updates the displayed window correctly instead of pinning at 200K.

## Notes

There's a separate, narrower issue in `applyDiscoveredContextWindows` where the cache stores bare model ids and uses min-of-all-providers. That can still under-report when the same bare id appears under both `anthropic` (200K) and `github-copilot` (1M) - but with the daemon warmup fixed, the qualified-then-bare lookup chain in `resolveContextTokensForModel` finds the right value first for the cases I've seen. Happy to follow up with a per-provider key if maintainers want.

target.title: fix(mcp): reject mixed command+url transport config (#72111)
target.body:
Closes #72111.

## Problem

When an MCP server config carries **both** a non-empty `command` and a non-empty `url` (e.g. a streamable-http endpoint plus a `command`/`args` pair), the runtime silently picks stdio and drops the HTTP transport. The user sees:

```
MCP server connection timed out after 30000ms
```

The spawned child never speaks JSON-RPC over stdio because the user actually wanted the streamable-http endpoint. The clawsweeper bot triaged this and recommended early rejection over silent fallback.

## Root cause

`resolveMcpTransportConfig` in `src/agents/mcp-transport-config.ts` resolves the stdio launch first when `command` is set, regardless of whether `url` is also set. The 30s timeout that follows is the user-visible symptom; the silent fallback at config-resolution time is the real bug.

## Reproducer

In `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json`:

```json
{
  \"mcp\": {
    \"servers\": {
      \"broken\": {
        \"command\": \"node\",
        \"args\": [\"something.js\"],
        \"url\": \"https://my-mcp.example.com/mcp\"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

Run OpenClaw → the `broken` server hangs, then errors with `MCP server connection timed out after 30000ms`. There is no clue in the logs about *which* transport was actually attempted.

## Fix

Reject the ambiguous shape early with a clear diagnostic that names the offending server and the requested transport hint, matching the file's existing \"skipped server X because Y\" diagnostic style and the clawsweeper bot's \"Best possible solution\":

> reject mixed `command` + `url`/HTTP transport configs early with a clear diagnostic and docs/test alignment. The current silent stdio precedence should not remain the user-visible timeout path.

Implementation: when `command` and a non-whitespace `url` are both present, log the warning, return `null` (caller already handles `null` as \"skip this server\"), and let the user fix their config.

Pure stdio configs (no `url`) and pure HTTP configs (no `command`) are unaffected. **Whitespace-only `url`** strings continue to be treated as \"no url\" so stdio still wins, matching the existing trimming in `mcp-stdio.ts`.

Diff: **+17** lines in `mcp-transport-config.ts`, **+52** in tests.

## Verification

```
pnpm vitest run src/agents/mcp-transport-config.test.ts
# 11 passed (7 original + 4 new)

pnpm check
# 0 errors, 0 warnings; all policy guards green
```

## Tests added (4 new)

1. **Mixed config + transport hint** → resolution returns `null`; warning lists both `command` and `url` and the requested transport.
2. **Mixed config without explicit transport hint** → still rejected; warning is informative.
3. **Whitespace-only `url` paired with real `command`** → resolution returns stdio (mirrors `mcp-stdio.ts` trimming).
4. **Sanitized transport in warning** → diagnostic does not leak full URL secrets / query strings.

## Risk notes

- **Behavior change is user-visible**: configs that used to silently fall back to stdio when both `command`+`url` were set now get rejected outright (server skipped, no spawn). That is the bot-blessed shape and matches the issue, but anyone relying on the silent fallback (unlikely — it was the bug) will see a behavior shift on upgrade.
- Did NOT touch `src/agents/mcp-stdio.ts` even though the bot's evidence cites it; the rejection is cleaner at the transport-config layer where both fields are visible together.
- PR collision check: 8 open MCP-related PRs in the repo (#66542, #69417, #69039, #65574, #62134, #64316, #62160, #60005); none touch `mcp-transport-config.ts` validation logic for the mixed shape. Safe to land independently.

target.title: fix(signal): enable inbound status reactions
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2-5 bullets:

- Problem: Signal has reaction send helpers, but inbound runs never wired `messages.statusReactions.enabled` into the shared status reaction lifecycle.
- Why it matters: Signal users get no queued/thinking/tool/compacting/done/error visual feedback during long-running agent turns even when status reactions are configured.
- What changed: Create a Signal status reaction controller for eligible inbound messages, route lifecycle callbacks through `dispatchInboundMessage`, and send reactions via `sendReactionSignal` for direct and group messages.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No config shape changes, no Signal REST/native transport changes, and no agent/tool message-action behavior changes.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #72020
- Related #20393
- Related #65919
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Signal's inbound handler dispatched messages without creating a `StatusReactionController` or passing lifecycle callbacks (`onReplyStart`, `onToolStart`, compaction callbacks, terminal state handling) into `dispatchInboundMessage`.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Signal had send-reaction helper tests, but no inbound regression test for `messages.statusReactions.enabled`.
- Contributing context (if known): Other channels already wire the shared status reaction controller; Signal had the transport primitive but not the inbound lifecycle wiring.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/signal/src/monitor/event-handler.status-reactions.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Signal direct and group inbound messages with `messages.statusReactions.enabled` send queued and done reactions through the correct direct recipient or groupId/targetAuthor route.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: It exercises the Signal inbound handler, dispatch callback wiring, and Signal reaction transport seam without needing a live Signal daemon.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Signal now honors `messages.statusReactions.enabled` for inbound runs by updating reactions on the triggering message during the agent lifecycle.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
Signal inbound -> dispatchInboundMessage -> no status reaction callbacks -> no lifecycle reactions

After:
Signal inbound -> StatusReactionController -> dispatch lifecycle callbacks -> sendReactionSignal -> queued/done status reactions
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux / Ubuntu 24.04
- Runtime/container: Node 22 + pnpm checkout
- Model/provider: N/A for regression test
- Integration/channel (if any): Signal
- Relevant config (redacted):

```json
{
  "messages": {
    "ackReaction": "👀",
    "ackReactionScope": "all",
    "statusReactions": {
      "enabled": true
    }
  },
  "channels": {
    "signal": {
      "dmPolicy": "open",
      "allowFrom": ["*"]
    }
  }
}
```

### Steps

1. Configure Signal with `messages.statusReactions.enabled: true`.
2. Send a Signal DM or allowed Signal group message that triggers an agent run.
3. Observe reactions on the inbound message during processing and completion.

### Expected

- Signal sends lifecycle status reactions such as queued/ack and done (plus intermediate thinking/tool/compacting when those callbacks fire).

### Actual

- Before this fix, Signal sent no automatic lifecycle status reactions because the inbound dispatch path did not wire status reaction callbacks.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Targeted verification:

- `pnpm test extensions/signal/src/monitor/event-handler.status-reactions.test.ts`
- `pnpm lint:extensions`

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: Direct Signal inbound status reaction routing and group Signal inbound status reaction routing through the new regression test.
- Edge cases checked: Direct recipient uses sender recipient; group reaction uses empty recipient plus `groupId` and `targetAuthor`.
- What you did **not** verify: Live Signal daemon/container end-to-end reaction delivery.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Signal reaction API failures could add noisy logs during long runs.
  - Mitigation: Reaction send failures are caught and logged through existing verbose logging; they do not fail message dispatch.

target.title: feat: Rust/GTK4 Linux companion app (#75, alternative track)
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds a native Linux desktop companion under `apps/linux-gtk/` written in **Rust + GTK4 + libadwaita**
- Alternative-track contribution for [#75](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/75) alongside @tiagonix's C/GTK4 work at `apps/linux/` — different language, zero path conflicts, same gateway protocol
- Steady base, early. Needs security review, packaging, and UI polish

## Why another one

I started on a prototype using web-tech (Electron) before reading through #75, then saw [@steipete's comment](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/75#issuecomment-3747113141):

> My vision for this is a cute gtk-native Linux app. Esp for the statusbar features.

Switched tracks to native GTK. @tiagonix's C implementation was the reference, but I wanted to see what the Rust ecosystem gives us here (borrow-checked gateway client, fewer NULL pointer surprises, tokio for async RPC, `cargo test` for unit coverage).

Happy to fold it in, leave it as a community showcase, or archive it — whichever serves the project.

## What's included

**Library crate** (`crates/gateway-client/`, no GTK dependency):
- Async tokio + tungstenite WebSocket client with auto-reconnect
- Typed request/event frames (serde_json-backed)
- Device identity with ed25519 challenge signing
- Env > config-file > defaults resolution for URL / token / TLS
- 24 unit tests

**GTK4 app** (`crates/app/`):
- AdwOverlaySplitView main window with responsive sidebar (collapses < 600sp)
- Bridge layer pumping tokio events into GLib main loop via async-channel
- 17 views: Chat, Overview, Agents, Channels, Sessions, Skills, Usage, Cron, Retros, Workflows, Instances, Logs, Config, Settings, Debug, Control Room, About
- **Chat**: live send/receive with Markdown rendering, per-session model picker (`sessions.patch`), typing indicator, thinking/tool-call toggles, history via `chat.history`
- **Agents**: master/detail with editable settings (`agents.update`) and workspace files (`agents.files.list`)
- Snapshot refresh pattern: parallel `agents.list` / `sessions.list` / `models.list` / `channels.status` RPCs on connect
- 42 unit tests

**Supporting files**: .desktop, .metainfo.xml, SVG icon, CSS (orange accent + libadwaita theming), README with per-distro build instructions

## Known gaps

- **Security review needed** — device identity handling, TLS verification flow, token persistence
- No packaging yet (Flatpak/deb/AppImage) — build-from-source only
- Tool-call rendering is basic (JSON in collapsible blocks)
- No system tray on tiling WMs (StatusNotifierWatcher absent) — fails gracefully
- Config editor is read-only
- Polling timers could be replaced with event-driven updates

## Test plan

- [x] `cargo build --release` — zero warnings
- [x] `cargo test --release` — 66 tests pass (24 gateway-client + 42 app)
- [x] Smoke-tested against gateway v2026.4.3 on Arch Linux (bspwm/X11, Cairo renderer)
- [ ] Test on Wayland / GNOME Shell
- [ ] Test on Ubuntu 24.04 with stock GTK4/libadwaita packages
- [ ] Security audit of device identity + TLS flow

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
