target.title: fix(openrouter): bound media response reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves
Success-path untrusted OpenRouter image/video response bodies used unbounded reads, which could OOM or hang the process if a provider streamed a large JSON body without Content-Length.

## Changes
- Replace the OpenRouter image success-path `response.json()` read with shared `readProviderJsonResponse` bounded parsing.
- Replace the OpenRouter video submit/poll success-path JSON read with shared `readProviderJsonResponse`, while preserving the existing malformed-response error for invalid JSON.
- Let OpenRouter video use the existing provider request config for private-network allowance instead of forcing the resolved request config to `false`; the default remains denied through the shared config helper.
- Add focused OpenRouter image/video tests for the bounded helper path and oversized submit-body cancellation.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed
- OpenRouter image and video success-path JSON responses now stop at the shared 16MiB cap and abort the streaming TCP response when exceeded.

Real environment tested
- Local Node.js v22.22.0 process using `node:http` loopback TCP servers, no `Content-Length`, true `fetch`, and the real exported OpenRouter image/video provider builders.

Exact steps
- `node --import tsx scratchpad/openrouter-bound-proof.mjs`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/openrouter/image-generation-provider.test.ts extensions/openrouter/video-generation-provider.test.ts extensions/openrouter/index.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.extensions.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions.tsbuildinfo`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test.tsbuildinfo`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base upstream/main`

Observed result
- Bounded OpenRouter image/video success JSON throws `exceeds 16777216 bytes`, aborts the server socket, and sends fewer bytes than the full oversized body.
- Raw `response.json()` negative control buffers the full oversized body.
- Small OpenRouter image/video JSON responses parse normally.

What was not tested
- Live OpenRouter API calls.
- The optional OpenRouter generation-cost lookup was not changed in this PR.

## Evidence
```text
$ node --import tsx scratchpad/openrouter-bound-proof.mjs
PASS openrouter image success JSON: threw "OpenRouter image generation: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes"; socket abort=true; bytesSent=16842764; full=17825792
PASS openrouter video submit success JSON: threw "OpenRouter video generation: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes"; socket abort=true; bytesSent=16842764; full=17825792
PASS negative control: raw response.json() buffered 17825792 bytes; bytesSent=17825806; cap=16777216
PASS happy path: OpenRouter image small JSON parsed normally
PASS happy path: OpenRouter video small JSON parsed normally
PASS openrouter node:http bounded success-path proof complete

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/openrouter/image-generation-provider.test.ts extensions/openrouter/video-generation-provider.test.ts extensions/openrouter/index.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose
Test Files  3 passed (3)
Tests  67 passed (67)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 128.39s

$ node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.extensions.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions.tsbuildinfo
(exit code 0)

$ node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test.tsbuildinfo
(exit code 0)

$ .agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base upstream/main
autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported
overall: patch is correct (0.86)
```

**Label**: security
_AI-assisted._

target.title: [codex] Update Claude CLI billing docs
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

OpenClaw's Anthropic provider docs still described the previously announced June 15, 2026 Claude Agent SDK / `claude -p` billing change as active. Anthropic's current support article now says that change is paused, so the old wording could mislead users about whether `claude -p` draws from a separate Agent SDK credit or from their subscription usage limits.

## Why This Change Was Made

This updates the Claude CLI billing guidance to match Anthropic's current public support language:

- Anthropic's Agent SDK plan article says the June 15 change is paused and that Claude Agent SDK, `claude -p`, and third-party app usage still draw from subscription usage limits for now: https://support.claude.com/en/articles/15036540-use-the-claude-agent-sdk-with-your-claude-plan
- Anthropic's Pro/Max Claude Code article documents subscription-backed Claude Code usage and plan-limit behavior: https://support.claude.com/en/articles/11145838-use-claude-code-with-your-pro-or-max-plan
- Anthropic's Team/Enterprise Claude Code article documents Team and Enterprise subscription behavior: https://support.claude.com/en/articles/11845131-use-claude-code-with-your-team-or-enterprise-plan
- Claude Code's CLI reference still documents `claude -p` as SDK-style non-interactive usage: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/cli-reference

The docs now point users back to Anthropic's support articles before they rely on subscription billing behavior.

## User Impact

Users evaluating Anthropic API keys, Claude CLI reuse, or Claude subscription proxy workflows get current billing guidance and direct official references. The recommendation to use API keys for predictable production or shared automation remains unchanged.

## Evidence

- `corepack pnpm docs:list`
- `corepack pnpm format:docs:check`
- `corepack pnpm docs:check-mdx`
- `git diff --check`
- `rg -n "Starting June 15|monthly Agent SDK credit first|no longer draws from normal Claude|separate monthly Agent SDK credit" docs -S` returned no matches

target.title: fix(tui): avoid duplicate assistant reply after history replay
target.body:
Closes #96967

## Summary

- Deduplicates the narrow TUI ordering where an assistant message replayed from history is immediately followed by the matching live final event for the same visible text.
- Keeps the existing `sessions.changed` `new`/`reset` reload behavior intact so external session refreshes still rebuild the current session.
- Adds focused `ChatLog` unit coverage for the replay/live duplicate case and the adjacent cases that must still render duplicate text.

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where users viewing the TUI could see the same assistant reply twice when a Telegram-originated turn caused history replay and the live final message to meet in the current chat log.

## Root Cause

History replay renders assistant messages without a run id, while live final events render with a run id. The existing run-id dedupe cannot relate those two paths, so a replayed assistant row and its matching live final can be appended as separate visible rows when the events arrive adjacent to each other.

## Why This Change Was Made

The fix is scoped to `ChatLog`: it records only the most recent no-run-id assistant row from history replay and suppresses a live final with a run id only when it immediately follows that replayed row, has identical text, and arrives within a short TTL. The marker is cleared when other content is appended, the log is cleared, or the tracked component is pruned, so intentional repeated messages remain visible.

This avoids a broader event-handler change and preserves the `sessions.changed` reload path needed for external session `new`/`reset` updates.

## User Impact

TUI users should no longer see a duplicated assistant reply for the reported replay/live-final interleaving. Legitimate repeated assistant output remains visible when separated by user content or when both messages are live finals.

## Validation / Pre-checks

- `git diff --check` — passed as a local pre-check.
- `git diff --cached --check` — passed before committing.
- Targeted Vitest command requested for this PR was not run locally because dependencies are not installed in this checkout (`node_modules` is absent), and no dependency install was performed for the local pre-check.
- GitHub Actions validation is expected to run on this PR after creation.

## Evidence

Focused tests added in `src/tui/components/chat-log.test.ts` cover:

- suppressing an adjacent no-run-id history replay followed by a matching live final with a run id;
- allowing the same text when a user row separates replay and live final;
- allowing two same-text live finals when neither came from history replay.

## Evidence source map

- Issue report: #96967 describes duplicate TUI assistant output after a Telegram-triggered turn.
- History replay source: `src/tui/tui-session-actions.ts` rebuilds assistant history via `chatLog.finalizeAssistant(text)` without a run id.
- Live final source: `src/tui/tui-event-handlers.ts` final chat events render via `chatLog.finalizeAssistant(finalText, evt.runId)`.
- Fix surface: `src/tui/components/chat-log.ts` now dedupes only the adjacent history-replay/live-final pair.
- Regression tests: `src/tui/components/chat-log.test.ts` covers the suppression and allowed-duplicate boundaries.

## Risk / Rollback / Docs

- Risk: low-to-medium. The change affects assistant row append behavior, but only for identical adjacent replay/live final text within the short TTL.
- Rollback: revert this commit to restore the previous append behavior.
- Docs: no user-facing docs changed; this is a TUI rendering fix.

## Follow-up proof added after ClawSweeper review

### Terminal behavior proof: sessions.changed replay + live final leaves one visible assistant row

A local, dependency-free Node 24 harness was run against this branch at `6acf71618376655a80a6f2f1104a256cd3e14c20`. The harness loads the PR's `src/tui/components/chat-log.ts` source into a temporary module with minimal rendering stubs only, then exercises the same TUI boundary ordering described in the issue:

1. history replay path (`tui-session-actions.ts`) calls `chatLog.finalizeAssistant(text)` with **no run id**;
2. live final path (`tui-event-handlers.ts`) calls `chatLog.finalizeAssistant(text, runId)` with a **run id**;
3. visible rendered assistant rows are counted after both events.

Output:

```text

visible assistant rows after live final: 1

render="redacted assistant final from replay/live ordering"

PASS exactly one visible assistant row for replay+live final, while intentional duplicates remain visible
```

This avoids external messaging and does not mutate any active user/Gateway/Telegram/TUI configuration. A true native Telegram/TUI visual capture would require maintainer-controlled Mantis/Telegram desktop infrastructure; the useful maintainer trigger remains:

```text
@openclaw-mantis visual task: verify openclaw-tui shows exactly one assistant reply after a Telegram-originated message triggers sessions.changed history replay.
```

### CI / check evidence

- Full GitHub Actions CI for this head SHA passed: run https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/28225940515 (`6acf71618376655a80a6f2f1104a256cd3e14c20`).
- CI TUI PTY lane passed in job https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/28225940515/job/83618175004: `test/vitest/vitest.tui-pty.config.ts` started at 08:19:17 UTC and passed `src/tui/tui-pty-harness.e2e.test.ts` plus `src/tui/tui-pty-local.e2e.test.ts`.
- The repository's `Real behavior proof` workflow check passed in run https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/28226954824 with log line `External PR includes problem context and evidence.`
- No existing CI log found an explicit run of `src/tui/components/chat-log.test.ts`; the local targeted Vitest command below could not run because this checkout intentionally has no installed dependencies.

```text
...

```

No dependency install was performed.

### OpenGrep changed paths diagnosis

The failed OpenGrep PR-diff check did not reach repository scanning. It failed while installing OpenGrep, before the changed-path scan step:


target.title: fix(discord): disable ws 8.21.0 receiver part limits for gateway sockets
target.body:
## Summary

ws 8.21.0 introduced `maxBufferedChunks` and `maxFragments` receiver limits (commit `[security] Limit retained message parts`) that can trigger `WS_ERR_TOO_MANY_BUFFERED_PARTS` and close connections with code 1008 under certain traffic patterns.

This PR explicitly sets both limits to `0` (disabled) for Discord gateway WebSocket connections, restoring the ws 8.20.0 behavior for the trusted Discord gateway peer while preserving the security fix for all other WebSocket usage.

## Changes

- **`extensions/discord/src/internal/gateway.ts`**: Base class `createWebSocket()` now passes `maxBufferedChunks: 0, maxFragments: 0` with a `ws.ClientOptions` cast (since `@types/ws` 8.18.1 does not yet expose these fields).
- **`extensions/discord/src/monitor/gateway-plugin.ts`**: Extended `DiscordGatewayWebSocketCtor` type to include the new options; monitor gateway creation path passes the same disabled limits.
- **`extensions/discord/src/internal/gateway.test.ts`**: Added test verifying base class WebSocket receives the expected options.
- **`extensions/discord/src/monitor/gateway-plugin.test.ts`**: Added test verifying monitor plugin WebSocket receives the expected options.
- **`extensions/discord/src/monitor/provider.proxy.test.ts`**: Updated two existing exact-match assertions to include the new options.

## Why `0` instead of raising the limits

- ws 8.21.0 treats `0` as disabled (`receiver.js:61`, `receiver.js:98`: `if (this._maxBufferedChunks > 0 && ...)`), matching the pre-8.21.0 behavior exactly.
- Discord gateway is a trusted peer; the security limits are designed to protect against untrusted senders.
- Choosing an arbitrary higher threshold would be fragile without real production traffic data to calibrate against.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: ws 8.21.0's retained-part receiver limits (`maxFragments` default 131072 / `maxBufferedChunks` default 1048576) abort a Discord gateway socket with `WS_ERR_TOO_MANY_BUFFERED_PARTS` / close `1008`; this PR sets both to `0` (disabled) on the Discord gateway sockets, restoring ws 8.20.0 behavior for that trusted peer.
- **Real environment tested**: Live Discord gateway connection from a Docker `node:22-bookworm-slim` container (Node `v22.22.3`) with the repo-pinned `ws@8.21.0` installed exactly, using a real bot token (redacted), connecting to `wss://gateway.discord.gg/?v=10&encoding=json`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: build a `node:22-bookworm-slim` image with `npm install ws@8.21.0`, then run a client that constructs the gateway socket with this patch's options (`new ws.WebSocket(url, { handshakeTimeout: 30000, maxBufferedChunks: 0, maxFragments: 0 })`), completes the HELLO/IDENTIFY/READY handshake against the real Discord gateway, sends heartbeats, holds a 60s stable window, then closes with code 1000.
- **Evidence after fix**: Live Discord gateway run with this patch's options applied (token, `session_id`, and `resume_gateway_url` redacted):

```
[env] node=v22.22.3 ws=8.21.0
[discord] connecting wss://gateway.discord.gg (maxBufferedChunks:0, maxFragments:0)
[discord] open
[discord] HELLO heartbeat_interval=41250ms
[discord] IDENTIFY sent (token redacted)
[discord] READY received (session_id/resume_gateway_url redacted)
[discord] HEARTBEAT_ACK #1
[discord] stable window 60000ms complete
[discord] close code=1000 reason=<empty>
[result] PASS live: READY + 1 heartbeat ACK; no WS_ERR_TOO_MANY_BUFFERED_PARTS during stable window
```

Supplemental deterministic mechanism repro against a local ws 8.21.0 gateway streaming one message in 131073 fragments (one over the client default `maxFragments`), exercising the same `ws.WebSocket` receiver path:

```
# default ws.WebSocket (ws 8.21 defaults)
[before] open
[before] error code=WS_ERR_TOO_MANY_BUFFERED_PARTS message=Too many message fragments
[before] close code=1006 reason=<empty>

# this patch's options applied
[after] open
[after] message received: 131073 bytes - NO receiver-limit close
```

- **Observed result after fix**: On the real Discord gateway, with the disabled limits applied, the connection reaches `READY`, exchanges heartbeats, and stays connected through the 60s window with no `WS_ERR_TOO_MANY_BUFFERED_PARTS` and no `1008` close; the only close is the intentional client-initiated `1000`. The deterministic repro confirms the same constructor options remove the receiver-limit close that the default ws 8.21 client hits.
- **What was not tested**: the live run confirms a real gateway session stays healthy with the patched options, but does not force Discord's production fragmentation pattern on demand, does not exercise the `zlib-stream` transport, and does not drive the full OpenClaw plugin wiring end-to-end. The deterministic repro proves the ws 8.21 retained-part mechanism and that this patch's options disable the exact receiver checks, but uses a local peer rather than Discord's live framing.

## Tests

- `gateway.test.ts`: 25 passed
- `gateway-plugin.test.ts`: 17 passed  
- `provider.proxy.test.ts`: 21 passed
- TypeScript check (tsgo): clean

Fixes #88330

target.title: fix(session-memory): strip orphan plain-text role lines from session-memory transcripts
target.body:
Related to #69943.

## What Problem This Solves

The session-memory sanitizer (PR #95791) strips `<|im_end|>`, `<tool_call>` XML, `<system>…</system>` blocks, `<media:…>` placeholders, and NO_REPLY markers — but does not remove plain-text orphan role-token lines: a line whose sole content is `user`, `assistant`, or `system`. These lines leak from quantized-local-model SSE streams into the assistant content channel, are persisted verbatim under "## Conversation Summary" in daily memory files, and are re-injected as context on the next `/new`. The model interprets the embedded role markers as active chat-template scaffolding and emits more malformed output, creating a self-reinforcing poisoning loop. Observed across 5 of 6 active agent workspaces in the reporter's deployment over 4 days.

ClawSweeper confirmed the gap on current main: "Plain orphan role-marker lines still pass through: the current sanitizer replacement set has no rule for newline-delimited plain role markers such as a line containing only user or assistant."

## Why This Change Was Made

`sanitizeSessionMemoryTranscriptText()` already has a six-step pipeline: model special token replacement → tool-call XML block removal → XML role directive block removal → XML role tag removal → media placeholder removal → trailing NO_REPLY removal. But plain-text role lines like `\nuser\n` or `\nassistant:\n` do not match any XML-based regex. The natural fix is a `/^...$/m` line-anchored regex appended to the same pipeline, so every caller of `getRecentSessionContent` (and thus the handler write path) benefits without needing changes in `handler.ts`.

## Root Cause

`SESSION_MEMORY_ROLE_DIRECTIVE_TAG_RE = /<\/?(?:system|assistant|user)\b[^>]*>/gi` only matches XML angle-bracket tags. A plain-text `user` on its own line has no angle brackets, so it passes through untouched. The `/^...$/m` regex fills this specific gap without changing any other sanitizer behavior.

## User Impact

Users on quantized local models (Qwen3 family, and any provider that occasionally leaks chat-template tokens into the content channel) will no longer experience the progressive degradation loop where:
1. SSE parser leaks role-token lines into assistant content
2. Session-memory hook persists them verbatim
3. Next `/new` re-injects them as context
4. Model emits more malformed output → saved again → loop
5. Agent becomes functionally non-responsive (NO_REPLY only)

## Evidence

**Behavior addressed**: Orphan plain-text role-token lines (`user`, `assistant`, `system` on their own line) are now stripped by the session-memory sanitizer before persistence.

**Real environment tested**: Linux 4.19.112, Node v22.11.0, branch `fix/session-memory-orphan-roles-69943` (rebased on upstream/main @ c05d0d5bbf).

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```console
$ npx vitest run src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/ --run

$ node --import tsx -e "
import { getRecentSessionContentWithResetFallback } from
  './src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/transcript.js';
const sessionFile = '$HOME/.openclaw/workspace-proof-69943/sessions/proof-session.jsonl';
const memoryContent = await getRecentSessionContentWithResetFallback(sessionFile, 15);
console.log(memoryContent);
"
```

**Evidence after fix** — BEFORE/AFTER comparison:

```
BEFORE FIX (old sanitizer, upstream/main):
INPUT:  "user\nVisible log output\nassistant\nIncomplete\nsystem\nShutting down"
OUTPUT: "user\nVisible log output\nassistant\nIncomplete\nsystem\nShutting down"
❌ Orphan role lines ["user", "assistant", "system"] persist in output
   → Written to memory file → re-injected → self-poisoning loop

AFTER FIX (new sanitizer with ORPHAN_ROLE_LINE_RE):
INPUT:  (same poisoned session JSONL through production code path)
OUTPUT:
  user: What is the server status?
  assistant: Visible log output

  Incomplete

  Shutting down
  user: Tell me more
  assistant: Here is more information about the system.
✅ No orphan role lines — loop is broken
✅ Content preserved: "Visible log output", "Incomplete", "Shutting down"
✅ Embedded "system" in "Here is more information about the system." preserved
```

```
$ npx vitest run src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/ --run
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  29 passed (29)
```

Input → output matrix:

| Input pattern | Sanitizer behavior |
|---|---|
| `user` (bare role word alone on a line) | Line removed |
| `assistant:` (role word + colon alone on a line) | Line removed |
| `assistant: Visible summary` (role word + colon + content) | Preserved |
| `The user submitted a form` (embedded in sentence) | Preserved |
| `user\nassistant\nsystem` (entire message is orphan role lines) | Returns null → message skipped |
| `<system>…</system>` (XML directive) | Already handled by existing rules |

**Observed result after fix**: Orphan plain-text role lines are stripped at the persistence boundary. Embedded role words in normal prose are not falsely removed. Pure role-line input returns null (message skipped entirely). The self-reinforcing poisoning loop is broken at the persistence leg.

**What was not tested**: Live quantized-local-model SSE stream (no quantized backend available). Cross-agent multi-cycle propagation in a running gateway. Recovery of already-poisoned memory files (out of scope — this prevents NEW poison).

## Regression Test Plan

```console
$ npx vitest run src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/ --run
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  29 passed (29)

$ node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/transcript.ts
(exit 0)
```

## Changes

- `src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/transcript.ts`: +9 lines — `SESSION_MEMORY_ORPHAN_ROLE_LINE_RE` regex definition + one `.replace()` pipeline step
- `src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/transcript.test.ts`: +50 lines — 2 test cases (direct function: 5 assertions + integrated transcript: 7 assertions)

target.title: fix: prevent memory flush failure from aborting user reply (#85645)
target.body:
## Summary

When `memoryFlush` fails during the reply pipeline (provider timeout, rate limit, network error), the visible error payload was treated as the final reply via `replyOperation.fail("run_failed", ...)`. This aborted the user's actual message. Since the session remained over threshold, every subsequent message triggered the same flush failure, creating a permanent death loop where no real replies could be delivered.

## Changes

**`src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts`**
- Memory flush visible error payloads are now logged via `logVerbose()` and **not** delivered as the reply. The main reply run proceeds normally.
- On flush exhaustion (`memoryFlushFailureCount` crosses `MAX_FLUSH_FAILURES` during the current turn), the bloated session is automatically rotated via `resetReplyRunSession()`. The old transcript file is **preserved on disk** (`cleanupTranscripts: false`) — rotation alone breaks the death loop (the new session no longer hits the bloated transcript), and keeping the old JSONL leaves it available for forensics/recovery. The active session only replays recent turns regardless of this flag.
- Added an **opt-in, non-terminal degraded notice**: when `agents.defaults.compaction.notifyUser` is enabled, flush exhaustion surfaces a short heads-up to the user via the existing `onBlockReply` bypass (same mechanism as `sendCompactionNotice`). It **never** calls `replyOperation.fail()` and **never** aborts the reply. Default (`notifyUser=false`) keeps the silent log-and-continue behavior. No new config surface is added.
- The `resetSession` / `resetSessionAfterRoleOrderingConflict` helpers are hoisted earlier in the function so they are available before the memory flush phase. No behavioral change to their existing callers; the role-ordering reset path keeps its original `cleanupTranscripts: true`.

**`src/auto-reply/reply/session.ts`**
- `initSessionState()` now clears `memoryFlushFailureCount`, `memoryFlushLastFailedAt`, and `memoryFlushLastFailureError` when a session is reset (e.g., via `/new`), preventing stale failure state from carrying over.

**`src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-session-reset.ts`**
- `resetReplyRunSession()` now clears `compactionCount` (reset to 0) and all `memoryFlush*` fields, ensuring a cleanly rotated session has no lingering flush state.

## What is NOT changed

- `runMemoryFlushIfNeeded` behavior is unchanged — it still tracks failures, increments `memoryFlushFailureCount`, and emits lifecycle events (`memory_flush_failed`, `memory_flush_exhausted`).
- `fallbacksOverride: []` is preserved — flush operations still use a fixed model/provider with no fallback chain.
- `MAX_FLUSH_FAILURES = 3` threshold is unchanged.
- No new configuration key is introduced — the opt-in notice reuses the existing `compaction.notifyUser` gate.

## Tests

- **`agent-runner-direct-runtime-config.test.ts`**: Verifies flush errors are logged and the main reply continues; verifies session rotation on flush exhaustion now uses `cleanupTranscripts: false`; covers the opt-in notice (fires on exhaustion when `notifyUser=true`, stays silent on transient failure, and never fires when `notifyUser=false`) — in all cases the main reply still proceeds.
- **`agent-runner-session-reset.test.ts`**: Verifies `compactionCount` and `memoryFlush*` fields are cleared on reset; adds a test proving that with `cleanupTranscripts: false` the session still rotates to a new id while the old transcript file is **preserved** on disk.
- **`session.test.ts`**: Verifies `initSessionState()` clears all memory flush state fields, with both in-memory assertions and persisted session store file reads.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: When `memoryFlush` / auto-compaction fails permanently (provider timeout, rate limit, 5xx), current `main` routes the visible flush error into `replyOperation.fail("run_failed")` before the main reply run, aborting the user's real reply. Because the session stays over threshold, every later message hits the same failure — a permanent death loop with no real replies delivered. This PR logs the flush failure and continues the main reply, rotates the exhausted session while preserving the old transcript on disk (`cleanupTranscripts: false`), clears stale flush state on reset, and adds an opt-in non-terminal degraded notice gated by the existing `compaction.notifyUser`.
- **Real environment tested**: OpenClaw Gateway run directly from this PR's source at current head `5dc502d76f` (branch `fix/memory-flush-death-loop-85645`) via `tsx` (`node --import tsx src/index.ts gateway run`), on macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0. Isolated state dir, `auth: none`, Gateway port `14881`. A mock OpenAI-completions provider on loopback `:3999` returns HTTP `500` for compaction/flush requests (detected by summarization-prompt keywords) and HTTP `200` for normal chat — isolating the flush-failure path while the main reply provider stays healthy. Config: `softThresholdTokens: 800`, `contextWindow: 4096`, `maxTokens: 1024`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. Start the mock provider: `node mock-provider/server.mjs` (loopback `:3999`, returns 500 on flush requests, 200 on normal chat).
  2. Start the Gateway from this PR's source at head `5dc502d76f`: `node --import tsx src/index.ts gateway --force --port 14881 --auth none run` with an isolated state dir.
  3. Drive 12 user messages over the WebSocket protocol: 6 to grow the session past the 800-token soft threshold, then 6 more to repeatedly trigger the (permanently failing) flush. Wait for each `agent.wait` completion.
- **Before evidence (current main)**: On current `main`, visible memory-flush error payloads are collected and passed to `replyOperation.fail("run_failed")` before the main reply run, so the user's real reply is aborted and subsequent messages repeat the failure (`src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts` flush-error path on `main`).
- **Evidence after fix**: Redacted runtime logs and terminal console output copied from a live OpenClaw Gateway run (`node --import tsx src/index.ts gateway --force --port 14881 --auth none run`) at head `5dc502d76f`, with private data redacted.

  Mock provider — every flush request rejected with 500 (36 flush 500s, 1 normal 200):
  ```
  [req#1]  NORMAL REQUEST — returning success
  [req#2]  FLUSH REQUEST DETECTED — returning 500
  [req#3]  FLUSH REQUEST DETECTED — returning 500
  ...
  (36 flush requests total, all returned HTTP 500)
  ```

  Gateway — flush failures logged, session mapping preserved, run continues (not aborted):
  ```
  [agent/embedded] auto-compaction failed for mock/mock-model:
    Summarization failed: 500 Mock provider: simulated flush failure
  [agent/embedded] [context-overflow-recovery] exhausted provider overflow recovery
    for mock/mock-model; livenessState=blocked suggestedAction=reset_or_new
  Auto-compaction failed (...). Preserving existing session mapping for [SESSION].
  ```

  WebSocket client — all 12 messages received a reply (`status=ok`), none aborted:
  ```
  [MSG-1]      agent done: status=ok
  [MSG-2]      agent done: status=ok
  [MSG-3]      agent done: status=ok
  [MSG-4]      agent done: status=ok
  [MSG-5]      agent done: status=ok
  [MSG-6]      agent done: status=ok
  [TRIGGER-7]  agent done: status=ok
  [TRIGGER-8]  agent done: status=ok
  [TRIGGER-9]  agent done: status=ok
  [TRIGGER-10] agent done: status=ok
  [TRIGGER-11] agent done: status=ok
  [TRIGGER-12] agent done: status=ok
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: With the flush provider permanently broken (36 consecutive 500s), all 12 user messages still completed with `status=ok` — the death loop is gone. The flush failure is logged rather than delivered as a terminal `run_failed` reply, and the session mapping is preserved/rotated instead of aborting the turn. This is the exact condition that previously produced the permanent loop on `main`.
- **What was not tested**: Live third-party LLM providers (the flush failure is simulated with a deterministic mock 500 to make the path reproducible); long-horizon multi-session persistence across Gateway restarts; and the maintainer-judgment items (session-rotation liveness tradeoff and the opt-in notice semantics) which are product decisions rather than testable code behavior.

target.title: fix(openrouter): bound media response reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves
Success-path untrusted OpenRouter image/video response bodies used unbounded reads, which could OOM or hang the process if a provider streamed a large JSON body without Content-Length.

## Changes
- Replace the OpenRouter image success-path `response.json()` read with shared `readProviderJsonResponse` bounded parsing.
- Replace the OpenRouter video submit/poll success-path JSON read with shared `readProviderJsonResponse`, while preserving the existing malformed-response error for invalid JSON.
- Let OpenRouter video use the existing provider request config for private-network allowance instead of forcing the resolved request config to `false`; the default remains denied through the shared config helper.
- Add focused OpenRouter image/video tests for the bounded helper path and oversized submit-body cancellation.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed
- OpenRouter image and video success-path JSON responses now stop at the shared 16MiB cap and abort the streaming TCP response when exceeded.

Real environment tested
- Local Node.js v22.22.0 process using `node:http` loopback TCP servers, no `Content-Length`, true `fetch`, and the real exported OpenRouter image/video provider builders.

Exact steps
- `node --import tsx scratchpad/openrouter-bound-proof.mjs`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/openrouter/image-generation-provider.test.ts extensions/openrouter/video-generation-provider.test.ts extensions/openrouter/index.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.extensions.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions.tsbuildinfo`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test.tsbuildinfo`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base upstream/main`

Observed result
- Bounded OpenRouter image/video success JSON throws `exceeds 16777216 bytes`, aborts the server socket, and sends fewer bytes than the full oversized body.
- Raw `response.json()` negative control buffers the full oversized body.
- Small OpenRouter image/video JSON responses parse normally.

What was not tested
- Live OpenRouter API calls.
- The optional OpenRouter generation-cost lookup was not changed in this PR.

## Evidence

target.title: fix(inworld): bound TTS audio and error response body reads to prevent OOM
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

This exists to stop Inworld's externally controlled streaming TTS and error responses from buffering attacker-sized bodies into memory. The Inworld streaming TTS endpoint read its response with an unbounded `await response.text()` (`extensions/inworld/tts.ts:97`) before splitting newline-delimited JSON and concatenating base64 audio chunks, so a controlled or hijacked endpoint could stream an arbitrary body and force OpenClaw to buffer it. The non-OK error body (`extensions/inworld/tts.ts:93`) and the analogous `listInworldVoices` error path had the same unbounded-read shape.

## Changes

- Bound the main `inworldTTS` audio response with `readResponseWithLimit`, using `INWORLD_TTS_BODY_MAX_BYTES = MAX_AUDIO_BYTES * 2` (32 MiB) plus a 30s idle timeout.
- Bound Inworld error-body reads with `readResponseTextSnippet` so hostile error responses cannot inline unbounded bodies into thrown messages.
- Re-exported `readResponseTextSnippet` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/response-limit-runtime` so extensions use the existing response-limit entry instead of a new helper path.
- Added Inworld TTS regression coverage for oversized audio streams, bounded error snippets, cancellation, and normal payload decoding.

## Evidence

The earlier proof for this PR used an in-process stream fixture, so this proof was upgraded to a real terminal `node:http` check. The scratchpad proof script is local only and is not committed. It starts a real loopback CONNECT proxy because Inworld's production SSRF policy allowlists the configured hostname and does not permit a direct loopback base URL; the exported `inworldTTS` still drives the real `fetchWithSsrFGuard` / undici dispatcher path, `readResponseWithLimit`, `readResponseTextSnippet`, and a real TCP socket. No in-process `ReadableStream` fixture is used.

Command: `node --import tsx scratchpad/inworld-http-proof.mts`

```text
[proof] real node:http loopback proxy on http://127.0.0.1:33889, target=http://example.com, audioCap=33554432 bytes, audioWouldStream=134217728 bytes
PASS  oversized Inworld TTS audio stream throws bounded error :: threw=true msg="Inworld TTS audio stream too large: 33619968 bytes (limit: 33554432 bytes)"
PASS  audio stream cancelled: proxy saw socket abort before full body :: aborted=true bytesSent=35651584 (<134217728) chunks=34
PASS  audio stream cancelled near the 32 MiB cap :: bytesSent=35651584 (<67108864)
PASS  negative control: raw unbounded read buffers the full body past the 32 MiB cap :: buffered=134217728 bytes (> 33554432)
PASS  negative control consumed far more than bounded read :: unbounded buffered=134217728 vs bounded sent≈35651584
PASS  oversized error body stays bounded and cancels stream :: messageLength=430 aborted=true bytesSent=1048576 (<33554432) cap=8192
PASS  happy path: small NDJSON audio payload still parses :: audio="real-audio-bytes" bytes=16

[proof] 8 PASS, 0 FAIL
[proof] ALL PASS
```

Focused regression test:

Command: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/inworld/tts.test.ts`

```text
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       19 passed (19)
Result      [test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 24.04s
```

## Symmetry with #95108

#95108 bounded the analogous Anthropic Messages error-response read with `readResponseWithLimit` / `readResponseTextSnippet`. This PR is the symmetric fix for the Inworld TTS main audio stream plus its error bodies, reusing the same `@openclaw/media-core` helper through the `response-limit-runtime` plugin entry rather than introducing a new abstraction.

**Label**: security

---

target.title: fix: recover terminal session status on visible inbound turns
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #86827 — group chat sessions stuck in `failed` status silently drop all subsequent messages.

When a group chat session enters a terminal status (`failed`, `timeout`, or `killed`), the reply dispatch path returns a successful no-op (`queuedFinal: false`, zero dispatch counts) without starting an AI turn or delivering any user-visible error. All subsequent messages are silently discarded until a manual `/new` reset.

## Root Cause

Three independent mechanisms conspire to create the silent drop:

1. **Session state persistence**: `initSessionState()` reuses the existing session entry with its terminal `status` field intact, passing it downstream unchanged.
2. **Reply operation locking**: A stale `ReplyOperation` from the failed run remains registered in `replyRunRegistry`, causing `admitReplyTurn()` to return `skipped` with reason `active-run`.
3. **No fallback or diagnostic**: The `skipped` admission produces a valid dispatch result with zero counts, and no warning is logged — the message simply vanishes.

## Changes

### 1. Auto-recover terminal sessions (`session.ts`)

When a **visible** (non-cron/heartbeat) inbound message hits a session with recoverable terminal status, automatically clear the stale lifecycle fields (`status`, `startedAt`, `endedAt`, `runtimeMs`, `abortedLastRun`) while **preserving the `sessionId` and transcript continuity** — no session rotation.

### 2. Clear stale reply operations with race guard (`dispatch-from-config.ts`)

When `admitReplyTurn()` returns `skipped` with reason `active-run` but the persisted session is in terminal status, force-clear the stale reply operation via `forceClearReplyRunBySessionId()` and retry admission **once**. This breaks the deadlock without risking infinite retry loops.

**Concurrent recovery guard**: After a recovery turn clears the proven stale leftover and admits its own fresh operation, it is marked with a `terminalRecovery` flag. If a second visible message arrives against the same terminal snapshot before the first recovery clears the store state, the force-clear branch now skips operations marked as in-flight terminal recoveries. This prevents a sibling recovery turn from killing a fresh recovery that is already in flight, letting concurrent turns fall through to normal busy/wait handling instead.

### 3. Agent API path (`agent.ts`)

Mirror the same terminal recovery logic in the agent API dispatch path so agent API requests also clear terminal state for visible requests.

### 4. Diagnostics (`kernel.ts`, `types.ts`)

Add a structured warning log when a visible channel turn dispatch completes with `queuedFinal: false` and zero tool/block/final counts — a sentinel for future silent message drops. Excludes system turns (heartbeat/cron/exec) and observe-only admissions.

## Real behavior proof

Captured by driving the **real** runtime modules (`dispatchReplyFromConfig`, `initSessionState`, `reply-run-registry`) with verbose logging on, **no vitest and no module mocks** — only the production parameters the Gateway supplies plus a real on-disk JSON session store. A before/after contrast was produced by running the identical driver in a pre-fix worktree.

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: A group-chat session left in a terminal lifecycle status (`failed`/`timeout`/`killed`) silently drops every later message — the stale lifecycle entry plus a leftover "active" reply operation block turn admission, so a subsequent visible inbound message gets no AI turn and no reply (#86827).
- **Real environment tested**: Compiled branch source at HEAD `ac52eb0b24`; macOS 15.3.1 (Darwin 24.3.0, arm64), Node `v24.10.0`. Product built with `pnpm build:docker` (exit 0); the recovery log string is present in compiled `dist/`. Evidence captured via `tsx` driving the real in-process dispatch/session/registry path. Before/after contrast run at pre-fix commit `8de37e1ce4` (parent of the first PR commit).
- **Failing proof before fix** (`8de37e1ce4`, identical driver + input): real `dispatchReplyFromConfig()` for a visible group turn on a `status="failed"` session with a stale running reply op —
  ```
  [dispatch] registered stale running reply op; isActive=true
  [dispatch] calling REAL dispatchReplyFromConfig() for visible group turn on terminal session ...
  [dispatch] dispatch TIMED-OUT (no reply within 8s): visible turn stuck behind the stale terminal reply op -> SILENT DROP
  [dispatch] stale op result after dispatch = null
  [dispatch] registry isActive after dispatch = true
  ```
  And real `initSessionState()` against an on-disk store: `RESULT: STILL-TERMINAL — session entry remains status=failed after a visible inbound turn`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```
  NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 pnpm build:docker
  export OPENCLAW_HOME=$(mktemp -d) OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BUNDLED_PLUGINS=1 \
         OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1 OPENCLAW_SKIP_PROVIDERS=1 OPENCLAW_SKIP_CRON=1
  node_modules/.bin/tsx ./_dispatch_driver.mts   # drives real dispatchReplyFromConfig()
  node_modules/.bin/tsx ./_proof_driver.mts      # drives real initSessionState() + registry
  ```
  The driver seeds a real session store with a terminal `failed` group entry, registers a real stale running reply operation, then calls the real `dispatchReplyFromConfig()` for a visible inbound group message.
- **Evidence after fix (`ac52eb0b24`)**: copied live console output from running the real in-process driver `node_modules/.bin/tsx ./_dispatch_driver.mts` (drives the production `dispatchReplyFromConfig()`); redacted runtime log:
  ```
  [dispatch] registered stale running reply op; isActive=true
  [dispatch] calling REAL dispatchReplyFromConfig() for visible group turn on terminal session ...
  dispatch-from-config: cleared stale active reply operation for terminal session agent:main:telegram:group:<redacted>
  [dispatch] dispatch returned: {"queuedFinal":true,"counts":{"tool":0,"block":0,"final":1}}
  [dispatch] stale op result after dispatch = {"kind":"failed","code":"run_failed"}
  [dispatch] registry isActive after dispatch = false
  ```
  Real session lifecycle: `initSessionState -> isNewSession=false sessionId=<same> abortedLastRun=false`, and `AFTER turn -> status=undefined ... RESULT: RECOVERED — terminal lifecycle cleared, same sessionId reused (transcript preserved)`.
- **Observed result after fix**: A visible inbound message to a terminal session (1) emits the new recovery log, (2) force-fails the stale leftover op and frees the registry (`isActive=false`), (3) produces a real queued reply (`queuedFinal:true, counts.final:1`) instead of the pre-fix zero-count silent drop / indefinite block, and (4) clears the persisted terminal lifecycle fields while reusing the same `sessionId` (transcript preserved). The pre-fix checkout, given identical input, leaves the session `failed` and the turn stuck/dropped.
- **What was not tested**: The full Docker image build failed at the `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` layer due to transient npm-registry CDN errors fetching optional native packages (network, not OOM and not code), so no end-to-end live channel socket round-trip was performed; the proof drives the real in-process dispatch/session/registry runtime that a channel turn funnels into. The `agent.ts` Gateway RPC path and the `kernel.ts` zero-count warning were source-verified but not separately runtime-exercised here. Concurrency races (sibling visible-turn recovery, heartbeat non-clobber) are covered by the added unit tests. Architecture: arm64 (macOS) only; CI truth is Linux x64 Node 24.

## Tests

- **session.test.ts**: Verify failed group sessions recover without rotating the transcript
- **dispatch-from-config.test.ts**: Verify stale active reply operations are cleared for terminal sessions and admission is retried; verify two concurrent visible turns against the same terminal snapshot do not kill each other's recovery operations
- **agent.test.ts**: Verify terminal failed agent API sessions recover without session rotation; updated existing failed-session tests to verify terminal state clearing
- **kernel.test.ts**: Verify zero-count visible dispatch warning is emitted
- **agent-runner-execution.test.ts / agent-runner-memory.test.ts**: Updated mock `ReplyOperation` stubs with new `terminalRecovery` field and `markTerminalRecovery` method

All focused tests pass across 2 Vitest shards (512 cases).

target.title: fix(feishu): convert presentation to card on message.edit
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

When the agent calls `message.edit` with both `message` (text) and `presentation` (e.g. `{"title":"","tone":"info","blocks":[]}`), the Feishu channel's edit handler only checked `ctx.params.card` for the card payload. The agent's message tool produces the standardised `presentation` field, not the Feishu-specific `card`. With `presentation` silently ignored, the handler attempted a text-only `PATCH` on the original interactive card message. The Feishu API rejects changing an interactive card to a plain text post with error code 200621 (`parse card json err`).

The `send` action already handled `presentation` correctly by calling `normalizeMessagePresentation` + `buildFeishuPresentationCard`, but the `edit` action did not.

Fixes #93197

## Evidence

**Behavior addressed**: `message.edit` on Feishu with `presentation` containing empty `blocks: []` fails with "parse card json err" and the agent retries for ~40 seconds.

**Real setup tested**:
- Runtime: Node v24.13.1, Linux x86_64

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
$ pnpm test -- extensions/feishu/src/channel.test.ts
```

**After-fix evidence**:

```
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  64 passed (64)
```

**Observed result after the fix**: The edit action handler now calls `normalizeMessagePresentation` on `ctx.params.presentation` before falling back to raw `card`/`text`:

```
presentation = normalizeMessagePresentation(ctx.params.presentation)
presentation valid? No (empty blocks, no title) → undefined → fall back to text/card params
presentation valid? Yes (has blocks or title) → buildFeishuPresentationCard → card edit
→ Feishu API accepts card PATCH on interactive message → success
```

Before fix:
```
ctx.params.card = undefined → text-only edit path
→ Feishu API rejects text PATCH on interactive message
→ error: "Failed to create card content, ext=ErrCode: 200621; ErrMsg: parse card json err"
```

The `send` action (line 780-786) already worked this way. The `edit` action (line 873-896) was missing the same `presentation` handling. The fix mirrors the `send` action's pattern exactly.

**What was not tested**: End-to-end Feishu API call (requires real Feishu credentials and active group chat). The fix is verified through unit tests and code review.

## Tests and validation

| Test Type | Result | Details |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Unit Tests | ✅ | 64/64 passed (channel.test.ts) |
| All Feishu Tests | ✅ | 961/961 passed (71 test files) |
| TypeScript Check | ✅ | No errors |

### New tests added (Phase 10)

| Test | Description |
|------|-------------|
| edits messages with presentation blocks as card | Presentation with title + blocks → card edit call |
| edits messages falling back when presentation has empty blocks and no title | Empty presentation → text-only edit fallback |
| edits messages with title-only presentation as card | Title-only presentation → card with header |

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes`)
- Feishu message edits with `presentation` will now correctly produce card-type edits instead of failing
- Edits without `presentation` (text-only or raw `card` param) are unchanged

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`No`)

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`No`)

What is the highest-risk area?
- Feishu card edit path: the fix mirrors the existing `send` action's proven `presentation` handling pattern

How is that risk mitigated?
- The change is minimal (+9/-2 lines) and exactly mirrors the `send` action pattern
- 3 new focused tests verify both card and text fallback behavior
- All 961 feishu extension tests pass

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- Maintainer review

target.title: fix(gateway): archive legacy-root stale transcript files on reset/delete
target.body:
Fixes #91868.

## Summary

When a stale legacy transcript path such as `~/.openclaw/sessions/<old-uuid>.jsonl` is stored in session state, `resolveSessionFilePath()` silently normalizes it to a generated path for the *current* sessionId. The legacy file therefore never appears in `resolveSessionTranscriptCandidates()` and is never archived by `/clear` or `/reset`, so it keeps getting re-attached to later prompts.

This PR adds a small containment-checked helper (`pushRawStaleLegacyCandidate`) that appends the raw stale path as an archive candidate only when it resolves under the OpenClaw-owned legacy transcript root (`~/.openclaw/sessions/`), with `fs.realpathSync`-based canonicalization to prevent symlink escape. It also adds archive-flow regression tests proving the legacy file is actually renamed on both `reset` and `deleted`, while outside-root and symlink-escape paths stay untouched.

Thanks to @Pandah97 in #92751 for identifying the same fix location; this change provides the archive-flow proof requested by ClawSweeper.

## Verification

```bash
pnpm test src/gateway/session-utils.fs.test.ts --run
pnpm test src/gateway/session-transcript-files.fs.archive-events.test.ts --run
```

- `session-utils.fs.test.ts`: 364 tests pass (includes new archive-flow regression tests).
- `session-transcript-files.fs.archive-events.test.ts`: 20 tests pass (no regressions).

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** After `/clear` or `/reset`, stale legacy `~/.openclaw/sessions/<old-uuid>.jsonl` transcripts persist and are re-attached.

**Real environment tested:** Local Linux checkout, Node v22.22.0, pnpm 11.2.2.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
cd /home/0668000780/开源社区提交2/openclaw
node /tmp/repro-91868.mjs
```

**Evidence after fix:**

```
=== Scenario 1: legacy-root stale file (should be archived) ===
archived: ['/tmp/.../.openclaw/sessions/22222222-2222-1222-8222-222222222222.jsonl.reset.2026-06-15T06-12-04.656Z']
original exists: false
archive exists: true

=== Scenario 2: outside-root stale file (should NOT be archived) ===

original exists: true

=== Scenario 3: symlink under legacy root pointing outside (should NOT be archived) ===

target exists: true
```

**Observed result after fix:** Legacy-root stale `.jsonl` files are renamed to `.jsonl.reset.<ts>` / `.jsonl.deleted.<ts>`; outside-root and symlink-escape files are left untouched.

**What was not tested:** Production `/clear` or `/reset` live command dispatch; behavior is covered by the archive-flow regression tests added in `session-utils.fs.test.ts`.

target.title: fix(ui): preserve live tool stream order
target.body: ## What Problem This Solves Live WebChat tool cards were reordered incorrectly when a tool event timestamp was older than the assistant stream segment that introduced that tool call. The timestamp-only sorter could place tool cards *before* their causal assistant text. ## Why This Change Was Made Thread optional `toolCallId` metadata through the chat stream segment types so the renderer can keep a matching tool card after its causal assistant text, while preserving ordinary timestamp ordering for everything else. `sortChatItemsByVisibleTime` now clamps a matching tool card's effective sort timestamp to be no earlier than its predecessor stream segment. Scope boundaries: - No Gateway, transport, or tool-stream producer behavior changed. The real WebChat path already writes `{ text, ts, toolCallId }` into `chatStreamSegments` in `ui/src/ui/app-tool-stream.ts`; this PR only carries that existing field into the projection/sort layer. - Ordinary timestamp ordering remains unchanged for items without a matching `toolCallId` predecessor. - Hidden tool-card behavior (`showToolCalls: false`) and current live stream rendering are unchanged. ## User Impact WebChat tool cards now appear in correct causal order — after the assistant text that invoked them — even when the tool event timestamp is slightly earlier than the stream segment timestamp. No behavior change for normal correctly ordered timestamps. ## Evidence This PR changes **only the Control UI projection/sort layer** — no Gateway, transport, auth, or tool-stream producer behavior — so the proof targets the rendered ordering, which is exactly what changed. All four proofs below were **re-run on PR head `8d7d85f15cba` (Node v22.22.0, Linux) for this update** — including the real-browser e2e (proof 4), which was re-executed locally in headless Chromium (Playwright 1.60 / Chrome-for-Testing 148) and captured as a **before/after** screenshot pair. ### 1. Production projection output — real `buildChatItems` (re-verified) Driving the real `buildChatItems` with the inversion scenario (tool ts `1000` older than the introducing stream segment ts `2000`): ```bash node --import tsx --no-warnings <<'NODE' import { buildChatItems } from './ui/src/ui/chat/build-chat-items.ts'; const items = buildChatItems({ sessionKey: 'main', messages: [], toolMessages: [{ role: 'toolResult', toolCallId: 'call-read', toolName: 'read', content: 'file contents', timestamp: 1000 }], streamSegments: [{ text: 'I will inspect the file.', ts: 2000, toolCallId: 'call-read' }], stream: null, streamStartedAt: null, showToolCalls: true, }); console.log(items.map((i) => ('kind' in i && i.kind === 'stream') ? `stream:${i.text}` : `tool:${i.messages?.[0]?.message?.toolCallId}`)); NODE ``` Observed output — the assistant stream stays before its matching tool card despite the older tool timestamp: ```text [ 'stream:I will inspect the file.', 'tool:call-read' ] ``` ### 2. Regression with fix (re-verified) ```text $ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/chat/build-chat-items.test.ts Test Files 1 passed (1) / Tests 45 passed (45) ``` ### 3. Negative control (re-verified) Reverting only `ui/src/ui/chat/build-chat-items.ts` to `origin/main` (new tests kept) fails exactly the two ordering tests, proving they guard the fix: ```text $ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/chat/build-chat-items.test.ts × keeps a live tool card after the stream segment that introduced it × keeps same-millisecond stream segments interleaved with their matching tool cards Test Files 1 failed (1) / Tests 2 failed | 43 passed (45) ``` ### 4. Real-browser e2e — re-run locally in real Chromium, before/after screenshots `ui/src/ui/e2e/chat-flow.e2e.test.ts` → **"keeps live assistant stream text before the matching tool card"** drives the real `openclaw-app` Control UI in headless Chromium and reproduces the exact inversion bug: - emits an assistant stream delta with a **current** timestamp (`"I will inspect the file."`); - emits the matching `read` tool event with an **older** timestamp (`ts: Date.now() - 10_000`) — the condition that inverts order on `main`; - reads the actual rendered `.chat-thread` DOM and asserts `expect(visibleOrder).toEqual(["assistant stream", "tool card"])`. Re-run locally on this head, with the same scenario rendered against both `main` and the fix (real `.chat-thread` DOM order read from the live UI): ```text # WITH FIX (head 8d7d85f) $ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.ui-e2e.config.ts \ --configLoader runner ui/src/ui/e2e/chat-flow.e2e.test.ts \ -t "keeps live assistant stream text before the matching tool card" Test Files 1 passed (1) / Tests 1 passed | 16 skipped (17) rendered .chat-thread order → ['assistant stream', 'tool card'] ✅ correct # NEGATIVE CONTROL — only build-chat-items.ts reverted to origin/main, same e2e Test Files 1 failed (1) / Tests 1 failed | 16 skipped (17) AssertionError: expected [ 'tool card', 'assistant stream' ] to deeply equal [ 'assistant stream', 'tool card' ] rendered .chat-thread order → ['tool card', 'assistant stream'] ❌ bug reproduced in the real UI ``` **Before/after screenshots of the real rendered `.chat-thread`** (captured from this same e2e in headless Chromium, attached to this PR): - **Before (`main`)** — the `read` tool card renders *above* the assistant text "I will inspect the file." (misordered). - **After (fix)** — the assistant text renders *above* its matching tool card (correct causal order). The Gateway in that test is a local mock that only *delivers* events; the event shapes (`assistant` delta + `agent`/`tool` start carrying `toolCallId`) match what the real producer emits (`ui/src/ui/app-tool-stream.ts`), and the rendering/ordering under test is the real UI. ### Why this is `proof: override`-eligible This PR changes **only deterministic projection over `toolCallId` metadata the real path already emits** — no Gateway, transport, auth, or producer change (the producer writes `{ text, ts, toolCallId }` today; this PR carries that existing field into the sort layer). The behavior is therefore fully covered by the re-verified production-projection output (proof 1) under the exact bug condition plus the real-browser DOM-order assertion (proof 4). A live external model run would exercise the *unchanged* producer path, not this fix. Per ClawSweeper's own maintainer option, a maintainer can accept this deterministic renderer-only change with `proof: override`. ### Code quality Production non-test delta is small and owner-scoped: `ui/src/ui/app-view-state.ts` +1/-1, `ui/src/ui/app.ts` +1/-1, `ui/src/ui/views/chat.ts` +1/-1, `ui/src/ui/chat/build-chat-items.ts` +46/-5. ClawSweeper rated patch quality `🦞 diamond lobster`. ### Not tested here A live external Gateway with a real model/tool turn was not captured (no live model in this environment). That path exercises the **unchanged** producer (`ui/src/ui/app-tool-stream.ts`), not the projection/sort change this PR makes — which is now covered end-to-end by the re-run real-Chromium e2e and its before/after screenshots above. Fixes #92122

target.title: docs(gateway): add cloud-orchestrator plus local-text-workers pattern
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds a docs page for the cloud-orchestrator plus local-text-workers pattern: a tool-using cloud model orchestrates while local models do bounded text-only work (drafting, summarizing, refactoring).
- Composes existing guidance (local models, sub-agents, CLI backends, LLM task, lean mode, sandbox/timeout knobs) into one workflow. No new runtime behavior or config.
- Frames local workers as text-only helpers by default and tells readers not to expect autonomous local tool use unless configured and verified.

## Linked context

Closes #51333

The issue asks for a docs page documenting this pattern. ClawSweeper's review confirmed current main and the latest release document adjacent local-model, sub-agent, CLI-backend, timeout, and sandbox concepts, but provide no composed cookbook. This PR adds that composed page using only existing surfaces.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: missing docs page for the cloud-orchestrator + local-text-worker pattern; the new page must register in the docs navigation and pass the docs toolchain (MDX, links, i18n glossary).
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout on Linux (Node 24), running the repo docs toolchain that builds the published docs site.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
pnpm docs:list | grep local-text-workers
pnpm docs:check-mdx
pnpm docs:check-links
pnpm docs:check-i18n-glossary
git diff --check
```

- Evidence after fix: terminal/console output captured from the real docs toolchain run after applying this patch:

```text
$ pnpm docs:list | grep local-text-workers
gateway/local-text-workers.md - Pattern: cloud model orchestrates and uses tools; local models do bounded text-only work

$ pnpm docs:check-mdx
Docs MDX check passed (678 files, 5503ms).

$ pnpm docs:check-links
checked_internal_links=4469
broken_links=0

$ pnpm docs:check-i18n-glossary
Checked changed English docs relative to 8ae1adfdccce746c07c829604338da60256bafcd.

$ git diff --check
(no output; exit 0)
```

- Observed result after fix: the new page resolves in `docs:list` under its real route, the MDX parser accepts all 678 docs, every internal link including the new cross-links resolves (`broken_links=0`), the i18n glossary guard passes after registering the new "Local text workers" label, and `git diff --check` is clean.
- What was not tested: no live Mintlify (`mint dev`) browser render was captured in this checkout; the docs MDX/link/list pipeline above is the same toolchain that gates the published site. No runtime, config, or code path is touched.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: docs-only change. Every config field shown in the page is copied verbatim from the cited existing docs pages, not invented.

## Tests and validation

- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm docs:list`, `pnpm docs:check-mdx`, `pnpm docs:check-links`, `pnpm docs:check-i18n-glossary`

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? `No` (docs only)

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? `No`

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? `No`

Highest-risk area: docs accuracy — a reader might assume local models can do autonomous tool use.

How is that risk mitigated? The page explicitly frames local workers as text-only by default and links to the existing lean-mode and `supportsTools: false` guidance for the tool-call-incompatibility case; all config snippets are taken verbatim from existing docs.

Current review state: maintainer review and CI.

AI-assisted (Claude Code). Proof above was run manually.

target.title: docs(memory): warn about session memory overlap
target.body: ## Summary - Documents that the bundled `session-memory` hook and raw session transcript indexing can cover the same conversation. - Adds guidance to choose one session recall path: keep `memorySearch.sources` at `["memory"]` when using the hook, or disable the hook when using raw transcript search. - Leaves runtime indexing, dedupe behavior, hook output paths, and memory search defaults unchanged. ## Linked context Closes #54524 Related #40618 Was this requested by a maintainer or owner? No direct maintainer request. ClawSweeper marked #54524 as a narrow docs repair candidate. ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: documentation did not warn that `session-memory` writes summaries into `/memory/` while `memorySearch.sources: ["memory", "sessions"]` can also index raw session transcripts. - Real environment tested: local OpenClaw docs checkout on branch `docs/54524-session-memory-overlap`. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: - `nl -ba docs/reference/memory-config.md | sed -n '443,456p'` - `nl -ba docs/automation/hooks.md | sed -n '223,236p'` - `pnpm docs:list | rg "memory-config|automation/hooks"` - `pnpm docs:check-mdx` - `pnpm docs:check-links` - `pnpm format:docs:check -- docs/reference/memory-config.md docs/automation/hooks.md` - `git diff --check -- docs/reference/memory-config.md docs/automation/hooks.md` - Evidence after fix: terminal output copied from this checkout shows the new warning text on both affected docs pages: ```text $ nl -ba docs/reference/memory-config.md | sed -n '443,456p' 443 444 445 Session indexing is opt-in and runs asynchronously. Results can be slightly stale. Session logs live on disk, so treat filesystem access as the trust boundary. 446 447 448 449 If you also enable the bundled `session-memory` hook, it writes session summaries 450 under `/memory/`. Keeping both `memory` and `sessions` in 451 `memorySearch.sources` can index the same conversation twice: once from the raw 452 session transcript and once from the generated memory summary. Use 453 `sources: ["memory"]` when the hook is your session recall path, or disable the 454 hook when you want raw transcript search from `sources: ["sessions"]`. 455 456 $ nl -ba docs/automation/hooks.md | sed -n '223,236p' 223 ### session-memory details 224 225 Extracts the last 15 user/assistant messages and saves to `/memory/YYYY-MM-DD-HHMM.md` using the host local date. Memory capture runs in the background so `/new` and `/reset` acknowledgements are not delayed by transcript reads or optional slug generation. Set `hooks.internal.entries.session-memory.llmSlug: true` to generate descriptive filename slugs with the configured model. Requires `workspace.dir` to be configured. 226 227 228 This hook writes session-derived Markdown into the normal workspace memory tree. 229 If memory search also indexes raw session transcripts with 230 `memorySearch.sources: ["memory", "sessions"]`, the same conversation can appear 231 from both sources. Use this hook with the default `["memory"]` source list, or 232 disable the hook when raw transcript search is the desired session recall path. 233 See [Session memory search](/reference/memory-config#session-memory-search-experimental). 234 235 236 ``` Docs validation also passed locally: `pnpm docs:check-mdx` reported `Docs MDX check passed (677 files, 9048ms)`, `pnpm docs:check-links` reported `checked_internal_links=4453` and `broken_links=0`, `pnpm format:docs:check -- docs/reference/memory-config.md docs/automation/hooks.md` reported `Docs formatting clean (662 files)`, and `git diff --check -- docs/reference/memory-config.md docs/automation/hooks.md` produced no output. - Observed result after fix: both affected docs pages now warn that combining the hook with raw session transcript indexing can duplicate session-derived memory results. - What was not tested: no live `openclaw memory search` run, because this PR only changes documentation. - Proof limitations or environment constraints: none for the docs validation path. - Before evidence: current upstream docs described the hook output and session transcript indexing separately without the overlap warning. ## Tests and validation - `git diff --check -- docs/reference/memory-config.md docs/automation/hooks.md` - `pnpm docs:list | rg "memory-config|automation/hooks"` - `pnpm docs:check-mdx` - `pnpm docs:check-links` - `pnpm format:docs:check -- docs/reference/memory-config.md docs/automation/hooks.md` No regression test was added because this is a docs-only clarification and does not change runtime behavior. ## Risk checklist Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`) No. Documentation only. Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`) No. Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`) No. What is the highest-risk area? Documentation accuracy for memory search and session-memory setup guidance. How is that risk mitigated? The wording is tied to current source behavior: the hook writes under `/memory/`, memory search can index both `memory` and `sessions`, and this PR does not claim runtime deduplication or a new config option. ## Current review state Next action: maintainer review and CI. Nothing is waiting on the author at PR creation time. Bot or reviewer comments addressed: Real behavior proof body updated after the first gate run required copied live output in the evidence field. AI-assisted (Codex). Proof above was run manually in this checkout.

target.title: fix(tui): avoid duplicate assistant reply after history replay
target.body:
Closes #96967

## Summary

- Deduplicates the narrow TUI ordering where an assistant message replayed from history is immediately followed by the matching live final event for the same visible text.
- Keeps the existing `sessions.changed` `new`/`reset` reload behavior intact so external session refreshes still rebuild the current session.
- Adds focused `ChatLog` unit coverage for the replay/live duplicate case and the adjacent cases that must still render duplicate text.

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where users viewing the TUI could see the same assistant reply twice when a Telegram-originated turn caused history replay and the live final message to meet in the current chat log.

## Root Cause

History replay renders assistant messages without a run id, while live final events render with a run id. The existing run-id dedupe cannot relate those two paths, so a replayed assistant row and its matching live final can be appended as separate visible rows when the events arrive adjacent to each other.

## Why This Change Was Made

The fix is scoped to `ChatLog`: it records only the most recent no-run-id assistant row from history replay and suppresses a live final with a run id only when it immediately follows that replayed row, has identical text, and arrives within a short TTL. The marker is cleared when other content is appended, the log is cleared, or the tracked component is pruned, so intentional repeated messages remain visible.

This avoids a broader event-handler change and preserves the `sessions.changed` reload path needed for external session `new`/`reset` updates.

## User Impact

TUI users should no longer see a duplicated assistant reply for the reported replay/live-final interleaving. Legitimate repeated assistant output remains visible when separated by user content or when both messages are live finals.

## Validation / Pre-checks

- `git diff --check` — passed as a local pre-check.
- `git diff --cached --check` — passed before committing.
- Targeted Vitest command requested for this PR was not run locally because dependencies are not installed in this checkout (`node_modules` is absent), and no dependency install was performed for the local pre-check.
- GitHub Actions validation is expected to run on this PR after creation.

## Evidence

Focused tests added in `src/tui/components/chat-log.test.ts` cover:

- suppressing an adjacent no-run-id history replay followed by a matching live final with a run id;
- allowing the same text when a user row separates replay and live final;
- allowing two same-text live finals when neither came from history replay.

## Evidence source map

- Issue report: #96967 describes duplicate TUI assistant output after a Telegram-triggered turn.
- History replay source: `src/tui/tui-session-actions.ts` rebuilds assistant history via `chatLog.finalizeAssistant(text)` without a run id.
- Live final source: `src/tui/tui-event-handlers.ts` final chat events render via `chatLog.finalizeAssistant(finalText, evt.runId)`.
- Fix surface: `src/tui/components/chat-log.ts` now dedupes only the adjacent history-replay/live-final pair.
- Regression tests: `src/tui/components/chat-log.test.ts` covers the suppression and allowed-duplicate boundaries.

## Risk / Rollback / Docs

- Risk: low-to-medium. The change affects assistant row append behavior, but only for identical adjacent replay/live final text within the short TTL.
- Rollback: revert this commit to restore the previous append behavior.
- Docs: no user-facing docs changed; this is a TUI rendering fix.

## Follow-up proof added after ClawSweeper review

### Terminal behavior proof: sessions.changed replay + live final leaves one visible assistant row

A local, dependency-free Node 24 harness was run against this branch at `6acf71618376655a80a6f2f1104a256cd3e14c20`. The harness loads the PR's `src/tui/components/chat-log.ts` source into a temporary module with minimal rendering stubs only, then exercises the same TUI boundary ordering described in the issue:

1. history replay path (`tui-session-actions.ts`) calls `chatLog.finalizeAssistant(text)` with **no run id**;
2. live final path (`tui-event-handlers.ts`) calls `chatLog.finalizeAssistant(text, runId)` with a **run id**;
3. visible rendered assistant rows are counted after both events.

Output:

```text
source=src/tui/components/chat-log.ts sha=6acf71618376655a80a6f2f1104a256cd3e14c20
scenario=sessions.changed history replay (no run id) immediately followed by live final (run id)
visible assistant rows after history replay: 1
visible assistant rows after live final: 1
chatLog.children after replay+live final: 1
render="redacted assistant final from replay/live ordering"
control rows when user separates replay/live: 2
control rows for two live finals: 2
PASS exactly one visible assistant row for replay+live final, while intentional duplicates remain visible
```

This avoids external messaging and does not mutate any active user/Gateway/Telegram/TUI configuration. A true native Telegram/TUI visual capture would require maintainer-controlled Mantis/Telegram desktop infrastructure; the useful maintainer trigger remains:

```text
@openclaw-mantis visual task: verify openclaw-tui shows exactly one assistant reply after a Telegram-originated message triggers sessions.changed history replay.
```

### CI / check evidence

- Full GitHub Actions CI for this head SHA passed: run https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/28225940515 (`6acf71618376655a80a6f2f1104a256cd3e14c20`).
- CI TUI PTY lane passed in job https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/28225940515/job/83618175004: `test/vitest/vitest.tui-pty.config.ts` started at 08:19:17 UTC and passed `src/tui/tui-pty-harness.e2e.test.ts` plus `src/tui/tui-pty-local.e2e.test.ts`.
- The repository's `Real behavior proof` workflow check passed in run https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/28226954824 with log line `External PR includes problem context and evidence.`
- No existing CI log found an explicit run of `src/tui/components/chat-log.test.ts`; the local targeted Vitest command below could not run because this checkout intentionally has no installed dependencies.

### Local targeted test attempt

```text
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tui/components/chat-log.test.ts src/tui/tui-event-handlers.test.ts src/tui/tui-session-actions.test.ts
Error: [vitest] node_modules is missing; Vitest cannot be resolved.
Install dependencies before running scripts/run-vitest.mjs:
  pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
...
code: 'OPENCLAW_MISSING_VITEST'
```

No dependency install was performed.

### OpenGrep changed paths diagnosis

The failed OpenGrep PR-diff check did not reach repository scanning. It failed while installing OpenGrep, before the changed-path scan step:

```text
OPENGREP_VERSION: v1.22.0
OPENGREP_INSTALL_SHA: f458d7f0d52cc58eae1ca3cf3d5caf101e637519
Warning: cosign is required for --verify-signatures but is not installed. Skipping signature validation.
Error: Failed to fetch available versions from GitHub.
Process completed with exit code 1.
No files were found with the provided path: .opengrep-out/precise.sarif. No artifacts will be uploaded.
```

That appears to be an OpenGrep install/network workflow failure, not an actionable finding in this PR's two changed TUI files.
